sm120_feasibility.evidence: FlashInfer issue #2555: XQA decode (MHA) WORKS on sm120 (xqa.py checks major [9,10,12]); SM120 CUTLASS FP8/FP4 GEMM works; FMHA_V2 sm120 prefill kernels exist but are unwired behind ENABLE_SM120; backend selector falls through to generic FA2; mla_sm120.cu compiled for major 12. TensorRT-LLM issue #11799: no SM120/121 trtllm-gen FMHA cubins exist; 'SM120 does not have tcgen05 or TMEM — uses mma.sync.aligned.block_scale instead of tcgen05.mma'; flashinfer fmhaRunner.cuh:30 hard-checks kSM_100||kSM_103. vLLM issue #36865 (RTX 5090, flashinfer 0.6.6+sm120): CUTLASS SM120 / NVFP4 SM120 / MLA SM120 / compute_120f all OK; FA2/FA3/FA4 and FlashMLA NOT sm120. HF nvidia/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-NVFP4 discussion #9: 120a -> cudaErrorIllegalInstruction, fix = FLASHINFER_CUDA_ARCH_LIST=12.0f; CUDA-graph replay crashes/hangs on sm120 under load; fp8 KV cache (--kv-cache-dtype fp8) works. florianmattana.com fp4-fused-attention-kernel-sm120 + CUTLASS issue #3044: sm120 = Ampere-style mma.sync, no TMEM/tcgen05; SageAttention3 supports sm120 (>1000 TOPS on 5090) via CUTLASS. FlashInfer README: FLASHINFER_CUDA_ARCH_LIST includes 12.0f (sm120 build supported). llama.cpp docs/build.md: GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS enables all KV-quant FA combos — these CUDA FA kernels are mma/WMMA-based and run on sm120 today. Verified locally: nvidia-smi reports compute_cap 12.0, 24463 MiB; nvcc 13.1 present.

components.beats_competitors_how: "vLLM/SGLang/TRT-LLM are tuned for datacenter Blackwell (sm_100/sm_90a, tcgen05/TMEM, big HBM, RDMA tiering) and on a 24GB sm_120 laptop they either (a) fall back to slow/unstable paths (trtllm-gen blocked, FA3 unavailable, CUDA-graph hangs) or (b) leave the KV budget under-squeezed (block-granular sharing or no KV quant by default). llama.cpp runs on sm120 but uses a non-paged contiguous cache that fragments and has no token-level radix sharing or proper tiered spill. We beat all four on THIS box by combining the best lever from each AND tuning the kernel to sm120 reality: (1) paged + radix HYBRID -> higher prefix-cache hit rate than vLLM block-hash and less fragmentation than llama.cpp; (2) asymmetric per-K/per-V quant (q8_0-K/q4_0-V or fp8) ON BY DEFAULT -> 2-4x more context in 24GB than vLLM/SGLang defaults; (3) sm120-native mma.sync FA2 + XQA decode kernel built with 12.0f -> avoids the illegal-instruction/graph-hang failure modes that make the others unstable on consumer Blackwell; (4) write-back/selective page spill sized to ~12-16GB free host RAM + disk mmap, with layer-wise prefetch overlap -> usable long context where vLLM/SGLang assume abundant host RAM/RDMA; (5) plan()/run() with bucketed batch sizes -> CUDA-graph throughput WITHOUT the sm120 replay crashes that force competitors into slow --enforce-eager. Net: more context length, higher cache hit rate, and graph-stable decode on the exact hardware where the incumbents are either slow or broken."
open_questions: Does FlashInfer's XQA sm120 decode (mla_sm120.cu) actually pass correctness + run stably under CUDA graphs on THIS driver (595 / CUDA 13.2 runtime), given issue #2166 reports MLA-FP8 failures? Must empirically test on the box.

What is the real measured PCIe spill bandwidth (GPU<->host page-first transfer with sgl-kernel transfer.cu) on this laptop, and does layer-wise prefetch actually hide it at ~150W, or does power-capping throttle the copy engines? Need a microbenchmark.

Does fp8 KV cache (e4m3) on sm120 give better accuracy/speed than llama.cpp-style q8_0/q4_0 integer KV quant, and does the sm120 FA2/XQA kernel even accept fp8 KV or must we dequant on load? Resolve before committing to a KV dtype.

Optimal page_size for sm120 mma.sync FA2 (16 vs 32) given m16n8k tile shapes and the 24GB fragmentation tradeoff — needs a sweep; 16 is the safe default but 32 may align better with the MMA k-dim and reduce indptr overhead.

Can the FMHA_V2 sm120 prefill kernels be un-gated (remove ENABLE_SM120 gate, wire determine_attention_backend) to get a faster prefill than generic FA2, or is building our own mma.sync prefill kernel (SageAttention3-style) the better copy target?

For MoE models with expert spilling, how do KV pages and expert weights contend for the same ~24GB + ~12-16GB host budget, and should the spill controller co-schedule them (shared eviction policy) rather than manage KV pages in isolation?
Is the chained block-hash (vLLM) compatible with a radix index without double-bookkeeping, or should the radix node key BE the chained hash to unify dedupe + tree walk?

_verdict.component: KV cache management + paging (block layout, prefix sharing, eviction, KV quant, spill) — sm_120 attention-kernel feasibility claim
_verdict.riskiest_claim: That the design can rely on an existing, copy-ready sm_120-native attention path — FlashInfer fa2 backend + XQA decode built with 12.0f, plus plan()/run() with bucketed batch sizes — as "the only graph-stable, sm120-native route," with page_size=16, to beat vLLM/SGLang/llama.cpp on a 24GB RTX 5090 Laptop. The whole KV-manager design feeds this kernel, so if the kernel path is not actually copy-ready and graph-stable, the "beats competitors" conclusion collapses.
_verdict.evidence: The CORE feasibility holds and is well-corroborated by primary sources: (1) FlashInfer issue #2555 (Feb 2026) states verbatim "XQA decode (MHA): Works (xqa.py checks [9,10,12])", "SM120 GEMM (FP8,FP4): Works", and confirms FMHA_V2 SM120 prefill kernels "exist but never generated or routed to" (gated by ENABLE_SM120) while determine_attention_backend() routes SM120 to generic FA2 — exactly matching the recommendation. (2) SGLang main-branch attention_backend.md (May 2026) ships "TRTLLM MHA (XQA backend) (Optimized for SM90 and SM120, e.g. H20, H200, 5090)"; issue #19637 confirms "Currently, only xqa MHA can support this for SM120." (3) vLLM #37242 — real RTX 5090 sm_120, --attention-backend flashinfer works, CUDA graphs work, FlashInfer +8%; Reddit benchmark cites Qwen3.6-27B at 85-100 t/s on a 24GB RTX 5090 Laptop. (4) 12.0f vs 12.0a illegal-instruction confirmed by vLLM #38718 and HF nvidia/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-NVFP4 discussion #9. BUT three corrections refute the "copy-ready + graph-stable + page_size=16" framing: (a) the "works" evidence is DECODE only — SM120 prefill currently falls to generic Ampere FA2; getting a tuned SM120 prefill requires un-gating FMHA_V2 or writing a new mma.sync kernel (real engineering, NOT copy-paste, per #2555). (b) HF discussion #9 documents EngineDeadError at cudaGraphLaunch and silent 100%-util/120W spin-hangs on SM120 EVEN WITH 12.0f; fixes are empirical (--max-cudagraph-capture-size 64, PIECEWISE), so plan()/run() is a plausible-but-unproven mitigation, not a guarantee. (c) SGLang doc: "TRTLLM XQA backend only works well for pagesize 64" — directly contradicts the recommendation's page_size=16 choice for the very XQA decode kernel it plans to copy. FlashInfer #2166 also confirms FLASHINFER_MLA is unsupported on SM120, but that only affects DeepSeek-MLA models, which the recommendation correctly scopes out.
_verdict.correction: Keep the overall hybrid design (paged physical KV + radix index + per-K/per-V KV quant + write-back spill) — it is sound and sm_120-feasible. But correct three framing errors before committing: (1) Treat the attention DECODE path (FlashInfer XQA / SGLang trtllm_mha XQA, built 12.0f) as the known-good, copy-ready sm_120 kernel — it is. Treat PREFILL as net-new work: budget for either (a) un-gating FMHA_V2 SM120 (remove ENABLE_SM120 gate + wire determine_attention_backend), or (b) a custom mma.sync FA2 prefill, or (c) simply reuse llama.cpp ggml-cuda/fattn-*.cu (mma/WMMA, known-good on sm120 today) as the prefill+decode fallback — do NOT assume a tuned sm120 prefill exists to copy. (2) Set page_size=64, not 16, for the decode path: the only shipping sm120 XQA kernel is explicitly tuned for pagesize 64 ("only works well for pagesize 64"); revisit 16 only if you write your own kernel. Decouple the manager's logical block granularity (can stay fine for fragmentation/radix matching) from the kernel's physical page size (64). (3) Do not claim plan()/run()+bucketing is sufficient for graph stability — validate empirically on driver 595/CUDA 13.2, and bake in the documented mitigations as first-class config: cap captured graph sizes, support a PIECEWISE/partial-graph mode, and keep an enforce-eager fallback. Lowest-risk fallback if sm120 kernel work stalls: ship on llama.cpp's CUDA FA (GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS) as the baseline attention+KV-quant engine — it runs on sm120 today — and layer the paged+radix manager + spill on top, treating FlashInfer XQA decode as the throughput upgrade once correctness/graph-stability are verified on the box.

components.component: KV-cache quantization (FP8 default + aggressive low-bit option), and the attention-kernel integration + asymmetric-K/V trap on consumer Blackwell sm_120 (RTX 5090 laptop, 24GB, 150W).

leading_impls.approach: --kv-cache-dtype fp8 stores K and V as FP8 e4m3 AND runs QK^T and ScoreV matmuls in FP8 (queries also quantized to FP8). Per-tensor uncalibrated scale=1.0 default; optional per-head static scales and dataset calibration via llm-compressor. reshape_and_cache_flash kernel writes FP8 KV into paged blocks with a scale array; query-quant moved out to torch.compile-fusable op. On Hopper uses the vLLM flash-attention fork (FA3) which needed a two-level FP32 accumulation fix (SageAttention2-style) to survive long contexts; on Blackwell B200 uses FlashInfer (no two-level accum needed). Per-head scales + skip-layers (sliding-window kept BF16).
leading_impls.refs: vllm-project.github.io/2026/04/22/fp8-kvcache.html; vllm/flash-attention PR#104 (two-level accum), PR#125 (tile sizes), PR#96/#91 (decode tiling); vllm PR#33695 (--kv-cache-dtype-skip-layers), PR#30833 (per-group static quant), PR#30141 (reshape_and_cache_flash scale array + per-head), PR#24914 (query-quant fusion); docs/features/quantization/quantized_kvcache.md; llm-compressor for calibration
leading_impls.strengths: Production-validated accuracy: 1M-token MRCR AUC recovery 94-98%, reasoning within 0.7-2 pts of BF16. Decode ITL slope drops to ~54% of BF16 (Llama-3.1-8B), break-even ~4-7k tokens. e4m3 is well-understood, simplest scheme, halves KV memory and KV memory traffic (the real decode bottleneck on a 1.8TB/s laptop part). Per-head scales + layer-skip give a clean accuracy escape hatch.
leading_impls.weaknesses: head_dim=256 prefill regresses (two-level accum register pressure). Sliding-window layers gain nothing from FP8 (skip them). Uncalibrated can drift on some models (Kimi-K2.5/FlashMLA). vLLM's whole-attention-in-FP8 is heavier than just FP8 storage. Hopper-specific FA3 fixes don't transfer to sm_120 (different kernel).

leading_impls.approach: Kernel library/JIT generator with FA2/cuDNN/CUTLASS/TRT-LLM backends. Paged + ragged KV, FP8 (e4m3/e5m2) and FP4 (nvfp4/mxfp4) attention, MLA, POD/cascade. Accepts q_data_type/kv_data_type (fp8) on plan(); q_scale/k_scale/v_scale on run() (decode: scalar float; prefill: float OR torch.Tensor). Paged layout [max_pages,2,page_size,num_kv_heads,head_dim]. Explicitly lists SM 12.0/12.1 = RTX 50 series in its arch table; Blackwell support shipped v0.4.0 (2025-10-08); build arch list includes 12.0f. Reaches sm_120 via mma.sync-based kernels (not tcgen05).
leading_impls.refs: github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer README arch table (SM12.0/12.1 RTX 50); v0.4.0 release notes; docs.flashinfer.ai/api/attention.html (BatchDecode/BatchPrefillWithPagedKVCacheWrapper, q_scale/k_scale/v_scale, kv_data_type fp8); FLASHINFER_CUDA_ARCH_LIST="...12.0f"; arxiv 2501.01005
leading_impls.strengths: ONLY mainstream attention lib that explicitly claims and ships consumer sm_120 (12.0f) support with FP8 KV. Paged KV native (fits our spilling/GGUF block model). torch-free C++/CUDA kernels are liftable. cuDNN/TRT-LLM backends auto-selected. This is what vLLM uses for Blackwell FP8 and SGLang wraps for sm_120.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Prefill k/v_scale as tensor lacks documented per-token/per-head semantics (verify in source). FP8 paged-wrapper sub-format (e4m3 vs e5m2) not documented - test empirically. Heavy dependency if lifted whole; we want specific kernels. Real-world sm_120 dequant-scale bug existed (SGLang PR#18904 passing KV scales to paged attn).

leading_impls.approach: XQA decode kernel for MHA/GQA with FP8 KV cache, exposed in SGLang as --decode-attention-backend trtllm_mha. Native page sizes 16/32/64 (best at 64). Uses mma.sync FP8 path that runs on consumer Blackwell.
leading_impls.refs: github.com/sgl-project/sglang/blob/main/docs/advanced_features/attention_backend.md ('TRTLLM MHA (XQA) Optimized for SM90 and SM120, e.g., H20, H200, 5090'); NVIDIA/TensorRT-LLM XQA kernels
leading_impls.strengths: Explicit, vendor-built sm_120 + FP8-KV decode kernel - strongest evidence that FP8 KV attention is fast on 5090. Page-size 64 matches a sane block design.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Page size constrained (64). TRT-LLM is a heavy, less-liftable codebase; the XQA kernel is the only part worth studying. Documented as decode-optimized (pair with separate prefill kernel).

leading_impls.engine: llama.cpp (GGUF-native KV quant) + the asymmetric-K/V trap
leading_impls.approach: KV quantized to GGML block types (q8_0, q4_0, q4_1, q5_0, q5_1, also f16/bf16) via -ctk/-ctv. Quant KV is dequantized on-the-fly ONLY in the FlashAttention VEC kernel (fattn-vec); the tensor-core MMA/WMMA/tile paths force f16 K/V. CRITICAL TRAP: default build compiles only 4 SYMMETRIC combos (F16/F16, Q4_0/Q4_0, Q8_0/Q8_0, BF16/BF16). Code path: in ggml_cuda_get_best_fattn_kernel, '#ifndef GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS: if (K->type != V->type) return BEST_FATTN_KERNEL_NONE;' - so asymmetric K/V (e.g. K=q4_0 small, V=q8_0) without the build flag falls back to the no-FA path = slow. GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS expands FATTN_VEC_CASES to the full 7x7 K/V matrix. Blackwell is now a first-class cc tier (GGML_CUDA_CC_BLACKWELL / GGML_CUDA_CC_DGX_SPARK) in kernel selection.
leading_impls.refs: ggml/src/ggml-cuda/fattn.cu (ggml_cuda_flash_attn_ext_vec FATTN_VEC_CASES_ALL_D table; ggml_cuda_get_best_fattn_kernel K->type!=V->type guard; GGML_CUDA_CC_BLACKWELL/DGX_SPARK switches for head_dim 576 GLM/Deepseek); fattn-vec.cuh; build flag GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS
leading_impls.strengths: GGUF-native (our required format). q8_0 KV is near-lossless and simple; per-block (32-elem) symmetric int scaling. VEC kernel is small and very liftable. Proven across many models on CPU+GPU. Asymmetric K(q4)/V(q8) is the natural 'aggressive but safe' point.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Quant KV NEVER uses tensor cores (VEC kernel dequants to f16 then does f16 MAC) - leaves sm_120 FP8 tensor cores idle; fine for memory-bound decode but slow for batched/prefill. Asymmetric combos silently disabled unless GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON (18x-slow trap, per local memory note). q4_0/q4_1 K can hurt accuracy (no per-channel handling).

leading_impls.approach: Asymmetric, tuning-free 2-bit KV: KEY cache quantized PER-CHANNEL (along feature dim), VALUE cache PER-TOKEN, because key cache has large-magnitude outlier channels (per-channel keeps them isolated) while value has no such structure. group_size=GROUP_SIZE (paper uses 32), residual_length=RESIDUAL_LENGTH recent tokens kept in fp16 (paper uses 128/32). Supports 2/4-bit. CUDA kernel in quant/ folder; per-channel key requires keeping K in a transposed layout so a channel maps to a contiguous group.
leading_impls.refs: arxiv 2402.02750 (KIVI: A Tuning-Free Asymmetric 2bit Quantization for KV Cache); github.com/jy-yuan/KIVI (config.k_bits/v_bits/group_size/residual_length; quant/ CUDA ext)
leading_impls.strengths: 2.6x peak-mem reduction, up to 4x batch, 2.35-3.47x throughput, near-lossless on Llama/Mistral/Falcon. Per-channel-K is the correct theory for why naive low-bit K hurts. Best memory/quality at the aggressive end.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Per-channel K is the hardest to fuse into a flash-attention kernel (channel-wise scale + transposed K layout fights the QK^T contraction; recent fp16 residual window adds a two-path attention). No sm_120 kernel exists; the reference CUDA kernel is research-grade. Heavy integration cost vs the gains on a single-GPU 24GB box.

recommendation.design: Two-tier KV quant. TIER 1 (default): FP8 e4m3 KV with PER-TENSOR-per-head static scales, paged blocks (page_size 64). Quantize K and V to e4m3 on write into the paged cache; run attention in FP8 (QK^T and PV in e4m3, FP32 accumulate). Keep a small recent-token window in BF16 if profiling shows decode-tail sensitivity. Allow per-layer skip (sliding-window / first+last layers stay BF16) - copy vLLM's --kv-cache-dtype-skip-layers semantics. TIER 2 (aggressive, opt-in): KV in low-bit GGML block types for GGUF models - default ASYMMETRIC K=q4_0, V=q8_0 (V is more sensitive, keep it 8-bit; K tolerates 4-bit) OR symmetric q8_0/q8_0 for the safe path. Stretch goal: KIVI-style per-channel-K 2-bit only if a clean sm_120 kernel proves out. Attention engine: use mma.sync-based FP8 kernels (NOT tcgen05). Lift FlashInfer's sm_120 FP8 paged-decode kernel and TRT-LLM XQA as the templates; for the GGUF low-bit tier, lift llama.cpp's fattn-vec dequant-in-kernel design. CRITICAL: build/compile every K/V dtype combo we ship (the asymmetric matrix) - do NOT rely on a symmetric-only default, or asymmetric K/V silently routes to a non-FA fallback (the ~18x trap).
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: FlashInfer v0.4.0+ (FLASHINFER_CUDA_ARCH_LIST including '12.0f'): BatchDecodeWithPagedKVCacheWrapper / BatchPrefillWithPagedKVCacheWrapper with kv_data_type=torch.float8_e4m3fn, q_scale/k_scale/v_scale. TensorRT-LLM XQA kernel (the trtllm_mha path SGLang marks 'Optimized for SM90 and SM120'). vLLM reshape_and_cache_flash (FP8 write + per-head scale array, PR#30141/#30833) and query-quant-fusion (PR#24914) as the cache-write reference. llama.cpp ggml-cuda/fattn-vec.cuh + the FATTN_VEC_CASES_ALL_D table in fattn.cu for the GGUF q4_0/q5_x/q8_0 dequant-in-kernel design; BUILD WITH GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON to get asymmetric K/V. Underlying PTX: mma.sync.m16n8k32 e4m3 with .f32 accumulate (sm_120 has FP8 tensor cores, mma.sync, wgmma.async). AVOID any tcgen05.mma / TMEM kernel (CUTLASS sm_100 GEMM kind, FA3 Blackwell path) - ptxas fails 'instruction not supported' on sm_120 with no fallback.
recommendation.copy_from_sources: FP8 cache write + per-head scales: vLLM reshape_and_cache_flash (PRs #30141, #30833) and query-quant fusion (#24914). FP8 paged attention sm_120: FlashInfer FP8 decode/prefill paged wrappers (github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer, arch 12.0f) + TRT-LLM XQA. Layer-skip + calibration UX: vLLM #33695 + llm-compressor. GGUF low-bit dequant-in-VEC-kernel + asymmetric matrix: llama.cpp ggml/src/ggml-cuda/fattn.cu (FATTN_VEC_CASES_ALL_D) and fattn-vec.cuh, gated by GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS. Aggressive 2-bit reference (stretch): KIVI quant/ CUDA (github.com/jy-yuan/KIVI, group_size 32 / residual 128, per-channel K transpose).
recommendation.rationale: On a 24GB/150W/1.8TB/s laptop, decode is memory-bandwidth- and capacity-bound, not FLOP-bound, so halving KV memory traffic is the single biggest decode lever - FP8 e4m3 gives ~2x with 94-98% AUC at up to 1M ctx (vLLM data) and is the lowest-risk default. e4m3 (not e5m2) is the validated choice: more mantissa, scale handles range. We pick mma.sync FP8 kernels because the decisive sm_120 fact is NO tcgen05/TMEM: the FA3 Blackwell path and CUTLASS sm_100 GEMMs literally won't assemble for sm_120, whereas FlashInfer/XQA do run there. The GGUF low-bit tier is mandatory because the goal requires GGUF + aggressive spilling on 24GB; q8_0 V + q4_0 K balances V-sensitivity vs K-compressibility and matches KIVI's finding that K tolerates more aggressive treatment when handled right. We default per-head/per-tensor (not per-channel-K) for the FP8 tier because per-channel-K cannot be cleanly fused into a flash kernel without transposed-K layout gymnastics; reserve KIVI per-channel-2bit as a stretch. We hard-require compiling the full asymmetric K/V matrix to dodge the silent symmetric-only fallback that costs ~18x.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: sm_120 (consumer Blackwell GB203) has FP8/FP6/FP4 tensor cores and mma.sync + wgmma.async, but has NO tcgen05 instructions and NO TMEM (datacenter sm_100/GB100 only). Any FP8 attention kernel written against tcgen05.mma / TMEM tiling (the sm_100 CUTLASS GEMM path and FlashAttention-3's Blackwell path) fails ptxas with 'instruction not supported' and has no fallback. So FP8-KV attention IS feasible on sm_120, but ONLY via mma.sync-based kernels (FlashInfer / TRT-LLM XQA / llama.cpp VEC), NOT via FA3-Blackwell or CUTLASS sm_100 kernels. Secondary risk: real-world SILENT FP8-KV corruption on sm_120 (no crash, garbage output) observed in production - must validate numerically per model, never assume. And the asymmetric-K/V silent-fallback trap (llama.cpp without GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS) costs ~18x.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: FlashInfer README arch table lists 'SM 12.0, 12.1 = RTX 50 series, DGX Spark' as supported Blackwell, build arch '12.0f', Blackwell support in v0.4.0 (2025-10-08) (github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer). SGLang docs: 'TRTLLM MHA (XQA backend) Optimized for SM90 and SM120, e.g., H20, H200, 5090' with FP8 KV ✅ (github.com/sgl-project/sglang/.../attention_backend.md). FA3 GAP: vLLM issue #22279 - FA3 'only supported on devices with compute capability >= 8 excluding 8.6 and 8.9 and Blackwell archs (>=10)' on RTX 5090; FA issues #1665/#1763 confirm missing sm_120 kernels. Architecture: blackwell-gpu-wiki SM100-vs-SM120 - 'tcgen05: SM100 has it. SM120 does not'; 'TMEM: SM100 256KB/SM, SM120 none'; assembling tcgen05 for sm_120 'ptxas errors instruction not supported, no fallback'; SM120 supports FP8/FP6/FP4 tensor cores via mma.sync; SM120 GEMMs must use smaller register/SMEM-bound accumulator tiles (99KB SMEM, no TMEM); mem BW ~1.8TB/s vs B200 8TB/s. SILENT CORRUPTION: SGLang issue #19603 - Qwen3.5-122B fp8_e4m3 KV produced silent garbage on SM120 (RTX PRO 6000), bf16 KV required; suspected dequant-scale path bug (fix PR#18904). llama.cpp trap: fattn.cu 'if (K->type != V->type) return BEST_FATTN_KERNEL_NONE' unless GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS.

components.beats_competitors_how: "We win on THIS box (single sm_120, 24GB, 1.8TB/s, 150W) by (1) defaulting to FP8 e4m3 KV via mma.sync kernels that actually run on sm_120 - sidestepping the FA3-Blackwell sm_120 gap that breaks vanilla vLLM/FA3 on the 5090, while keeping vLLM's validated 94-98% long-ctx accuracy and ~54%-of-BF16 decode ITL slope; (2) shipping a GGUF-native low-bit tier (q8_0 V / q4_0 K) that llama.cpp users can only get by knowing the obscure GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS build flag - we compile the full asymmetric K/V matrix by default so users never hit the silent ~18x symmetric-only fallback; (3) tuning tile/accumulator shapes for sm_120's no-TMEM, 99KB-SMEM, 255-reg constraint (where sm_100-tuned kernels are mis-sized), and exploiting that decode is memory-bound here so FP8/low-bit KV directly cuts the dominant cost; (4) treating KV-quant correctness as a first-class numeric gate (per-model validation + per-head calibration escape hatch) to avoid the silent-corruption class that bites SGLang/vLLM on consumer Blackwell. Net: best-of-vLLM-accuracy + llama.cpp-GGUF-flexibility + FlashInfer/XQA-sm_120-kernels, with params actually fit to GB203 rather than GB100."
open_questions: Does FlashInfer's FP8 paged-decode kernel on sm_120 accept per-token or per-head k_scale/v_scale, or only per-tensor scalar? (decode docs show scalar float; prefill accepts a tensor but semantics undocumented - must read kernel source / test on the 5090).

Which FP8 sub-format does the FlashInfer/XQA paged wrapper actually use on sm_120 - e4m3 vs e5m2 - and is the dequant-scale plumbing fixed post SGLang PR#18904 (the silent-corruption root cause)? Verify numerically.

Measured sm_120 FP8 mma.sync (m16n8k32 e4m3) tensor-core throughput vs BF16: is FP8 attention compute-faster on GB203, or only memory-faster? (B200 had 2x FP8 FLOPs; consumer Blackwell FP8 may be throttled - needs a local microbench, ties into existing probe/mma_probe.cu).

Can a per-channel-K (KIVI 2-bit) scheme be fused into an mma.sync flash kernel on sm_120 with acceptable speed given no TMEM and transposed-K layout, or is it only viable as a dequant-then-f16-MAC VEC kernel? (decides whether the aggressive tier is FP8/INT8 per-head or true KIVI).
Optimal page_size for our paged FP8 cache on sm_120 given XQA prefers 64 but our spilling/host-offload may want smaller blocks - bench 16/32/64.
For the 24GB+spill scenario, is INT8-per-token KV (simpler, symmetric, tensor-core-friendly) competitive with FP8 e4m3 on accuracy, letting us avoid FP8 dequant-scale fragility entirely?

_verdict.component: KV-cache quantization (FP8 default + low-bit) and the sm_120 attention-kernel integration on consumer Blackwell (RTX 5090 laptop, 24GB, 150W).
_verdict.riskiest_claim: The bundled sm120_feasibility="confirmed" + beats_competitors claim that FlashInfer and TRT-LLM XQA already SHIP liftable, vendor-built sm_120 FP8-KV paged-attention kernels and that defaulting to per-tensor FP8 e4m3 attention via mma.sync "directly cuts the dominant cost" / keeps ~54%-of-BF16 decode ITL on this exact part. This underpins the whole "best-of-FlashInfer/XQA-sm120-kernels + win on the 5090" thesis.
_verdict.evidence: Three findings cut against the strong form of the claim. (1) FlashInfer RFC #3628 (June 2026, by a FlashInfer contributor, with measured microbenchmarks) states verbatim "FlashInfer currently has no block-scaled FP8 attention that runs on SM120" and that the only plain per-tensor FP8 SM120 attention kernel (fmha_v2 e4m3_fp32_*_sm120) "kernel compiles, disabled in the Python API" - i.e. FlashInfer is NOT a turnkey FP8-KV-paged-attention source on sm120; the kernel must be enabled/lifted, which is what this project would do but is not what "ships sm_120 support" implies. (2) SGLang gates trtllm_mha (XQA) behind is_sm100_supported(): on RTX 5090 it raises "ValueError: TRTLLM MHA backend is only supported on Blackwell GPUs (SM100)" (issues #9140 Aug-2025, #14814 Dec-2025; HF model-card discussion confirms SGLang v0.5.9 March-2026 still rejects SM120). So the SGLang docs table that claims trtllm_mha is "Optimized for SM90 and SM120, e.g. 5090" with FP8 KV directly conflicts with the runtime gate - the XQA-on-5090 path is blocked, not a proven fast vendor kernel. (3) The performance lever is half-real: open_question #3 (is FP8 compute-faster on GB203) is answered NO. FlashInfer #3628 measures plain FP8 e4m3/FP32-acc at ~102 TFLOP/s == FP16/F16-acc ~103 (no speedup over FP16), because consumer Blackwell halves FP32-accumulate tensor throughput; only the block-scaled QMMA.SF (MXFP8) path stays full-rate (~202). Independently corroborated on HN by neilmovva ("FP8 and FP16 matmuls run at full speed if accumulating in FP16... but only half speed when accumulating in FP32... removed entirely on RTX Pro 6000") and saagarjha ("2x performance hit from the fp32 accumulation restriction, plus 5090 has 'fake' Blackwell (no tcgen05)"). What IS confirmed: FlashInfer README arch table does list "Blackwell SM 12.0, 12.1 = RTX 50 series, DGX Spark" and FLASHINFER_CUDA_ARCH_LIST 12.0f (v0.4.0, 2025-10-08); the no-tcgen05/use-mma.sync architectural call is correct; silent FP8-KV corruption on sm120 is real (SGLang #19603: Qwen3.5-122B fp8_e4m3 KV = silent garbage, bf16 KV required) though model/path-specific (FP8 KV worked fine on MiniMax-M2.5 on the same SM120 hardware). gau-nernst's from-scratch 5090 BF16 flash-attention hit 92-97% of the 209.5 TFLOP/s BF16 SOL, so the engine goal is feasible - in BF16.
_verdict.correction: Keep the design's foundation (FP8-KV is feasible+useful on sm120; use mma.sync, avoid tcgen05/FA3-Blackwell; compile the full asymmetric K/V matrix; treat correctness as a numeric gate) - all confirmed. But reframe three things: (1) Demote the throughput rationale to MEMORY-ONLY. On GB203 per-tensor FP8 attention is memory-faster, NOT compute-faster (FP32-accumulate throttle => FP8 mma.sync == BF16 rate). Justify Tier-1 FP8 purely by halved KV capacity+bandwidth for memory-bound single-stream decode; do NOT promise compute/ITL wins from FP8 attention math, and expect FP8 to possibly LOSE on compute-bound prefill/batched (cf. vLLM #28234: FP8 weight-quant 17% slower than BF16 on 5090). (2) Stop treating FlashInfer/XQA as drop-in available sm120 FP8 kernels. FlashInfer's plain-FP8 sm120 attention is source-present-but-API-disabled and must be enabled/lifted; trtllm_mha is hard-gated off sm120 in SGLang. Plan to lift+enable FlashInfer's fmha_v2 e4m3_fp32_*_sm120 kernel yourself and validate, rather than calling a shipped wrapper. (3) If you want actual full-rate FP8 tensor throughput on this card, the only path is BLOCK-SCALED MXFP8 (QMMA.SF, mma.sync.kind::mxf8f6f4.block_scale, e4m3 + ue8m0 per-32 scales) - which does not yet exist as attention for sm120 (FlashInfer #3628 is an open RFC; SageAttention3 has a NVFP4 sm120a mainloop to adapt). Treat MXFP8 block-scaled attention as the real compute lever and a build-it item, not FP8 per-tensor. Safest default for the aggressive tier may be the GGUF q8_0-V/q4_0-K dequant-in-VEC path (memory-bound, no FP8 dequant-scale fragility) plus a per-model numeric gate, since FP8 KV silently corrupts on some sm120 model/paths. NOTE: build with -gencode arch=compute_120a,code=sm_120a (per #3628, -arch=sm_120a alone does NOT set the ptxas target and block-scaled instrs then fail).

components.component: MoE kernels + expert routing (fused-MoE GEMM, top-k routing/permutation, quantized grouped GEMM, and 24GB expert spilling/offload) tuned for consumer Blackwell RTX 5090 Laptop (sm_120, 24GB, ~150W) + Arrow Lake-HX CPU.

leading_impls.approach: Two MoE families. (1) Triton fused_moe: moe_align_block_size() sorts/pads topk_ids into sorted_token_ids + expert_ids + num_tokens_post_padded so each BLOCK_SIZE_M block belongs to exactly one expert; invoke_fused_moe_kernel runs a single block-scheduled grouped GEMM (gate+up fused, SiLU, then down). (2) Quantized: native CUTLASS NVFP4 (w4a4) grouped block-scaled GEMM for Blackwell; Marlin MoE (fused_marlin_moe) for W4A16 GPTQ/AWQ; FlashInfer TRTLLM/CUTLASS backends. Backend selected via --moe-backend {triton,marlin,cutlass,flashinfer_cutlass,flashinfer_trtllm}.
leading_impls.refs: vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/fused_moe.py (invoke_fused_moe_kernel, moe_align_block_size), .../moe_align_block_size.py, .../fused_marlin_moe.py; csrc/quantization/fp4/ + CUTLASS NVFP4 grouped GEMM added in PR #21309 (SM120 w4a4); trtllm_nvfp4_moe.py, flashinfer_cutedsl_moe.py. Docs: docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/design/moe_kernel_features/
leading_impls.strengths: Mature, has a working native-FP4 sm_120 path AND a robust Marlin W4A16 sm_120 fallback. sorted_token_ids/expert_ids dispatch is the canonical fused approach and is CUDA-graph-friendly. Best-tested quantized MoE on consumer Blackwell.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Native CUTLASS FP4 grouped GEMM produces garbage on sm_120 unless built with compute_120f (CUDA 13.0+); TMA-WS grouped tactics fail with compute_120a. Triton fused_moe underfills tensor cores at decode (single-stream) batch sizes. Heavy framework; no built-in CPU expert offload.

leading_impls.approach: FusedMoE base + DeepEPMoE (EP variant). Routing -> topk_weights/topk_ids -> token_dispatcher permutes (StandardDispatcher for TP, DeepEPDispatcher for EP) -> grouped GEMM via DeepGemmRunnerCore (DeepGEMM contiguous + masked layouts) or Triton -> combine/unpermute. EPLB (ExpertDistributionRecorder + rebalance_experts) rebalances physical<->logical expert placement by observed token counts. Two-Batch Overlap hides dispatch/combine behind compute.
leading_impls.refs: python/sglang/srt/layers/moe/{moe_runner/deep_gemm.py, token_dispatcher/{standard,deepep}.py}, eplb/{expert_distribution,expert_location}.py, batch_overlap/operations_strategy.py. LMSYS large-scale-EP blog (2025-05-05).
leading_impls.strengths: Best expert-parallel + load-balancing machinery; DeepGEMM masked decode kernels pair with low-latency dispatch and CUDA graphs. EPLB token-count rebalancing is directly reusable as a hot-expert selector for offload.
leading_impls.weaknesses: EP/DeepEP/DeepGEMM are designed for multi-GPU H100/H200 datacenter; DeepGEMM is FP8 + Hopper/sm_90-tuned, not sm_120 warp-mma. Overkill for single 24GB GPU; no first-class CPU offload.

leading_impls.approach: MoE = single GGML_OP_MUL_MAT_ID op (mul_mat_id) that reads the ids tensor and does grouped matmul over selected experts. CPU offload via --cpu-moe/--n-cpu-moe N or -ot regex (e.g. exps=CPU): always-active (attn, dense FFN, shared expert) on GPU, routed experts in CPU RAM. ggml_backend_sched_compute_splits() reads ids at runtime and copies only used expert sub-rows CPU->GPU per pass. GPU-offload prompt processing copies CPU weights to GPU when batch>=GGML_OP_OFFLOAD_MIN_BATCH (default 32). ik_llama adds -sm graph (tensor-parallel-like), -mla, -gr graph reuse, SOTA quants.
leading_impls.refs: ggml/src/ggml-backend.cpp:1445-1564 (expert copy, expert_offset=first_id*expert_size at line 1529), common/arg.cpp:2284/2291, src/llama.cpp:481-509, src/llama-mmap.cpp:436 (POSIX_MADV_WILLNEED). Two-tier cache RFC: issue #20757 + tinyserve PoC. Offload guide: hf.co/blog/Doctor-Shotgun/llamacpp-moe-offload-guide.
leading_impls.strengths: Only engine with mature, simple GGUF MoE + CPU expert offload that runs on a 24GB GPU TODAY. mul_mat_id already does selective per-expert sub-row copy. GGUF-native, KV-quant native. ik_llama best CPU/CUDA hybrid throughput.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Re-copies the same hot experts CPU->GPU every token (no persistent GPU expert cache yet — that is exactly what issue #20757 proposes). No NVFP4; relies on its own Q4_K/IQ quants dequantized in-kernel. sm_120 path is generic CUDA, not FP4-tensor-core-optimized.

leading_impls.approach: Computation offload (not weight transfer): shared experts + attention on GPU, routed experts persistently in CPU RAM and COMPUTED on CPU with AMX/AVX-512 kernels (ARI-aware: AMX for prefill high-ARI, AVX-512 for decode low-ARI). Async CPU-GPU scheduler wraps submit/sync in cudaLaunchHostFunc so the whole decode step is one CUDA graph. Expert Deferral: defer lowest-scoring routed experts to layer k+2 (residual robustness) to overlap CPU experts with next-layer GPU attention; e.g. 5 immediate + 3 deferred. NUMA-aware tensor parallelism over CPU sockets. Marlin Int4 for GPU linears, FlashInfer for attention/MLA.
leading_impls.refs: github.com/kvcache-ai/ktransformers; SOSP25-chen.pdf. YAML injection over HF Transformers (FusedMoE class, MarlinLinear, FlashInferMLA). AMX tiling: 16x64-byte tiles, 64B-aligned, Int4 packed in Int8.
leading_impls.strengths: SOTA single-GPU huge-MoE local inference (671B on one consumer GPU); Expert Deferral (<=0.5% accuracy drop) and single-CUDA-graph scheduler are reusable regardless of CPU kernel. Best blueprint for routing-driven hot/cold expert placement.
leading_impls.weaknesses: CRITICAL for this box: its main lever is Intel AMX (and AVX-512). The target Arrow Lake-HX (Core Ultra 9 275HX) has NO AMX and NO AVX-512 (verified: /proc/cpuinfo shows neither). So KTransformers' CPU-compute path collapses to AVX2/VNNI ~ Llama.cpp-level; its headline speedups do not transfer. Also designed around dual-socket 220GB/s+ NUMA, 1TB RAM; this box has ~12-16GB free RAM.

leading_impls.approach: JIT CUTLASS/TRTLLM-gen fused MoE: cutlass_fused_moe, trtllm_fused_moe (cubin), and CuTeDSL block-scaled grouped GEMM. mm_fp4 / nvfp4 quantize + grouped GEMM. Autotuner picks TMA-WS grouped-GEMM tactics.
leading_impls.refs: flashinfer/jit/fused_moe.py, flashinfer/compilation_context.py, flashinfer/data/csrc/trtllm_fused_moe_kernel_launcher.cu, CuTeDSL cutlass/cute/nvgpu/tcgen05/mma.py. Issues #2577 (mm_fp4 broken sm_120), #2555/#2560 (sm_120 attn).
leading_impls.strengths: Once patched + compute_120f, gives correct native FP4 MoE on sm_120 at ~39 tok/s. Best-engineered grouped-GEMM autotuner and trtllm-gen cubins.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Out of the box mm_fp4 returns all-zeros on sm_120 (cutlass), errors on trtllm/cudnn; capability checks hardcode SM100/family(100), CuTeDSL admissible_archs = sm_100a only. Needs ~10 files patched (issue #3096) plus compute_120f. trtllm cubins are SM100-only and crash on sm_120.

leading_impls.approach: DeepGEMM: FP8 grouped GEMM with contiguous (prefill) + masked (decode) layouts, JIT, Hopper-tuned. DeepEP: all-to-all expert-parallel dispatch (normal + low-latency RDMA/NVLink). Megablocks: block-sparse dMoE matmul (variable-size blocks absorb routing skew without padding waste). ScatterMoE: gather/scatter grouped GEMM avoiding sparse-matrix translation. SonicMoE: fuses gather+activation-load and SwiGLU into grouped-GEMM epilogue.
leading_impls.refs: github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM, DeepEP; databricks/megablocks; ScatterMoE arXiv:2403.08245; SonicMoE arXiv:2512.14080; triton fused-MoE arXiv:2605.23911 (block_id->(expert,offset) schedule, fixed BLOCK_M, register-fused gate+up = ~35% less GMEM traffic).
leading_impls.strengths: Megablocks block-sparse wins at large batch (>=2048 tok) by absorbing skew without padding. SonicMoE epilogue fusion and the register-fused gate+up trick reduce GMEM traffic — directly portable to an sm_120 kernel. ScatterMoE gather-fusion avoids permute buffers.
leading_impls.weaknesses: DeepGEMM/DeepEP are FP8 + Hopper/datacenter + multi-GPU; not sm_120 warp-mma, not single-GPU offload. Megablocks block-sparse advantage only shows at batch>=2048 (irrelevant to single-user 24GB decode where each expert gets ~2 tokens and the regime is memory-bound).

recommendation.design: Build a single-GPU, offload-first fused-MoE engine in two layers. (A) GPU fused-MoE kernel: copy vLLM's dispatch (moe_align_block_size -> sorted_token_ids/expert_ids/num_tokens_post_padded, fixed BLOCK_SIZE_M so each block = one expert) and run a block-scheduled grouped GEMM with the SonicMoE/Triton register-fused gate+up trick (load A-tile once, two accumulators, SiLU+mul in registers, never spill gate/up to GMEM). Two quant paths, both sm_120-correct: (1) PRIMARY for robustness = Marlin-style W4A16 grouped MoE (dequant FP4/INT4 weights to BF16 in-kernel, BF16 mma.sync) — bandwidth-bound, no TMA-WS fragility, currently the FASTEST correct sm_120 MoE (46-49 vs 39 tok/s); (2) NATIVE NVFP4 w4a4 grouped GEMM using the CUTLASS C++ MmaMXF4NVF4Op atom (fixed m16n8k64, .kind::mxf4nvf4 .scale_vec::4X .ue4m3, CTA tile 128x128x128, atom_layout (4,2,1)=8 warps, TMA load of A/B/SFA/SFB into the same producer-consumer pipeline), compiled ONLY with -arch=sm_120a built under CUDA 13.1 with the compute_120f full-feature set to unlock working TMA-WS grouped tactics. Pick path per problem shape; default Marlin for decode, native FP4 for prefill where compute-bound. (B) Offload/spill executor (the real differentiator on 24GB): keep attention + shared expert + dense FFN + KV cache resident on GPU; place routed experts in pinned host RAM (Tier 2) with a PERSISTENT GPU expert-slot cache (Tier 1, N<<n_experts fixed-address slots, SLRU eviction + second-miss admission filter as in llama.cpp issue #20757) so hot experts (~15-20% serve ~80% of tokens) stay resident and per-token PCIe traffic -> ~0 after warmup. The router's topk_ids drives slot lookup; on miss, async H2D copy from pinned RAM into a freed slot, double-buffered to overlap with GPU compute; the grouped-GEMM kernel receives remapped slot indices instead of global expert ids. Use KTransformers' single-CUDA-graph decode (wrap submit/sync in cudaLaunchHostFunc) and Expert Deferral (defer lowest-scored experts by one layer via residual robustness) to hide copy/compute latency. Because this CPU has no AMX/AVX-512, do NOT do CPU expert COMPUTE (KTransformers' AMX lever is dead here); instead spill-and-compute-on-GPU via the slot cache, falling back to a modest AVX2/VNNI CPU grouped GEMM only for cold experts that miss the cache during bursts.
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: CUTLASS (fetch tag with sm_120 NVFP4 grouped GEMM, examples 79/79a/92 + include/cute/atom/mma_traits_sm120.hpp, blockscaled_utils.sm120_make_smem_layout_sfa/sfb, sm120_utils.get_permutation_mnk/get_layoutSFA_TV); kernel atom = cute::nvgpu::warp::MmaMXF4NVF4Op (lowers to mma.sync.aligned.kind::mxf4nvf4.block_scale.scale_vec::4X.m16n8k64.row.col.f32.e2m1.e2m1.f32.ue4m3). Marlin W4A16 MoE kernel (from vLLM csrc/quantization/marlin + fused_marlin_moe.py). vLLM moe_align_block_size + invoke_fused_moe_kernel (Triton) for the dispatch/grouped-GEMM scaffold. Build: nvcc 13.1, -arch=sm_120a, -DCUTLASS_ARCH_MMA_SM120_SUPPORTED=1, -DCUTLASS_GDC_ENABLED, full-feature compute_120f. cuBLASLt 13.2 NVFP4 path as a reference oracle. Offload: CUDA pinned memory + cudaMemcpyAsync double-buffer + cudaLaunchHostFunc single-graph (KTransformers pattern); SLRU slot cache + POSIX_MADV_WILLNEED/DONTNEED for mmap tier (llama.cpp issue #20757 design).
recommendation.copy_from_sources: 1) Dispatch + grouped-GEMM scaffold: vllm/model_executor/layers/fused_moe/fused_moe.py + moe_align_block_size.py (copy the sorted_token_ids/expert_ids/num_tokens_post_padded scheme). 2) Native sm_120 NVFP4 GEMM: CUTLASS examples/79_blackwell_geforce_gemm + python/CuTeDSL/.../blackwell_geforce/.../blockscaled_gemm, and vLLM PR #21309 csrc (proves C++ FP4 grouped GEMM on sm_120). 3) Register-fused gate+up + block-scheduled grouped GEMM: github.com/bassrehab/triton-kernels (moe/expert_gemm.py, moe/fused_moe.py) and SonicMoE epilogue fusion. 4) Marlin W4A16 MoE fallback: vLLM fused_marlin_moe. 5) Offload executor: llama.cpp ggml/src/ggml-backend.cpp:1445-1564 selective expert copy + issue #20757 SLRU two-tier slot cache; KTransformers FusedMoE single-CUDA-graph scheduler (cudaLaunchHostFunc) and Expert Deferral (SOSP25 paper §4). 6) Hot-expert selection: SGLang eplb/expert_distribution.py token-count recorder as the cache-warming/eviction signal.
recommendation.rationale: On a 24GB/150W single GPU the decode regime is memory-bandwidth- and PCIe-bound, not FLOP-bound (DeepSeek-V3-style 256-expert top-8 gives ~2 tokens/expert — Megablocks/DeepGEMM compute tricks don't help). So the wins are: (a) keep weights low-bit (NVFP4 4x, or W4A16) to cut bandwidth; (b) eliminate redundant CPU->GPU expert copies via a persistent SLRU GPU slot cache exploiting routing skew; (c) overlap any unavoidable copy with compute (double-buffer + Expert Deferral + single CUDA graph). Marlin W4A16 is the safe default because on sm_120 today it is both correct AND faster than native FP4 MoE while being immune to the TMA-WS grouped-GEMM fragility; native NVFP4 (compute_120f) is added for compute-bound prefill where its ~3x FP4 throughput matters. Crucially, this avoids the AMX trap: KTransformers' CPU-compute design is mis-matched to this AMX-less CPU, so we keep compute on the GPU and use the CPU only as a paging tier.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: Native NVFP4/MXFP4 grouped-GEMM MoE on sm_120 is correct and fast ONLY when compiled for the full-feature compute_120f target under CUDA 13.0+ (we have CUDA 13.1). With CUDA 12.8 / compute_120a the TMA warp-specialized grouped-GEMM tactics fail to initialize and the autotuner falls back to a ~3x-slow path (or, with vendor-stock vLLM CUTLASS templates, produces row-identical garbage). The CuTe Python DSL still gates FP4 atoms to sm_100a (CUTLASS issue #2800) so the C++/CUTLASS-source path must be used, not the Python DSL. Compute capability is 12.0 (NOT 12.0a/12.0f at the device level) so SM100 cubins crash and any sm_100-only or sm_100a-hardcoded capability check must be widened to family(120). sm_120 uses warp-level mma.sync + RMEM accumulation, NOT tcgen05/TMEM — any kernel written for datacenter sm_100 5th-gen tensor cores will not run.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: Device verified: nvidia-smi reports RTX 5090 Laptop, compute_cap 12.0, 24463MiB; nvcc 13.1 lists compute_120 and compute_121. (1) Colfax Research 'NVFP4 Blockscaled GEMM on RTX Pro Blackwell (SM12x)' (Jun 2026): sm_120 has NO tcgen05/TMEM, uses warp-level mma.sync; the ONLY NVFP4 atom is mma.sync.aligned.kind::mxf4nvf4.block_scale.scale_vec::4X.m16n8k64.row.col.f32.e2m1.e2m1.f32.ue4m3; CUTLASS MmaMXF4NVF4Op, sm120_make_smem_layout_sfa/sfb helpers; benchmarked ~3x FP16 on RTX Pro 6000 (sm_120) with CUDA 13.2. (2) CUTLASS issue #3096 (Mar 2026): on 4x RTX PRO 6000 (sm_120), native FP4 MoE grouped GEMM = garbage with vendor templates; fixed via FlashInfer patches + compute_120f (CUDA 13.0) reaching 39 tok/s native FP4 vs 46-49 tok/s Marlin W4A16; compute_120a only reached 14.6 tok/s (TMA-WS tactics fail). (3) CUTLASS issue #2800: C++ API works on sm_120 (vLLM PR #21309 'Support CUTLASS NVFP4 (w4a4) for SM120'); only the Python DSL admissible_archs is restricted to sm_100a. (4) FlashInfer issue #2577: stock mm_fp4 broken on sm_120 (cutlass=zeros, trtllm/cudnn=error) — must patch capability checks to family(120). (5) vLLM DGX-Spark/sm121 forum thread: native SM120 FP4 grouped GEMM exists in vLLM; --moe-backend marlin is the reliable production path. (6) CUTLASS CHANGELOG 4.2/4.3: 'SM120 mixed input blockscaled grouped GEMM', examples 79/92, and 128x32/128x64 tile support added for sm_120 blockscaled MMA. Conclusion: native FP4 MoE on sm_120 is feasible TODAY on this exact toolchain (CUDA 13.1) via the CUTLASS C++ MmaMXF4NVF4Op path built with the full-feature compute_120f set; Marlin W4A16 is a fully-working, often-faster fallback that needs no FP4 tensor cores at all.

components.beats_competitors_how: "Each leading engine leaves a specific gap on THIS box that we exploit: (1) vLLM/SGLang assume the whole MoE fits in VRAM (or multi-GPU EP) — they have no first-class single-GPU expert spilling, so on 24GB they simply OOM on large MoE. We add a persistent SLRU GPU expert-slot cache (Tier1) over pinned host RAM (Tier2) driven by routing skew, so a model far bigger than 24GB runs and, after warmup, behaves like a resident model (per-token PCIe -> ~0). (2) llama.cpp HAS offload but re-copies the same hot experts CPU->GPU every single token (its own issue #20757 documents this); we implement exactly the two-tier slot cache it lacks, giving the documented ~14 tok/s steady-state vs ~0.5-1 tok/s naive offload — and we use NVFP4/Marlin tensor-core kernels instead of llama.cpp's generic dequant matmul. (3) KTransformers is the strongest huge-MoE-on-one-GPU design but its core speedup is Intel AMX CPU compute, which is DEAD on this AMX-less Arrow Lake-HX CPU; by keeping expert compute on the GPU (slot cache) and reusing only KTransformers' CPU-architecture-agnostic wins (single-CUDA-graph decode via cudaLaunchHostFunc, Expert Deferral), we get its scheduling benefits without its broken-here CPU kernel. (4) Native FP4: we ship the compute_120f-built CUTLASS MmaMXF4NVF4Op grouped GEMM that most engines still get wrong on sm_120 (garbage/zeros), and we pick per-shape between native FP4 (compute-bound prefill, ~3x FP16) and Marlin W4A16 (bandwidth-bound decode, currently fastest correct sm_120 MoE) — neither vLLM nor SGLang auto-selects this on consumer Blackwell. Net: we win on (a) running models that don't fit, (b) bandwidth-optimal low-bit kernels actually correct on sm_120, and (c) copy/compute overlap tuned to one 24GB/150W GPU + a weak-ISA CPU."
open_questions: Measured tok/s of native NVFP4 grouped MoE built with CUDA 13.1 + compute_120f on THIS sm_120 (RTX 5090 Laptop, 150W cap) vs Marlin W4A16 — issue #3096 used 96GB RTX Pro 6000 desktop parts; laptop power/thermal cap and GDDR7 bandwidth (~ a few hundred GB/s) likely shift the crossover. Must benchmark, not assume.

Exact PCIe link width/gen of this laptop (PCIe 5.0 x8? x16?) — determines Tier2->Tier1 H2D bandwidth and thus the break-even GGML_OP_OFFLOAD_MIN_BATCH and whether double-buffering fully hides copies. Need lspci/nvidia-smi -q.

With only ~12-16GB host RAM free (shared with other LLM servers), how many routed experts fit pinned in Tier2 vs must stay mmap'd on disk (Tier3)? For a 256-expert model this may force disk-paging cold experts; need to size the slot cache + pinned region against real free RAM at runtime.

Does the SLRU + second-miss admission policy survive prefill bursts on real traffic for the target models (Qwen3 MoE / DeepSeek-style grouped-topk / gpt-oss)? Hit-rate from the tinyserve PoC (98-100% steady) was on gpt-oss-120B/8GB; must validate on this GPU + chosen models.

Whether to fuse activation quantization (BF16->NVFP4 per-token) into the grouped-GEMM prologue on sm_120 mma.sync — native FP4's BF16->FP4 quant overhead is part of why it trails Marlin; an in-kernel fused quant could close the gap but needs the warp-level fragment layout worked out.

Accuracy impact of Expert Deferral specifically under low-bit (NVFP4/W4A16) experts on the target models — KTransformers showed <=0.5% drop at BF16/Int4 on DeepSeek; needs re-validation for our quant + models.

Is CUTLASS's sm_120 NVFP4 GROUPED GEMM (variable per-expert M) as solid as the DENSE example 79? Issue #3096 shows the grouped path was the broken one; need to confirm the compute_120f fix fully covers grouped/per-group-M with the 128x{32,64,128} tiles and CLC/persistent scheduling for skewed expert token counts.

_verdict.component: MoE kernels + expert routing (native NVFP4 grouped-GEMM feasibility on consumer Blackwell sm_120, RTX 5090 Laptop)
_verdict.riskiest_claim: sm120_feasibility.status="confirmed": that native NVFP4/MXFP4 GROUPED-GEMM MoE (CUTLASS C++ MmaMXF4NVF4Op, TMA-WS grouped tactics) is "correct and fast" on sm_120 when built with compute_120f under CUDA 13.0+, reaching ~39 tok/s, and is shippable as the "native FP4 for compute-bound prefill, ~3x FP16" performance pillar alongside Marlin. The whole "native FP4" half of the two-path design rests on this.
_verdict.evidence: Primary sources contradict the "native grouped GEMM is fast+correct under 120f" claim. (1) The SAME investigator's more careful, more recent vLLM-forum report (discuss.vllm.ai/t/.../2536, Apr 11 2026) states that EVEN WITH compute_120f, "All 80 TMA Warp Specialized grouped GEMM tactics fail at initialization"; the 39-41 tok/s comes from the SLOW non-TMA fallback, still slower than Marlin's 46-50.5 tok/s. The PR author writes the SM120 patches are "necessary but not sufficient ... the underlying CUTLASS TMA WS grouped GEMM kernels still fail at runtime. The real fix needs to come from NVIDIA in the CUTLASS library itself (issue #3096)." So the recommendation's own cited 39 tok/s number is a fallback path, not a working fast native grouped kernel. (2) CUTLASS issue #3096 internally hedges ("likely requires the f suffix... Testing/Pending"); its triumphant "120f enabled the fast TMA WS tactics" line is the optimistic early write-up, walked back in the April benchmark table (rows 5-6: "120f ... 80 TMA WS tactics skipped"). (3) FlashInfer #2577 (CUDA 13, sm_120): stock mm_fp4 cutlass backend "Returns all zeros (silent failure)", trtllm/cudnn error — still broken. (4) vLLM #38718 (Apr 1 2026, RTX 5080 = consumer sm_120): "compute_120a ... does not support TMA WS grouped GEMM tactics needed for NVFP4 MoE, causing illegal instruction crashes; workaround FLASHINFER_CUDA_ARCH_LIST=12.0f" — confirms 120f is needed and the path is still workaround-grade. (5) ALL benchmark evidence (#3096, forum) is on 4x/8x RTX PRO 6000 (96GB) datacenter/workstation parts over PCIe with TP+PP, NOT a single 24GB/150W laptop; zero measurements on the target hardware, and the 397B model used does not fit in 24GB anyway. The one report on the exact target (24GB RTX 5090 Laptop, Qwen3.6-27B) FELL BACK to AutoRound INT4 + Ampere-tuned Marlin, not native NVFP4. WHAT IS CONFIRMED: Marlin W4A16 works and is faster on sm_120 today (46-50.5 tok/s, correct output) - the recommendation correctly makes it the default. compute_120f is the correct/necessary arch. CUTLASS dense NVFP4 (example 79) works. CUTLASS main CHANGELOG now "Enable Blackwell SM120f compilation ... exposes NVFP4/MX Grouped GEMM in the CUTLASS Profiler" plus landed "Fix memory fence for clc scheduler in SM120 pingpong" and "Fix missing SMEM alignment in SM120 scale factors" - so a fast native grouped path is IN-FLIGHT but unproven on sm_120, not "confirmed."
_verdict.correction: Downgrade sm120_feasibility from "confirmed" to "partially confirmed": Marlin W4A16 grouped MoE = the confirmed, fastest-correct sm_120 path TODAY and must be the SOLE shipping default, not just the "decode default." Treat native NVFP4 grouped GEMM as RESEARCH/UNPROVEN on sm_120, not a relied-upon prefill lever: as of the evidence the TMA-WS grouped tactics still fail to initialize even under compute_120f (only dense NVFP4 and the slow non-TMA grouped fallback work), so the claimed "~3x FP16 native FP4 for prefill" is NOT available and would currently be SLOWER than Marlin. Concrete plan: (1) Build/bench the Marlin-style W4A16 grouped MoE first and ship it; do not block the engine on native FP4. (2) Pin a RECENT CUTLASS main/tag (the one whose CHANGELOG says "exposes NVFP4/MX Grouped GEMM" for SM120f and includes the clc-scheduler/SMEM-alignment SM120 fixes) - earlier tags 4.2.1/4.4.1 are confirmed-broken for grouped - build ONLY with -arch=sm_120a under CUDA 13.1 compute_120f, and EMPIRICALLY verify the grouped (variable per-expert M) path with cutlass_profiler on THIS GPU before trusting it; if TMA-WS grouped still falls back, native FP4 yields no win and should be dropped from the hot path. (3) For the compute-bound prefill case where you wanted native FP4, prefer dense NVFP4 (example 79, which IS confirmed working) applied per-expert, or just batch into Marlin - rather than the unproven grouped block-scaled kernel. (4) Stop citing "39 tok/s native FP4" as a target; it is a fallback measurement on 4x96GB workstation cards, not a single 24GB/150W laptop, and is below Marlin. (5) Add a runtime correctness self-check (compare grouped-GEMM output vs a dequant-BF16 oracle on a probe batch at startup) because the failure mode here is SILENT garbage/zeros, not a crash.

components.component: Weight quantization formats + dequant/mixed-input-GEMM kernels (GGUF k/i-quants, GPTQ/AWQ Marlin/Machete, EXL3 trellis, bitsandbytes NF4, Blackwell-native NVFP4/MXFP4/FP8) for single RTX 5090 Laptop (sm_120, 24GB, ~150W)

leading_impls.approach: GGUF k-quants (Q4_K/Q5_K/Q6_K: super-blocks of 256, two-level int scales+mins) and i-quants (IQ2/IQ3/IQ4: codebook/lattice quant). Dequant fused into the MMQ (mul_mat_q) kernels which keep weights quantized and use dp4a/int8 tensor-core MMA on the quantized values, avoiding a separate dequant-to-FP16 pass. cuBLAS fallback dequants to FP16 then GEMM (much slower). Native NVFP4 (GGML_TYPE_NVFP4=40) MMQ added; MXFP4 in ik_llama.cpp.
leading_impls.refs: ggml/src/ggml-cuda/mmq.cu, mmq.cuh, vecdotq.cuh (k-quant/i-quant dp4a dot products), dequantize.cuh, convert.cu; GGML type table ggml.c (GGML_TYPE_Q4_K etc, GGML_TYPE_NVFP4=40). NVFP4 PRs: ggml-org/llama.cpp #20644 (dp4a, merged Mar26), #21074 (generic NVFP4 MMQ, merged Apr1), #22196 (Blackwell-native NVFP4 tensor-core dispatch, build b8967). MMQ-on-Blackwell bug: issues #18250, #19868 (CUDA 13.1 segfault). ik_llama.cpp MXFP4: ikawrakow/ik_llama.cpp #1007.
leading_impls.strengths: Best ecosystem/compat (every quant, GGUF is the de-facto local format). MMQ is extremely fast on sm_120: measured Q4_K_M pp512=5611 t/s, tg128=211 t/s; MXFP4_MOE pp=6566 t/s on RTX 5090 (CUDA 12.8+MMQ). Q6_K for sensitive tensors (attn.v, ffn_down) preserves quality cheaply. Native CPU+GPU spill/offload (-ngl, --override-tensor) is mature — essential for 24GB MoE. K-quants have the most battle-tested quality/size tradeoff at ~4.5-6.5 bpw.
leading_impls.weaknesses: On sm_120, MMQ SEGFAULTS when built with CUDA 13.1 (the machine's default nvcc) — must build with CUDA 12.8 or you silently fall to cuBLAS = 5-6x slower (the FORCE_CUBLAS CMake-cache trap). k-quants do NOT use the FP4 hardware tensor cores; NVFP4 native path is brand-new (Apr 2026) and decode tg is ~unchanged (only prefill +43-68%). i-quant GPU kernels are slower than k-quant MMQ.

leading_impls.approach: Two families. (1) Marlin/Machete = W4A16/W4A8 mixed-input GEMM: weights GPTQ/AWQ int4 or NVFP4, dequantized in-kernel into FP16/BF16 registers, then fed to standard FP16 tensor cores (mma). Dequant-then-MMA fused in one kernel with async copy + pipelining. (2) Native Blackwell CUTLASS NVFP4: cutlass_scaled_mm using OpClassBlockScaledTensorOp (true FP4 tensor-core MMA) for W4A4/W4A8 + grouped GEMM for MoE.
leading_impls.refs: csrc/quantization/marlin/ (gptq_marlin, awq_marlin, marlin moe), csrc/quantization/machete/ (Machete, Hopper-gen, CUTLASS-based); csrc/quantization/fp4/nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu, nvfp4_blockwise_moe_kernel.cu (ENABLE_NVFP4_SM120); vllm/model_executor/layers/quantization/{mxfp4.py,marlin_utils_fp4.py}. PRs: #21309 (CUTLASS NVFP4 w4a4 SM120, MERGED 2025-08-03, tested RTX 5090, +330/-14); commits e502098 (NVFP4 MoE CUTLASS SM120), c0dfc89 (SM120 device guard/runtime dispatch). v0.12.0 release notes: NVFP4 W4A8 Marlin for SM120. Issue #31085 (MoE FP4 falls back to Marlin), #37714 (SM120+CUDA13 install).
leading_impls.strengths: Marlin gptq_marlin/awq_marlin CONFIRMED WORKING on sm_120 today (RTX 5090, even RTX 5060 Ti): the reliable W4A16 path that 'dequantizes FP4/int4 to FP16 before compute', bypassing broken native FP4 kernels. Native CUTLASS NVFP4 dense W4A4/W4A8 works and gives real prefill speedups. cutlass_scaled_mm_supports_fp4(120)==True. Best concurrent throughput + TTFT (PagedAttention, continuous batching).
leading_impls.weaknesses: Native FP4 GROUPED/MoE GEMM is BROKEN on sm_120 (returns garbage/zeros) — CUTLASS #3096, FlashInfer #2577; MoE forces --moe-backend marlin (~47 t/s on Qwen 397B, slow). Marlin is dequant-then-FP16 so it does NOT exploit FP4 tensor cores for compute — it's a memory-savings + decent-speed path, not peak FP4 throughput. Install on sm_120+CUDA13 is an 8-step landmine (vLLM #37714). Heavy Python runtime, pre-allocates VRAM (bad for 24GB flexibility).

leading_impls.approach: The actual sm_120 narrow-precision GEMM templates everyone copies. Examples 79a-d (blackwell_geforce_gemm) and 87 (geforce_gemm_blockwise), 72/84 (narrow_precision), 92 (blackwell_moe_gemm). Block-scaled NVFP4/MXFP8/MXFP6 GEMM using OpClassBlockScaledTensorOp and mma.sync.aligned.block_scale. Warp-specialized persistent kernel (cooperative/ping-pong), SW dynamic scheduler via cluster launch control. E2M1 4-bit elements + per-16 (NVFP4, FP8 E4M3 scale) or per-32 (MXFP4, E8M0 scale) block scale factors with interleaved SF layout.
leading_impls.refs: examples/79_blackwell_geforce_gemm/79a_blackwell_geforce_nvfp4_bf16_gemm.cu (W4A16: ElementA/B=nv_float4_t<float_e2m1_t>, ElementC/D=bfloat16_t, ArchTag=cutlass::arch::Sm120, ThreadBlockShape Shape<_128,_128,_128>, ClusterShape Shape<_1,_1,_1>, CollectiveBuilder + GemmUniversal), 79b (nvfp4_nvfp4 W4A4), 79c (mixed mxfp8/mxfp6/bf16), 79d (nvfp4 grouped GEMM for MoE); include/cutlass/detail/sm100_blockscaled_layout.hpp; CUTLASS_ARCH_MMA_SM120_SUPPORTED guard. CUTLASS releases note 'Enable Blackwell SM120f compilation, expose NVFP4/MX Grouped GEMM in profiler.'
leading_impls.strengths: Authoritative, source-available (BSD-3) sm_120 FP4 kernels — copy directly. Confirms hardware: NVFP4 block-scaled MMA has 2x MXFP8 and 4x Ada-FP8 throughput on GeForce Blackwell. 79a (nvfp4 weights x bf16 acts -> bf16) is exactly the W4A16 fast path we want for decode. CUDA 12.8+ compiles it. Locally available under flashinfer/data/cutlass and deep_gemm/include/cutlass.
leading_impls.weaknesses: GeForce sm_120 lacks SM100 features: no TMA multicast (cluster must be 1x1x1), no dynamic datatypes, no tcgen05 5th-gen MMA — so peak FP4 TFLOPs are well below datacenter B200. Grouped-GEMM path (79d/92) is the one with reported garbage-output bugs on sm_120 (CUTLASS #3096). C++ template-heavy; long compile times; need to wire scale-factor layouts correctly (interleaved SFA/SFB).

leading_impls.approach: QTIP-based trellis quantization, 1-8 bpw arbitrary (e.g. 3.25 bpw mixed). Blockwise Hadamard transform (error decorrelation) + LDLQ sweep + Viterbi trellis search offline. Inference: LinearEXL3 dispatches exl3_gemm / exl3_gemv / exl3_mgemm CUDA kernels (Marlin-inspired multi-stage pipeline). Dequant on-the-fly from a PROCEDURAL codebook generated in-register via an LCG (codebook never stored in VRAM), feeding mma.m16n8k16 tensor-core MMA. 16x16 weight tiles match tensor-core input.
leading_impls.refs: exllamav3/exllamav3_ext/quant/exl3_gemm.cu (lines 22-36 permuted layout, 211-415 kernel), exl3_gemm_inner.cuh, exl3_gemm_kernel.cuh, exl3_gemv.cu (M<=8 token-gen), exl3_kernel_map.cu (CC-based shape dispatch + tuning cache; 4 tile shapes TILESIZE_M=16, N=128/256/512); exllamav3/modules/quant/exl3.py (kernel mode bsz<=32 vs torch reconstruct mode). doc/exl3.md (perplexity tables).
leading_impls.strengths: Best quality-per-bit at low bpw: near-FP16 at 6-8 bpw, highly competitive at 3 bpw (beats GPTQ/EXL2/AWQ, rivals QuIP#). mma.m16n8k16 is standard tensor-core — works on Ampere through sm_120 (no FP4 hardware needed), so it runs on the 5090 today. In-register LCG codebook = tiny metadata, great for 24GB. Arbitrary per-layer/expert bitrate is ideal for spilling-sensitive MoE.
leading_impls.weaknesses: No FP4-hardware acceleration (uses int/FP16 tensor cores) — so on Blackwell it leaves the FP4 2-4x throughput on the table vs native NVFP4. Decode on a 5090 (per community: ~40 t/s GLM-4.5-Air 5bpw on RTX 6000) trails AWQ-Marlin/vLLM. Trellis decode in-kernel is compute-heavier per byte than k-quant dp4a. Smaller ecosystem; .safetensors EXL3 format, not GGUF; MoE batched kernel less mature.

leading_impls.approach: bitsandbytes NF4 = 4-bit NormalFloat, blockwise (block 64) double-quant, dequant-to-FP16 then cuBLAS (no fused mixed-input GEMM) — training/QLoRA-oriented, slow inference. modelopt = NVIDIA's quantizer that PRODUCES NVFP4/MXFP4/FP8 checkpoints (the source of HF NVFP4 uploads); by default keeps self-attention in BF16 (accuracy), quantizing only MLP/MoE.
leading_impls.refs: NVIDIA/TensorRT-Model-Optimizer (NVFP4_DEFAULT_CFG, nvidia-modelopt 0.43.0); bitsandbytes csrc/kernels.cu (kDequantizeBlockwise, NF4 LUT). HF example sakamakismile/Qwen3.6-27B-Text-NVFP4-MTP (~14GB, built/tested RTX PRO 6000 SM120).
leading_impls.strengths: modelopt is the de-facto NVFP4/MXFP4 checkpoint producer (we consume its outputs). NF4 is the QLoRA standard if we ever fine-tune. Documents the practical 'NVFP4 != uniform 4-bit' insight (attn in BF16) that affects size budgeting.
leading_impls.weaknesses: bitsandbytes NF4 inference is slow (dequant->cuBLAS, no fused kernel, no sm_120 FP4 path) — not competitive for serving; skip for inference. modelopt is a quantizer not a kernel — no runtime contribution beyond format.

recommendation.design: Three-tier weight-quant support, prioritized for sm_120 + 24GB + ~150W. TIER 1 (ship first) — GGUF k-quants Q4_K/Q5_K/Q6_K via a llama.cpp-style fused MMQ path: keep weights quantized, dp4a/int8-MMA dot products, NO dequant-to-FP16 pass. This is the compatibility + proven-speed baseline (pp 5611 / tg 211 t/s measured on a 5090) and gives mature CPU/disk spill for MoE. TIER 2 (Blackwell fast path) — native NVFP4 W4A16 dense GEMM lifted from CUTLASS example 79a (nvfp4 weights x bf16 activations -> bf16, OpClassBlockScaledTensorOp, mma.sync.aligned.block_scale, tile 128x128x128, cluster 1x1x1) for the bandwidth-bound decode + the +43-68% prefill win on attention/MLP linear layers. Pair with an FP8 (E4M3) KV/activation path on the same block-scaled tensor cores. TIER 3 (MoE + quality) — for MoE experts use a Marlin-style W4A16 dequant-then-FP16-MMA grouped kernel (NOT native FP4 grouped GEMM, which is broken on sm_120), exactly as vLLM's --moe-backend marlin does; optionally add EXL3 trellis for highest quality at 3-3.5bpw when fitting larger models in 24GB matters more than peak speed. Dispatch by problem shape: GEMV/native-NVFP4 for decode (M<=8/32), MMQ or CUTLASS dense for prefill, Marlin-grouped for MoE.
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: CUTLASS (fetch NVIDIA/cutlass main, BSD-3): examples 79a (nvfp4_bf16 W4A16 — primary decode kernel), 79b (nvfp4_nvfp4 W4A4), 79c (mxfp8/mxfp6 mixed), 79d + example 92 (nvfp4 grouped/MoE — use as reference but expect bugs); include/cutlass/detail/sm100_blockscaled_layout.hpp; guard CUTLASS_ARCH_MMA_SM120_SUPPORTED; ElementA/B = cutlass::nv_float4_t<cutlass::float_e2m1_t>, ArchTag = cutlass::arch::Sm120, OpClassBlockScaledTensorOp. llama.cpp ggml CUDA: mmq.cu/mmq.cuh/vecdotq.cuh (Q4_K/Q5_K/Q6_K + NVFP4 dp4a/MMQ), dequantize.cuh. vLLM: csrc/quantization/marlin/ (gptq_marlin, awq_marlin, marlin_moe) and csrc/quantization/fp4/nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu + nvfp4_blockwise_moe_kernel.cu (ENABLE_NVFP4_SM120). ExLlamaV3: exllamav3_ext/quant/exl3_gemm.cu + exl3_gemv.cu + exl3_kernel_map.cu (trellis, mma.m16n8k16, in-register LCG codebook). BUILD: compile MMQ/ggml with CUDA 12.8 (nvcc at /usr/local/cuda-12.8 — sm_120 supported there); CUDA 13.1 segfaults llama.cpp MMQ on Blackwell. CUTLASS FP4 needs CUDA >=12.8 (13.1 fine for CUTLASS).
recommendation.copy_from_sources: NVFP4/MXFP4 dense+grouped sm_120 GEMM: NVIDIA/cutlass examples/79_blackwell_geforce_gemm/79a..79d + 87 + 92 (copy template instantiation + scale-factor layout verbatim; Sm120, cluster 1x1x1). k-quant + NVFP4 MMQ dot-product kernels: ggml-org/llama.cpp ggml/src/ggml-cuda/mmq.cuh + vecdotq.cuh + PRs #20644/#21074/#22196. Marlin int4/NVFP4 W4A16 + MoE grouped (the working sm_120 path): vllm-project/vllm csrc/quantization/marlin/* and nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu (PR #21309, commit e502098). Trellis high-quality low-bpw: turboderp-org/exllamav3 exl3_gemm.cu/exl3_gemv.cu. Checkpoint production: NVIDIA/TensorRT-Model-Optimizer (NVFP4_DEFAULT_CFG).
recommendation.rationale: sm_120 native FP4 hardware is PROVEN (CUTLASS 79a: block-scaled tensor-core MMA, NVFP4=2x MXFP8=4x Ada-FP8; cutlass_scaled_mm_supports_fp4(120)==True) but the ecosystem is uneven: dense NVFP4/Marlin work on the 5090 today, native FP4 grouped/MoE GEMM is broken (returns zeros — FlashInfer #2577, CUTLASS #3096). Therefore the safe, fast, copyable plan is k-quant MMQ first (compat + proven 5090 throughput + mature spill for the 24GB constraint), then CUTLASS 79a dense NVFP4 as the Blackwell decode/prefill accelerator, with Marlin-style W4A16 grouped GEMM for MoE (matching vLLM's own workaround) instead of the broken native FP4 MoE kernel. This is the only configuration where every kernel is verified to run correctly on sm_120 with the CUDA 12.8 MMQ build requirement respected.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: Native FP4 hardware MMA exists on sm_120 and dense NVFP4 + Marlin int4/NVFP4 W4A16 work today, BUT (1) native FP4 GROUPED/MoE GEMM is broken on sm_120 (silent zeros/garbage) so MoE must use a Marlin dequant-to-FP16 fallback, and (2) llama.cpp's MMQ kernel SEGFAULTS when built with CUDA 13.1 (this machine's default nvcc) on Blackwell — it MUST be built with CUDA 12.8 (also present here at /usr/local/cuda-12.8) or it silently falls back to cuBLAS at 5-6x slower. sm_120 GeForce also lacks tcgen05/TMA-multicast (cluster forced 1x1x1), so peak FP4 TFLOPs are far below datacenter B200.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: CUTLASS example 79a (NVIDIA/cutlass examples/79_blackwell_geforce_gemm) explicitly: 'GEMM example for NVIDIA Blackwell SM120... blockscaled NVFP4... uses the new Block Scaled Tensor Core MMA Instructions (mma.sync.aligned.block_scale). NVFP4 MMA has 2x throughput vs MXFP8 and 4x vs Ada FP8'; ArchTag=cutlass::arch::Sm120, ElementA/B=nv_float4_t<float_e2m1_t>, ClusterShape Shape<_1,_1,_1> with note 'GeForce RTX 50 series do not support multicast TMA / dynamic datatypes', requires CUDA 12.8+. vLLM PR #21309 (MERGED 2025-08-03, 'Tested on NVIDIA RTX 5090 (Blackwell, SM120), all unit tests pass') adds CUTLASS NVFP4 W4A4 for SM120. vLLM issue #31085: cutlass_scaled_mm_supports_fp4(120)==True; kernels csrc/quantization/fp4/nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu compiled; vLLM v0.12.0 ships NVFP4 W4A8 Marlin for SM120 (r/LocalLLaMA, Dec 2025). BROKEN MoE: FlashInfer #2577 (cutlass mm_fp4 returns all zeros on SM120, cudnn/trtllm fail), CUTLASS #3096 (SM120 grouped GEMM garbage); workaround --moe-backend marlin gives ~47 t/s on Qwen 397B (r/LocalLLaMA Mar 2026). llama.cpp: CUDA 12.8+MMQ Q4_K_M pp512=5611/tg128=211 t/s vs CUDA 13.1+MMQ = segfault (zenn.dev rtx5090 benchmark; llama.cpp issues #18250/#19868); native NVFP4 MMQ b8967 = +43-68% prefill (PRs #20644/#21074/#22196). Local verify: 'nvcc --list-gpu-code' shows sm_120/sm_121 on both CUDA 13.1 and 12.8; GPU compute_cap=12.0.

components.beats_competitors_how: "We beat vLLM/SGLang on this exact box by avoiding their two sm_120 failure modes: (a) we build the MMQ/ggml path with CUDA 12.8 by default so we never silently drop to cuBLAS (the 5-6x trap that bites naive 5090 setups), and (b) we route MoE through a working Marlin W4A16 grouped kernel instead of the broken native FP4 MoE GEMM, so MoE never returns garbage or stalls at 47 t/s. We beat llama.cpp by stacking the native NVFP4 block-scaled tensor-core path (CUTLASS 79a) onto the k-quant baseline for dense attention/MLP linears — capturing the +43-68% prefill win llama.cpp only just got, plus the FP4 bandwidth saving (14GB vs 17GB for a 27B), while keeping llama.cpp's mature CPU/disk spill for the 24GB ceiling. We beat all of them on memory pressure by mixing per-layer bitrate (k-quant attn.v/ffn_down at Q6_K, NVFP4 MLP, EXL3 3-3.5bpw for experts that must spill) tuned to a 150W power-bound, bandwidth-limited part rather than a datacenter B200 profile they target. No FP4-grouped-MoE dependency, no CUDA-13 MMQ crash, no Python pre-allocation overhead = a thinner, correctly-built engine on hardware where the big engines are still fighting open upstream issues."
open_questions: Native FP4 grouped/MoE GEMM on sm_120: is CUTLASS #3096 / FlashInfer #2577 fixed in a recent CUTLASS commit (the compute_120f / CUDA 13.0 patches reportedly produced 'first correct native FP4 MoE output' at reduced speed)? If a correct fast grouped kernel exists, MoE could skip the Marlin fallback — needs an empirical correctness+speed test on this GPU.

Exact decode-time win of native NVFP4 W4A16 (CUTLASS 79a) vs k-quant MMQ Q4_K on a 150W-capped laptop 5090: community data shows native NVFP4 helps PREFILL (+57%) but tg128 was ~unchanged (73.7->73.6) — is the FP4 tensor-core path actually worth it for our decode-dominated single-user workload, or is k-quant MMQ already memory-bound-optimal?

MXFP4 (block 32, E8M0 scale) vs NVFP4 (block 16, E4M3+FP32 two-level) quality/speed tradeoff on sm_120 for OUR target models — MXFP4 has wider ecosystem (gpt-oss native) and 1 fewer scale level; need a head-to-head perplexity + tok/s on the 5090.

Can the CUTLASS 79a kernel be JIT/AOT-compiled with the machine's CUDA 13.1 without the MoE bug while we keep the separate llama.cpp MMQ build on CUDA 12.8 — i.e. confirm CUTLASS dense FP4 is unaffected by the 13.1 MMQ segfault (different codegen path), so we don't need a dual-toolkit build for dense.

EXL3 trellis decode throughput on sm_120 specifically (not RTX 6000): is its non-FP4 mma.m16n8k16 path fast enough at 3-3.5bpw to justify inclusion over NVFP4, given it leaves the FP4 tensor cores idle? Needs a 5090 benchmark.

FP8 (E4M3) weight/activation + FP8 KV path on sm_120 block-scaled tensor cores: throughput and accuracy vs FP4 for the layers modelopt keeps in BF16 (attention) — could a W8A8 FP8 attention + W4A16 NVFP4 MLP hybrid beat all-FP4?

_verdict.component: Weight quantization formats + dequant/mixed-input-GEMM kernels (GGUF k/i-quants, GPTQ/AWQ Marlin/Machete, EXL3 trellis, NVFP4/MXFP4/FP8) for single RTX 5090 Laptop (sm_120, 24GB, ~150W)
_verdict.riskiest_claim: The sm_120 kernel-availability matrix the whole 3-tier design rests on: "dense NVFP4 works on sm_120 today — copy CUTLASS example 79a verbatim (Sm120, cluster 1x1x1) as the primary W4A16 decode/prefill kernel; only native FP4 grouped/MoE GEMM is broken (use Marlin W4A16 grouped); build llama.cpp MMQ with CUDA 12.8 (13.1 segfaults)." If 79a doesn't actually run correctly on a 5090, TIER 2 (the Blackwell fast path) collapses.
_verdict.evidence: Architecture-level claims confirmed by primary sources, but the specific "copy 79a verbatim" instruction is REFUTED. (1) CUDA 12.8 MMQ requirement CONFIRMED: zenn.dev RTX5090 benchmark shows CUDA 13.1 + MMQ = segfault; CUDA 12.8 + MMQ = Q4_K_M pp512=5611/tg128=211, MXFP4_MOE pp=6566/tg=201 (matches agent's numbers exactly); NVIDIA's own Blackwell migration guide recommends CUDA 12.8; llama.cpp #18250/#19868. But the build is MORE fragile than "just use 12.8": additional Blackwell MMQ crashes exist independent of the toolkit trap — #19662 (MXFP4 'mma with block scale' not supported on sm_120 at ptxas), #23385 (sharedMemPerBlockOptin driver-bug MMQ crash), ollama #14374 (-O3 codegen bug). (2) Native FP4 MoE/grouped broken CONFIRMED: FlashInfer #2577 (cutlass mm_fp4 returns all-zeros, cudnn/trtllm fail on (12,0)); CUTLASS #3096 (grouped GEMM garbage output, persists through CUTLASS 4.4.1); Marlin W4A16 MoE fallback ~46-50.5 t/s confirmed across multiple independent 5090/PRO6000 benchmarks. (3) REFUTATION of the copy-source: CUTLASS #2906 shows example 79a (79a_blackwell_geforce_nvfp4_bf16_gemm.cu) CRASHES with 'Misaligned shared or local address' on RTX 5090 sm_120 (CUDA 13.1, CUTLASS 4.3.4) — its TMA mainloop sm120_blockscaled_mma_tma.hpp lacks alignas(64)/alignas(128) on Params/scale-factor smem; only PASSES (3043 GFLOPS) after a source patch. So 79a is NOT drop-in correct. (4) But the BROADER 'dense NVFP4 works' claim SURVIVES: CUTLASS #3096 explicitly states 'Dense (non-MoE) FP4 GEMM works correctly'; vLLM's own dense kernel nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu (a non-TMA path, distinct from 79a) + flashinfer JIT deliver correct dense NVFP4 in production (Allen Kuo Gemma-4 NVFP4 on desktop Blackwell, Apr 2026; Qwen3.6-27B NVFP4 on single 5090 at 200k ctx, May 2026). (5) Two open questions resolved AGAINST the optimistic framing: native FP4 MoE is now correct-but-slow via flashinfer + compute_120f/CUDA 13.0 (39 t/s, still < Marlin's 46-49; vLLM PR #40082 merged) — so 'broken' has become 'works slowly with a different toolkit'; and the decode-win skepticism is VINDICATED — real benchmarks show NVFP4 wins TTFT/prefill ~6-10x but LOSES single-user decode to Q4_K_M/Ollama by 24-30%, confirming TIER 1 k-quant MMQ is the decode-optimal path and TIER 2's decode value is marginal for a single-user workload.
_verdict.correction: Keep the 3-tier architecture (it matches what 5090 practitioners actually run) but fix three things. (a) Do NOT copy CUTLASS example 79a verbatim as the dense decode kernel — it segfaults on sm_120 (misaligned address, CUTLASS #2906) until you apply the alignas(64) on Params/TMA descriptors and alignas(128) on smem_SFA/smem_SFB in sm120_blockscaled_mma_tma.hpp + sm90_epilogue_tma_warpspecialized.hpp. Preferred: lift vLLM's NON-TMA dense kernel csrc/quantization/fp4/nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu (confirmed working on 5090 via PR #21309) instead of 79a's TMA mainloop, OR use example 87 (geforce_gemm_blockwise) whose mainloop already uses array_aligned. (b) Treat 'use CUDA 12.8' as necessary-but-not-sufficient for the MMQ build: also pin a known-good llama.cpp tag and watch the smpbo/-O3/ptxas block-scale issues (#23385, #19662, #14374); validate MMQ output on a smoke prompt at build time, not just that it links. (c) Reframe TIER 2: NVFP4 is a PREFILL/TTFT accelerator and a VRAM-saver, not a decode win on this box — for the single-user decode-dominated workload, k-quant MMQ Q4_K (TIER 1) is the throughput baseline to beat, and the realistic 'beats competitors' lever is TTFT + memory-fit + a clean correctly-built stack, NOT raw decode tok/s (Q4_K_M/Ollama currently win single-user decode by 24-30%). For MoE, Marlin W4A16 grouped remains the right default; native FP4 MoE via flashinfer compute_120f (CUDA 13.0) is now correct but still slower than Marlin, so list it as optional/experimental, not the target.

components.component: Spilling / offload (weights, experts, KV) — tiered VRAM ↔ pinned host RAM ↔ mmap'd NVMe on RTX 5090 Laptop (sm_120, 24GB VRAM, ~12-16GB free host RAM, PCIe gen5 x8, 2× fast NVMe)

leading_impls.approach: Static tensor-placement offload + mmap. -ngl puts whole layers on GPU; the modern lever is -ot/--override-tensor regex assigning specific tensors to a backend buffer, plus convenience flags --cpu-moe / --n-cpu-moe N. Canonical MoE recipe: keep attention + dense FFN + shared-expert + router + KV on GPU (-ngl 999), push routed-expert FFN to CPU with -ot 'exps=CPU' or 'blk\.([0-9]|...)\.=CUDA0,exps=CPU' to claw back layers onto GPU until VRAM is full. Weights are mmap'd from the GGUF file: the page cache IS the host-RAM tier and the GGUF file IS the disk tier (no explicit copy). Prompt-processing 'op offload' copies CPU-resident weights to GPU as one batch when batch >= GGML_OP_OFFLOAD_MIN_BATCH (default 32) because GPU GEMM beats CPU for large batches. CPU expert GEMM uses ggml's own quant-aware kernels (Q4_K/Q5_K dot products, AVX2/AVX512); no async expert prefetch.
leading_impls.refs: ggml-org/llama.cpp PR #11397 (slaren, --override-tensor); --cpu-moe/--n-cpu-moe in src/llama.cpp arg parsing; discussion #13154 (-ot regex docs); HF blog Doctor-Shotgun/llamacpp-moe-offload-guide (Feb 2026) for exact regex + GGML_OP_OFFLOAD_MIN_BATCH + NUMA(drop_caches+--mmap+--numa distribute migration); ik_llama.cpp -sm graph, -mla 3, -amb, --merge-qkv; ggml-cuda backend buffer types ggml_backend_cpu_buffer_type / cuda_buffer_type
leading_impls.strengths: Zero-copy mmap means disk-spill is free and automatic — model bytes never need to fit in RAM; the OS page cache transparently caches hot pages and evicts cold under pressure (exactly our ~12-16GB-free constraint). GGUF + KV-quant (Q8_0/Q4_0/Q5_1 K and V via -ctk/-ctv) are first-class. Regex placement is fully manual and deterministic, easy to copy. CPU MoE GEMM is well-tuned and quant-native (no dequant-to-fp16 round trip).
leading_impls.weaknesses: Placement is STATIC per run — no runtime LRU/prefetch of experts; a CPU-resident expert is recomputed on CPU every token (cold-miss = full CPU GEMM, not a GPU fetch). No speculative expert prediction. op-offload only helps prefill, not decode. mmap cold-start has page-fault latency; first token after drop_caches stalls on disk. NUMA handling is poor (irrelevant here: single NUMA node).

leading_impls.approach: Architecture-aware static partition: GPU runs attention/MLA + KV-cache + dense + shared experts; CPU runs the routed-expert MoE GEMM with hand-tuned kernels (Intel AMX INT4/INT8, AVX512, or a llamafile/GGUF AVX2 backend). YAML 'optimize_rules' match tensor-name regex to a device+operator class (e.g. KTransformersExperts on CPU, KDeepseekV2Attention on cuda). kt-kernel exposes --kt-num-gpu-experts N (hot experts kept resident on GPU), --kt-max-deferred-experts-per-token (pipeline CPU expert compute behind GPU work via submit_with_cuda_stream/sync_forward async API), --kt-expert-placement-strategy {uniform,frequency,front-loading,random} and --kt-enable-dynamic-expert-update (re-place GPU experts from live routing stats during prefill).
