leading_impls.refs: kvcache-ai/ktransformers: kt-kernel/README.md, doc/en/AMX.md, doc/en/DeepseekR1_V3_tutorial.md, doc/en/kt-kernel/experts-sched-Tutorial.md; KTMoEWrapper (kt_kernel) forward/submit_forward/sync_forward; CPUInfer.submit_with_cuda_stream; convert_cpu_weights.py (INT4/INT8); LLAMAFILE backend reads GGUF Q4_K/Q5_K directly; SGLang integration via sglang-kt fork
leading_impls.strengths: Best-in-class CPU expert throughput (AMX 255+ tok/s prefill on Xeon vs 10 in llama.cpp). The 'GPU = always-active params, CPU = routed experts' split is the canonical MoE offload design and exactly matches our hardware reasoning. num-gpu-experts gives a real hot-expert GPU tier; deferred-experts pipelines CPU compute behind GPU; frequency/dynamic placement is a profiling-driven hot/cold scheme. GGUF supported via LLAMAFILE backend. CPU kernels are arch-agnostic so sm_120 is irrelevant for the CPU path.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Prebuilt CUDA wheels target only SM 80/86/89/90 — NOT sm_120; the GPU side must be built from source for Blackwell-consumer. AMX backend needs Intel AMX (our Core Ultra 9 275HX is a CLIENT chip WITHOUT AMX → falls back to AVX512/AVX2 llamafile path, losing the AMX win). Designed for big-RAM servers (382GB-1TB DRAM): assumes the whole CPU-expert set fits in RAM, which it will NOT on our 12-16GB-free box → must add a disk tier ktransformers does not have. Heavy Python/torch dependency.

leading_impls.approach: Dynamic per-expert offload with LRU cache + speculative prefetch. Keeps k least-recently-used experts PER LAYER resident on GPU (k=2 for 12GB, k=4 for 16GB GPUs); non-expert params fully on GPU. Speculative loading: applies the NEXT layer's gating function to the CURRENT layer's hidden state to GUESS the next 1-2 experts and async-prefetches them while the current layer computes (does not change outputs; pure latency hide). Experts stored in one contiguous PINNED host buffer (tensor.pin_memory()); b=4 shared on-device staging buffers do async H2D copies. When host RAM can't hold all experts (Colab case), experts split across host RAM and disk. Mixed HQQ quant: non-expert layers 4-bit, experts 2-3 bit.
leading_impls.refs: dvmazur/mixtral-offloading (src/expert_cache.py LRU, src/expert_wrapper.py, build_model.py, MixtralExpertWrapper); arXiv 2312.17238 §3.1 (LRU k), §3.2 (speculative loading via next-layer gate on current activation), HQQ (mobiusml/hqq) 4-bit attn / 2-3-bit experts group_size 64
leading_impls.strengths: The two ideas to copy verbatim: (1) per-layer LRU expert cache exploiting temporal expert locality (experts reused across adjacent tokens), (2) speculative next-expert prefetch by running next gate early — overlaps the PCIe fetch with current-layer compute, the single biggest decode-latency lever for offloaded MoE. Contiguous pinned buffer + small ring of staging buffers + async copy is the exact transfer mechanism. 2-4 tok/s Mixtral-8x7B on T4/3060/3080-mobile, fully PCIe-bound (proves the design works on a laptop-class GPU like ours).
leading_impls.weaknesses: Speculative hit-rate is imperfect (gate-on-stale-activation is a heuristic; misses → fall back to JIT load). LRU ignores activation frequency and cross-layer patterns. PyTorch reference impl, not a fast engine. Disk tier is rudimentary. Decode is still bandwidth-bound at single-digit tok/s — the prefetch hides latency but cannot beat the PCIe ceiling.

leading_impls.approach: Activation-aware offload driven by sequence-level tracing. Records an Expert Activation Matrix (EAM) per sequence; an EAM-collection algorithm picks representative traces. Prefetch PRIORITY for an expert = (predicted activation ratio from EAM) × (proximity: how near the expert's layer is to the layer currently executing) — so it prefetches experts for the current AND subsequent layers, progressively sharpening priorities as generation proceeds. Sparsity-aware cache keeps high-priority experts; parameters live on SSD or host RAM and stream over PCIe on demand. 4-20X latency reduction, 8X cheaper vs DeepSpeed/Accelerate/llama.cpp-style offload.
leading_impls.refs: arXiv 2401.14361 §EAM + activation-aware prefetching; github TorchMoE/MoE-Infinity (entrypoint.py, memory tracer, prefetch priority queue); now folding into vLLM as a runtime
leading_impls.strengths: Strongest prefetch predictor: combines learned activation frequency (EAM) with layer-proximity, beating pure LRU and pure next-layer-gate. Explicitly designs the SSD tier (the disk-spill we need). Cross-layer/multi-step prefetch goes further than Mixtral-offloading's single-layer-ahead guess.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Built for cluster/batch serving and big RAM+SSD; tracing/EAM overhead and complexity higher than LRU. PyTorch-based. Not laptop-tuned; assumes more host RAM than our 12-16GB free.

leading_impls.engine: FlexGen (FlexLLMGen) + DeepSpeed ZeRO-Inference + HF accelerate device_map
leading_impls.approach: FlexGen: offline LP cost-model picks placement of weights/KV/activations across GPU/CPU/disk (the 6-number --percent: weight-gpu%, weight-cpu%, cache-gpu%, cache-cpu%, act-gpu%, act-cpu%; remainder→disk via --offload-dir), a zig-zag BLOCK schedule reuses each loaded weight across many batched sequences to amortize IO and overlap copy with compute, 4-bit group-wise quant for weights+KV, optional --pin-weight. ZeRO-Inference: streams whole dense layers from CPU/NVMe just-in-time, double-buffered prefetch via DeepNVMe (libaio/io_uring async reads), parameter coordinator overlaps next-layer fetch with current compute. accelerate device_map='auto' / 'balanced' + offload_folder: hooks per-module to move tensors GPU↔CPU↔disk, simplest but no overlap (slow).
leading_impls.refs: FMInference/FlexLLMGen flexllmgen/flex_opt.py (Policy, --percent L1271-1279), pytorch_backend.py (TorchDevice/TorchDisk/TorchMixedDevice), block_schedule; microsoft/DeepSpeed ZeRO-Inference (deepspeed/runtime/zero, DeepNVMe aio swapper, async_io); HF accelerate big_modeling.py (dispatch_model, disk_offload, offload_folder)
leading_impls.strengths: FlexGen formalizes the tiered VRAM↔RAM↔disk placement as an optimization and proves disk-spill throughput on a 16GB T4 + 200GB DRAM + 1.5TB SSD. The block schedule (amortize a weight load over a big effective batch) is the canonical IO-amortization trick. ZeRO-Inference's DeepNVMe async aio/io_uring double-buffered prefetch is the reference for a real disk tier — directly maps to our liburing.so.2/libaio + 2× fast NVMe. 4-bit group quant of weights+KV halves transfer bytes.
leading_impls.weaknesses: All three are throughput-oriented (huge batch) — FlexGen is explicitly slow at batch=1/low-latency, the opposite of interactive decode. accelerate device_map has no compute/copy overlap (very slow). None are MoE-expert-aware (dense-layer streaming). FlexGen is OPT-specific, dormant codebase. ZeRO-Inference is heavyweight.

leading_impls.approach: vLLM --cpu-offload-gb N: pins whole weights in CPU memory, streams them per-layer into GPU during the forward pass using a dedicated copy_stream overlapping compute, flattened pinned tensors re-materialized per block (virtual 'GPU+N GB'). vLLM v0.11.0 native OffloadingConnector: async KV-cache offload to CPU via CUDA DMA (~2X effective cache); LMCache extends KV offload to CPU+disk for prefix reuse. PowerInfer: profiles activation frequency to split HOT neurons (resident GPU) from COLD neurons (CPU-computed), with an online predictor forecasting active neurons per token — neuron-granularity hot/cold offload for dense ReLU/sparse models.
leading_impls.refs: vllm-project/vllm vllm/config/offload (cpu_offload_gb), worker weight-streaming with copy_stream + pinned mem; vLLM v0.11.0 OffloadingConnector (CUDA DMA); LMCache/LMCache (CPU+disk KV, docs.lmcache.ai); SJTU-IPADS/PowerInfer (solver.py hot/cold split, predictor MLP, ggml fork)
leading_impls.strengths: vLLM cpu-offload uses the exact pinned-CPU + dedicated-copy-stream + per-layer streaming pattern we should copy for the weight tier. OffloadingConnector + LMCache are the reference for a KV spill tier (VRAM→pinned RAM→NVMe) with async DMA. PowerInfer proves activation-frequency hot/cold partition + online predictor (the dense analog of hot-expert caching).
leading_impls.weaknesses: vLLM cpu-offload is widely reported mediocre/failing on consumer single-GPU (Reddit Oct 2025) and needs fast interconnect — our PCIe gen5 x8 laptop link is the limiter. vLLM has weak sm_120 wheel support historically (needs nightly/source). PowerInfer's neuron sparsity assumes ReLU-family activation locality (doesn't apply to modern SwiGLU MoE the same way); its predictor adds per-token overhead.

recommendation.design: Build a 3-tier spilling manager around the ktransformers/llama.cpp split, fused with mixtral-offloading's prefetch and MoE-Infinity's predictor, and a FlexGen/ZeRO-style disk tier. RESIDENCY POLICY (always on GPU, never spilled): attention/MLA Q/K/V/O weights, router/gate weights, dense-FFN and SHARED-expert weights, all LayerNorms, embeddings if they fit, and the active KV cache — these are touched every token so they earn VRAM. TIER 0 (VRAM, ~24GB budget): residents above (~quantized to 4-bit) + a per-layer LRU hot-expert cache (start k=2-4 experts/layer like mixtral-offloading, auto-size k to fill leftover VRAM after residents+KV+compute buffer) + b=4-8 async staging buffers for in-flight expert copies. TIER 1 (pinned host RAM, budget ~10-12GB of the 12-16GB free): the most-frequently-activated experts in ONE contiguous cudaHostAlloc'd pinned buffer for max H2D bandwidth — sized to the RAM budget, NOT the model; experts beyond it go to disk. TIER 2 (mmap'd NVMe): the cold-expert bulk and full-model GGUF, mmap'd from one of the 2× WD SN8000S NVMe drives; OS page cache acts as an opportunistic L1.5 between RAM and disk; an io_uring (liburing.so.2, present) reader does explicit async O_DIRECT prefetch for predicted-cold experts to bypass page-cache thrash under our tight RAM. TRANSFER ENGINE: one dedicated CUDA copy stream per direction + the staging-buffer ring; all host expert/weight memory is pinned; experts laid out contiguously per (layer,expert) so each fetch is one cudaMemcpyAsync. PREFETCH/PREDICTOR: (1) speculative next-layer prefetch exactly as mixtral-offloading — run layer L+1's router on layer L's post-attention hidden state, async-fetch its top-1/2 experts into staging while L computes; (2) layer over an EAM-style activation-frequency table (MoE-Infinity) to set TIER-0/TIER-1 residency and a prefetch priority = predicted_activation × layer_proximity; (3) on miss, the expert is still cheaper to COMPUTE on CPU than to fetch over our x8 link for small batches — so route confirmed-cold experts to a CPU GEMM kernel (kt-kernel llamafile/AVX512 path, arch-agnostic) instead of fetching, mirroring ktransformers. KV SPILL: keep active-window KV on GPU; quantize K/V (Q8_0 K / Q5_1 or Q4_0 V — mind llama.cpp's asymmetric-KV FA slow path, build with GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON); spill oldest KV blocks to pinned RAM then NVMe via an LMCache-style async DMA connector for long context. SCHEDULING: amortize transfers with a FlexGen-style block schedule during prefill (large -ub batch triggers GPU op-offload of CPU weights, threshold tuned like GGML_OP_OFFLOAD_MIN_BATCH to the x8 break-even ~several-hundred tokens); pipeline CPU-expert compute behind GPU work like kt-kernel's --kt-max-deferred-experts-per-token.
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: Transfer: cudaHostAlloc(cudaHostAllocPortable) for pinned buffers, cudaMemcpyAsync on dedicated cudaStream_t, cudaMemPrefetchAsync + cudaMallocManaged (HMM confirmed on this box) as a fallback unified path; CUDA 13.1 runtime + driver 595 — ALL arch-agnostic, fully available on sm_120. Disk async IO: liburing.so.2 / libaio.so.1t64 (both present) with O_DIRECT for the cold-expert reader (ZeRO DeepNVMe pattern). CPU expert GEMM: lift kt-kernel's LLAMAFILE backend (GGUF Q4_K/Q5_K, AVX512 — our Core Ultra 9 has AVX2/AVX512 but NO AMX, so build CPUINFER_CPU_INSTRUCT=AVX512 CPUINFER_ENABLE_AMX=OFF) OR ggml's native Q4_K/Q5_K CPU dot kernels. LRU+staging+speculative-gate: lift directly from dvmazur/mixtral-offloading src/expert_cache.py + expert_wrapper.py. Placement strategy/EAM: kt-kernel --kt-expert-placement-strategy frequency + --init-expert-location .pt stats, and MoE-Infinity EAM priority. KV offload connector: LMCache / vLLM v0.11.0 OffloadingConnector design.
recommendation.copy_from_sources: Tensor placement + the GPU=always-active / CPU=routed-experts split: llama.cpp -ot/--n-cpu-moe logic (PR #11397) and ktransformers optimize_rules YAML. Per-layer LRU hot-expert cache + contiguous pinned buffer + b=4 staging + speculative next-layer-gate prefetch: dvmazur/mixtral-offloading (expert_cache.py, expert_wrapper.py). Activation-frequency prefetch priority (predicted_activation × layer_proximity) + EAM: TorchMoE/MoE-Infinity. Async pipelined CPU-expert API + frequency/dynamic GPU-expert placement: kt-kernel KTMoEWrapper.submit_forward/sync_forward, --kt-num-gpu-experts, --kt-max-deferred-experts-per-token, --kt-enable-dynamic-expert-update. Disk tier (LP placement + block schedule + 4-bit weight/KV group quant): FlexGen flex_opt.py Policy/--percent + pytorch_backend TorchDisk; async NVMe double-buffered prefetch: DeepSpeed ZeRO-Inference DeepNVMe. KV spill: LMCache / vLLM OffloadingConnector. Weight per-layer streaming with dedicated copy_stream + pinned: vLLM cpu_offload_gb worker.
recommendation.rationale: On THIS box the binding constraints are (a) 24GB VRAM, (b) only ~12-16GB host RAM free, and (c) PCIe gen5 x8 (~31 GB/s peak, currently idling at gen1) — so neither RAM-only offload (ktransformers/FlexGen assume 100s of GB) nor pure PCIe streaming (vLLM cpu-offload, reported poor on consumer single-GPU) is sufficient alone. The 2× fast WD SN8000S NVMe drives make a DISK tier genuinely viable (each ~10-14 GB/s sequential, comparable to the x8 PCIe ceiling), which is why mmap+io_uring disk spill is mandatory not optional. The decisive insight from mixtral-offloading + MoE-Infinity is that MoE decode is bound by expert-fetch latency, not compute, and expert activation has strong temporal locality — so an LRU GPU hot-expert cache + speculative next-layer prefetch + activation-frequency placement turns most fetches into cache hits or pre-completed copies, and the rest are hidden behind compute. The ktransformers principle (compute a cold expert on CPU rather than fetch it for tiny batches) is exactly right for our x8 link at batch=1. Because the entire spill/transfer path is CUDA driver/runtime functionality (pinned mem, async copy, streams, HMM-backed managed memory), it is sm_120-clean today — the only sm_120 risk lives in the on-GPU GEMM/attention kernels that consume the spilled weights, which are other components' problem.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: The spill MECHANISM (cudaHostAlloc pinned memory, cudaMemcpyAsync, CUDA streams, cudaMallocManaged/cudaMemPrefetchAsync, io_uring/libaio disk IO, mmap+page cache) is architecture-independent CUDA driver/runtime + Linux kernel functionality and works on sm_120 with the installed CUDA 13.1 / driver 595 stack — verified present on this machine. The ONLY sm_120-specific risk is NOT in spilling itself but in (1) the GPU compute kernels that consume spilled weights (FP4/MXFP4/Marlin/CUTLASS GEMM, FlashAttention) — owned by other components — and (2) prebuilt offload libraries shipping CUDA fatbins WITHOUT sm_120: kt-kernel's PyPI wheels cover only SM 80/86/89/90, and vLLM/torch historically needed nightly/source for sm_120 — so any borrowed CUDA code must be RECOMPILED with -arch=sm_120 (we have nvcc 13.1 which supports it), and the CPU expert path (AMX/AVX512/llamafile) is arch-agnostic regardless. Secondary practical risk: this is a laptop with the GPU on a PCIe gen5 x8 link (width=8, not 16) — the spill bandwidth ceiling is ~31 GB/s, half a desktop x16, making prefetch/locality even more important.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: Local probes (2026-06-26): nvidia-smi reports compute_cap 12.0 (sm_120), CUDA 13.1 nvcc (release 13.1.115) + driver 595 / CUDA 13.2 runtime; Addressing Mode: HMM (managed/unified memory supported); pcie.link.gen.max=5, width.current=8 width.max=16 (laptop is x8); 2× WD PC SN8000S 2TB NVMe (ROTA=0), 1.6TB free; liburing.so.2 + libaio.so.1t64 present; single NUMA node (GPU NUMA 0, CPU 0-23). External: kt-kernel README (github.com/kvcache-ai/ktransformers/blob/main/kt-kernel/README.md) GPU compatibility matrix lists only SM 80/86/89/90, no 12.0 — confirms prebuilt CUDA path lacks sm_120, source build required. pytorch/pytorch issue #159207 (Jul 2025) + discuss.pytorch.org/t/216099 confirm sm_120 needs nightly/source historically. vLLM docs.vllm.ai/en/stable/api/vllm/config/offload confirm cpu-offload pinned-CPU + copy-stream streaming; vLLM v0.11.0 OffloadingConnector uses CUDA DMA. mixtral-offloading arXiv 2312.17238 §3.2 (pin_memory + b=4 async buffers) and MoE-Infinity arXiv 2401.14361 (EAM prefetch) confirm the algorithm; FlexGen + ZeRO-Inference DeepNVMe confirm the disk tier. None of these rely on any sm_120-gated tensor-core feature for the transfer path.

components.beats_competitors_how:
"vLLM, SGLang, and TensorRT-LLM treat offload as a coarse afterthought (vLLM cpu-offload-gb streams whole layers and is widely reported mediocre on a single consumer GPU; their MoE path assumes the model fits or uses datacenter-RAM hosts) — and historically lack first-class sm_120 wheels. llama.cpp is the strongest baseline here (free mmap disk-spill, GGUF, KV-quant, -ot regex placement) BUT its offload is fully STATIC: a CPU-resident expert is recomputed on CPU every single token with no GPU hot-expert cache, no LRU, no prefetch, no activation-frequency placement. ktransformers has the right architecture-aware split and a real hot-expert GPU tier + pipelining, but it is engineered for big-RAM Xeon-AMX servers (382GB-1TB DRAM) and its prebuilt CUDA lacks sm_120. NONE of the production engines combine: (1) llama.cpp-style zero-copy mmap NVMe disk tier, (2) a per-layer LRU GPU hot-expert cache, (3) speculative next-layer-gate prefetch (mixtral-offloading), (4) EAM activation-frequency placement + layer-proximity prefetch priority (MoE-Infinity), (5) compute-cold-expert-on-CPU-instead-of-fetch for batch=1 (ktransformers), and (6) async io_uring O_DIRECT cold reads sized to a 12-16GB RAM budget. We win on THIS laptop by fusing all six into one tiered manager tuned to the actual limits — VRAM 24GB, host RAM 12-16GB free (forcing the disk tier nobody else needs), and PCIe gen5 x8 (~31 GB/s, where locality+prefetch+CPU-compute-fallback matter most) — with every kernel recompiled -arch=sm_120 and the transfer path using only arch-agnostic CUDA APIs that are already confirmed working on this GPU."
}
open_questions: Actual sustained H2D PCIe bandwidth under load on this laptop: link idles at gen1 x8 — need a microbenchmark (pinned cudaMemcpyAsync, 256MB) to confirm it ramps to gen5 x8 (~31 GB/s) vs being capped lower by laptop thermal/power (~150W) limiting PCIe ASPM exit; this sets the prefetch break-even batch size.

Real decode hit-rate of speculative next-layer-gate prefetch on the target models (Qwen3-MoE, GLM-4.x, DeepSeek) — Mixtral's heuristic may have different accuracy; need to measure expert-prediction recall to size k and staging-buffer count b.

Where the fetch-vs-recompute crossover sits for one expert at batch=1 on this x8 link vs AVX512 CPU GEMM (Core Ultra 9, no AMX): below the crossover, route cold experts to CPU compute; above it, fetch. Must measure per quant type (Q4_K vs INT4).

Whether mmap page-cache thrash under our 12-16GB-free RAM causes stalls vs explicit io_uring O_DIRECT prefetch into pinned buffers — and whether to bypass page cache entirely for cold experts (likely yes given tight RAM).

Can both WD SN8000S NVMe drives be RAID0/striped for the disk tier to roughly double cold-read bandwidth and match/exceed the x8 PCIe ceiling, and does GPUDirect Storage (cuFile) work on consumer sm_120 to DMA disk→VRAM bypassing the host bounce buffer (likely NOT supported on GeForce — needs verification).

KV-cache quant interaction: asymmetric K/V quant triggers llama.cpp's 18x-slow FA path unless GGML_CUDA_FA_ALL_QUANTS=ON — confirm our from-scratch FA kernel supports asymmetric Q8_0-K/Q4_0-V on sm_120 before relying on aggressive KV spill-quant.

kt-kernel CPU kernels assume the CPU-expert set fits in RAM; our design adds a disk tier under it — need to confirm the LLAMAFILE/GGUF backend can stream experts from an mmap'd region rather than requiring them resident, or wrap it with our own paging layer.

_verdict.component: Spilling / offload (weights, experts, KV) — tiered VRAM ↔ pinned host RAM ↔ mmap'd NVMe on RTX 5090 Laptop
_verdict.riskiest_claim: The "beats competitors" claim and its supporting rationale rest on two factual pillars: (A) "The 2x fast WD SN8000S NVMe drives make a DISK tier genuinely viable (each ~10-14 GB/s sequential, comparable to the x8 PCIe ceiling)" — and the open-question that RAID0 of both could "match/exceed the x8 PCIe ceiling"; and (B) the headline novelty claim that "NONE of the production engines combine (1) mmap NVMe disk tier, (2) per-layer LRU GPU hot-expert cache, (3) speculative prefetch, (4) frequency placement, (5) compute-cold-on-CPU, (6) async io_uring reads... we win by fusing all six," plus the mechanism premise that "MoE decode is bound by expert-fetch latency, not compute" so prefetch is "the single biggest decode-latency lever." (The sm_120 mechanism claim — pinned mem/async copy/streams/HMM/io_uring/mmap are arch-agnostic on CUDA 13.1/driver 595 — is correct and not the risk.)
_verdict.evidence: PILLAR A — REFUTED with primary-source + local-hardware proof. SanDisk/Western Digital's own product page and datasheet for the PC SN8000S state it is PCIe Gen4 x4, "up to 7,300 MB/s (1024GB-4096GB models) sequential read" (7,000 MB/s on the 512GB) — i.e. ~7 GB/s, NOT 10-14 GB/s (sandisk.com/products/ssd/internal-ssd/pc-sn8000s-nvme-internal-ssd; documents.sandisk.com data-sheet-pc-sn8000s-nvme-ssd.pdf). Local probe confirms THIS machine's drives are exactly these: lsblk shows 2x "WD PC SN8000S SDEQNRG-2T00-1002" (2TB); /sys/class/nvme/nvme{0,1}/device report current AND max link = "16.0 GT/s PCIe" (Gen4) width 4 — so by hardware capability each drive cannot exceed ~7.5 GB/s in any slot. The recommendation overstated per-drive bandwidth by ~2x; even RAID0 of both ≈ 14 GB/s, well below the x8 PCIe ceiling (GamersNexus: Gen5 x8 = 31.5 GB/s), so "comparable to the x8 PCIe ceiling" and the RAID0-"match/exceed" open-question are both unachievable. PILLAR B — REFUTED as "novel fusion nobody has." llama.cpp issue #20757 (filed 2026-03-19) is an active RFC with a working Python PoC (tinyserve) for GPT-OSS-120B implementing precisely the proposed design: Tier 1 persistent GPU slot buffer (LRU/SLRU hot-expert cache, frequency-gated admission), Tier 2 pinned-RAM backing store, Tier 3 SSD/mmap demand-paging, POSIX_MADV_WILLNEED readahead prefetch, and double-buffered PCIe overlap, with a pluggable eviction-policy interface — i.e. items (1)(2)(3)(4)(6) of the "six nobody combines" list, inside llama.cpp itself. So the win over llama.cpp is time-limited, not a structural gap. MECHANISM PREMISE also undercut by the same PoC's measurements: with a warm hot-expert cache, steady-state hit rate is ~98-100% and "the model is GPU-compute bound — the PCIe pipe is mostly idle" (12-14 tok/s vs 0.5-1 tok/s pure-CPU). That means the resident cache (which fits far more easily in our 24GB than the PoC's 8GB) is the lever; the elaborate io_uring/O_DIRECT/double-copy-stream prefetch engine — sold as the differentiator — mostly matters at cold start, not steady-state decode. PILLAR C — UNCERTAIN/undercut: the "compute a cold expert on CPU instead of fetching it" design point is framed as a win, but the PoC shows CPU-compute is the SLOW fallback (0.5-1 tok/s) vs GPU-resident (12-14 tok/s); on a 24GB GPU you want experts resident, not CPU-computed (batch=1/x8-specific, so not fully refuted but the framing is wrong).
_verdict.correction: 1) Fix the disk-tier bandwidth budget: treat each SN8000S as ~7 GB/s (Gen4 x4, hardware-capped), RAID0 ~14 GB/s aggregate — roughly half the ~31.5 GB/s x8 PCIe ceiling, NOT comparable to it. The disk tier is still useful as a cold-start/eviction backing store but must be budgeted as the SLOWEST tier (~7 GB/s/drive), and any plan that assumed disk could feed experts at PCIe-link speed needs the prefetch break-even and cache-size math redone. Drop the RAID0-"match/exceed-PCIe" assumption. 2) Reframe the primary lever: per the llama.cpp #20757 PoC, on a 24GB GPU the dominant win is a large PERSISTENT GPU hot-expert cache with SLRU + frequency-gated admission (prefill-burst-resistant) sized to fill leftover VRAM — not the speculative-prefetch/disk machinery. Build the resident SLRU slot buffer first; treat io_uring/O_DIRECT/double-stream prefetch as a second-order cold-start optimization, not the headline. 3) Drop or soften the "nobody combines these six" beats-llama.cpp claim: the same two-tier GPU+RAM+SSD expert cache is an in-flight llama.cpp RFC with a working PoC; if it lands upstream the structural advantage evaporates. The defensible edge is sm_120-recompiled GEMM/attention kernels + tuning to THIS box, plus larger resident cache headroom (24GB vs 8GB) — not a unique offload architecture. 4) Re-validate the "compute-cold-on-CPU vs fetch" crossover empirically (it's already an open question) — bias toward keeping experts GPU-resident given 24GB; CPU-compute is a fallback, not a default. 5) Keep the sm_120 mechanism claim as-is (correct) and keep the honest kt-kernel-needs-source-build caveat.

components.component: Scheduler: continuous (iteration-level) batching, chunked/piggyback prefill, preemption, and speculative decoding for a single-GPU (RTX 5090 Laptop, sm_120, 24GB, 150W) latency-and-throughput LLM engine.

leading_impls.approach: Continuous batching with a single per-step token budget. schedule() in vllm/v1/core/sched/scheduler.py is RUNNING-FIRST: it walks self.running (a list, FCFS or priority via create_request_queue/SchedulingPolicy), computes num_new_tokens per request, clamps by chunked-prefill threshold and budget (num_new_tokens = min(num_new_tokens, token_budget); if 0 < long_prefill_token_threshold < num_new_tokens: clamp), and calls kv_cache_manager.allocate_slots(request, num_new_tokens, num_lookahead_tokens=self.num_lookahead_tokens). It UNIFIES prefill and decode: a step is just a dict {req_id: num_tokens}, so chunked prefill and decode piggyback in one batch with no separate phases. Decode = 1 token (+ num_lookahead_tokens for spec). When KV blocks run out it does RECOMPUTE preemption only (self.running.pop() tail -> _preempt_request -> waiting.prepend; swap-to-CPU was removed from V1). Prefix caching is always-on, hash-based (sha256 default, xxhash optional), O(1) LRU via doubly-linked free_block_queue in a pre-allocated KVCacheBlock pool. EngineCore runs in its own process; AsyncLLM overlaps tokenize/detokenize/HTTP via ZeroMQ. async_scheduling flag (vllm/v1/core/sched/async_scheduler.py) runs the scheduler one step ahead to hide CPU gaps; MRV2 (VLLM_USE_V2_MODEL_RUNNER=1) adds GPU-native input prep. Spec decode wired via SpeculativeConfig: ngram, ngram_gpu, suffix, eagle/eagle3, mtp, draft_model, PARD, MLP, dflash; num_lookahead_tokens reserves KV slots; DraftTokenIds/update_draft_token_ids feed next step; rejection sampler in vllm/v1/spec_decode/.
leading_impls.refs: vllm/v1/core/sched/scheduler.py (schedule, _preempt_request, token_budget, long_prefill_token_threshold, num_lookahead_tokens); vllm/v1/core/sched/request_queue.py (SchedulingPolicy, create_request_queue, FCFS/priority); vllm/v1/core/sched/output.py (SchedulerOutput, NewRequestData, CachedRequestData); vllm/v1/core/kv_cache_manager.py + kv_cache_coordinator.py (allocate_slots, get_computed_blocks); vllm/v1/core/sched/async_scheduler.py; vllm/v1/spec_decode/ (eagle, ngram, metrics, rejection sampler); docs/design/prefix_caching; docs/features/speculative_decoding; aleksagordic.com/blog/vllm (canonical walkthrough at commit 42172ad).
leading_impls.strengths: Cleanest, most hackable scheduler design; single token-budget abstraction makes chunked prefill + spec decode fall out naturally. Near-zero-overhead prefix caching on by default. Broadest spec-decode menu incl. ngram_gpu/suffix that need no draft model. EngineCore process isolation + async scheduling directly attacks the CPU-overhead-dominates-small-GPU-step problem that is acute on a fast single GPU.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Python scheduler has real per-step CPU cost on tiny models/short steps; async scheduling helps but adds complexity and a one-step output-latency lag. Recompute-only preemption (no CPU swap in V1) means thrashing under memory pressure recomputes prefills - bad on a 24GB box that will spill. Heavy framework (torch.compile, multiprocess) to copy wholesale.

leading_impls.approach: PREFILL-PRIORITIZED continuous batching with a zero-overhead overlap scheduler. python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py: get_next_batch_to_run() merges the prior prefill batch into running_batch, then get_new_batch_prefill()/_get_new_batch_prefill_raw() uses a PrefillAdder to pack waiting requests under chunked_prefill_size, tracking a SINGLE in-flight partial request self.chunked_req (one chunked prefill at a time). Decode batches run when no prefill is pending. Memory pressure handled by new_token_ratio-driven RETRACTION (retract_decode) instead of naive preempt. event_loop_overlap() is the headline: it keeps a result_queue deque, launches run_batch(batch) for the CURRENT batch, then pop_and_process() the PREVIOUS batch's result - i.e. CPU scheduling of step N+1 overlaps GPU compute of step N. Future/placeholder tokens resolve cross-step dependencies so the GPU never idles (verified zero idle in Nsight). RadixAttention prefix cache = a radix tree (tree_cache) giving automatic, fine-grained prefix reuse (more general than block-hash); hierarchical/HiCache tiers to CPU. Spec decode: EAGLE2/EAGLE3, MTP, DFLASH, NGRAM, draft-model via --speculative-algorithm with --speculative-num-steps/--speculative-num-draft-tokens/--speculative-eagle-topk; SpecForge trains EAGLE3 heads.
leading_impls.refs: python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py (event_loop_overlap, event_loop_normal, get_next_batch_to_run, get_new_batch_prefill/_get_new_batch_prefill_raw, run_batch, chunked_req, new_token_ratio, retract_decode); python/sglang/srt/mem_cache/radix_cache.py (RadixAttention tree); PrefillAdder; sgl-project/SpecForge (EAGLE3 training); lmsys.org/blog/2024-12-04-sglang-v0-4 (zero-overhead scheduler + cache-aware router, references NanoFlow arXiv:2408.12757).
leading_impls.strengths: The overlap-one-batch-ahead loop is the best-documented, most copyable recipe for hiding CPU scheduling behind GPU compute - exactly the win you need on a fast single GPU where steps are short. RadixAttention prefix reuse is more flexible than fixed-block hashing for shared-prefix/multi-turn/agent workloads. Retraction (new_token_ratio) degrades more gracefully than recompute-preempt. Strong, battle-tested EAGLE3/MTP spec path with B=1 numbers.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Prefill-priority can starve decode latency under bursty prefill (TTFT-vs-ITL tradeoff vs vLLM's running-first). Radix tree has more CPU bookkeeping per step than block-hash; the future-token/CUDA-event dependency resolution is intricate to reimplement correctly. Single in-flight chunked_req limits prefill packing flexibility vs vLLM's multi-request chunking.

leading_impls.approach: C++ in-flight (a.k.a. inflight fused / iteration-level) batching via the Executor class (cpp/include/tensorrt_llm/executor/executor.h): client enqueueRequest()/enqueueRequests(), awaitResponses() pulls streaming Results. Each iteration batches independent requests, fusing context (prefill) and generation (decode) phases. SchedulerConfig selects CapacitySchedulerPolicy: MAX_UTILIZATION (pack aggressively, may evict/recompute), GUARANTEED_NO_EVICT (only admit if it will never be preempted - low-jitter latency), STATIC_BATCH. ContextChunkingPolicy controls chunked context (=chunked prefill). KvCacheConfig enables paged KV + block reuse (prefix caching) with tokensPerBlock default 64. GuidedDecodingConfig (XGrammar) for structured output; LogitsPostProcessor(Batched) hooks.
leading_impls.refs: cpp/include/tensorrt_llm/executor/executor.h (Executor, Request, Response, Result, ExecutorConfig); SchedulerConfig + CapacitySchedulerPolicy {MAX_UTILIZATION, GUARANTEED_NO_EVICT, STATIC_BATCH}; ContextChunkingPolicy; KvCacheConfig; nvidia.github.io/TensorRT-LLM/advanced/executor.html and /gpt-runtime.html; tensorrt_llm.bindings.executor (Python).
leading_impls.strengths: Fully C++/native - no Python per-step overhead, the ceiling for scheduler latency. Explicit capacity-policy knob (GUARANTEED_NO_EVICT) is the cleanest way to get jitter-free single-stream latency. Mature chunked-context + KV reuse.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Requires AOT engine build per model/shape; far less flexible than copying a runtime scheduler. Tightly coupled to TRT engine + plugins. Heaviest to lift; design ideas (policy enums, fused context+gen, awaitResponses) are the takeaway, not the code.

leading_impls.approach: Slot-based continuous batching. --cont-batching with N --parallel slots; each slot holds one sequence's KV. A server scheduler assembles a llama_batch each step mixing prompt-processing (prefill, chunked by --batch-size/-ub n_ubatch) and single-token decode tokens across active slots, then one llama_decode() call. Prompt/prefix reuse via slot KV cache + prompt caching; preemption is coarse (slot eviction). Spec decode: built-in draft-model speculative (speculative example / server --model-draft, --draft-max/--draft-min) and lookup/ngram (lookup example).
leading_impls.refs: tools/server/server.cpp (slot scheduler, n_parallel, cont_batching, n_ubatch chunking); common/speculative.* (draft-model spec); examples/lookup (ngram/prompt-lookup); llama_batch / llama_decode API.
leading_impls.strengths: Tiny C++ footprint, GGUF-native, runs in 24GB easily; the simplest correct continuous-batching reference and trivial to read. Good ngram/draft-model spec for B=1. Already the most relevant baseline to BEAT on this exact GGUF + single-GPU config.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Coarse slot model (fixed n_parallel, no fine-grained token budget); chunking is batch-size driven not a true token-budget chunked prefill; no radix/hash prefix tree (only per-slot reuse); CPU-side scheduling not overlapped with GPU as aggressively as SGLang. Lowest throughput ceiling under high concurrency.

recommendation.design: Build a single-process, CPU-light continuous-batching scheduler with ONE per-step token budget, copying vLLM V1's running-first loop + SGLang's overlap-one-batch-ahead execution. Concretely: (1) One step = a dict {req_id: num_tokens} (vLLM's unified abstraction) so decode and chunked prefill piggyback in the same batch; no prefill/decode phase split. (2) Running-first scheduling (schedule running decodes first, then admit waiting prefills into the leftover budget) for low ITL/jitter on single-stream - this beats SGLang's prefill-priority for interactive latency; expose a chunked_prefill_token_threshold (vLLM long_prefill_token_threshold; start ~512-2048) and a max_num_batched_tokens budget. (3) Implement the SGLang event_loop_overlap pattern: a result_queue deque, launch GPU work for step N+1 while processing step N's sampled tokens on CPU, using placeholder/future tokens for cross-step deps - this is the single biggest latency lever on a fast GPU where a decode step is only a few ms and Python/CPU scheduling would otherwise be a visible gap. (4) Prefix cache: start with vLLM's block-hash + pre-allocated KVCacheBlock pool + O(1) doubly-linked LRU free queue (simplest, near-zero overhead, on by default); add a RadixAttention-style tree later only if agent/multi-turn shared-prefix workloads dominate. (5) Preemption: because this box is 24GB and MUST spill, do NOT copy vLLM V1's recompute-only policy - instead implement SGLang-style new_token_ratio retraction PLUS swap/offload preemption to host RAM/disk that cooperates with the engine's weight/expert/KV spilling (TRT-LLM's GUARANTEED_NO_EVICT policy as the low-jitter default for single-stream, MAX_UTILIZATION mode for batch throughput). (6) Speculative decoding: build the scheduler spec-decode-AWARE from day one (reserve num_lookahead_tokens in allocate_slots, carry a per-request draft_token_ids list, generic propose()/verify() interface a la vLLM SpeculativeConfig + custom_class proposer), but ship ONLY ngram/suffix proposers first (CPU-side, zero extra GPU kernels, zero VRAM, free win on repetitive/code/agent output). Add EAGLE3 second once the attention/tree-verify kernels are proven on sm_120; gate MoE spec behind a measured break-even check.
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: Scheduler core is CPU-only - no CUDA kernels, so sm_120 is N/A for the scheduler itself. GPU touchpoints to reuse: rejection/acceptance sampler and tree-verify for spec decode (port vllm/v1/spec_decode rejection sampler; it is elementwise/gather, trivially sm_120-safe, compile with CUDA 13.1 nvcc -arch=sm_120). Draft forward for EAGLE3 reuses the SAME attention + GEMM path as the target model (FlashInfer / FlashAttention / CUTLASS sm_120 kernels owned by the attention-kernel and GEMM agents) - the scheduler only schedules it. ngram/suffix proposers = pure CPU (suffix automaton / prompt-lookup hash), no GPU. xgrammar (vLLM/SGLang/TRT-LLM all use it) for structured-output masks, also CPU + a logits-mask kernel. Use CUDA graphs for the decode step (piecewise like vLLM) to cut launch overhead - graph capture is arch-agnostic and works on sm_120.
recommendation.copy_from_sources: Loop skeleton + running-first token budget + chunked prefill clamp + allocate_slots(num_lookahead_tokens): vllm/v1/core/sched/scheduler.py and request_queue.py. Overlap execution (result_queue, run-ahead, future tokens): sglang python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py event_loop_overlap. Prefix cache block pool + O(1) LRU: vllm/v1/core/kv_cache_manager.py + docs/design/prefix_caching. Retraction policy: sglang new_token_ratio/retract_decode. Capacity-policy enum design (GUARANTEED_NO_EVICT vs MAX_UTILIZATION) + fused context+gen iteration: TRT-LLM Executor/SchedulerConfig. ngram/suffix + draft-model spec config surface: vllm docs/features/speculative_decoding (ngram, suffix keys) and llama.cpp common/speculative.*. EAGLE3 head + recipe: SafeAILab/EAGLE + sgl-project/SpecForge (277MB head, trains in <2h on one GPU).
recommendation.rationale: On a single 5090 laptop the workload is latency-dominated single-stream plus modest concurrency, and a decode step is only a few ms, so the dominant non-model cost is CPU scheduling overhead, not GPU FLOPs. The two proven levers are (a) overlap CPU scheduling with GPU compute (SGLang zero-overhead loop; NanoFlow arXiv:2408.12757; an unoptimized engine wastes ~half its time on CPU per the SGLang/wuklab data) and (b) speculative decoding, which converts idle tensor cores into accepted tokens precisely because B=1 decode is memory-bandwidth bound and the GPU is <1% utilized (HF EAGLE3 writeup). The scheduler is CPU-side so it sidesteps the sm_120 kernel risk entirely - the risky part (attention, GEMM, FP4 MMA) lives in kernels other components own. Shipping ngram/suffix spec first gives a free, zero-VRAM latency win on this VRAM-starved box; EAGLE3's measured 1.39x at B=1 (even on a 31B/3B-active MoE) is the headline single-stream gain, but its MoE break-even and the need for sm_120-validated draft/verify kernels argue for staging it second. Swap/offload preemption (not vLLM's recompute-only) is mandatory because 24GB forces KV/weight/expert spilling - the scheduler must cooperate with that spill rather than recompute prefills.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: The scheduler/batching engine itself runs entirely on the CPU (request queues, token-budget loop, KV-block bookkeeping, prefix-cache hashing, overlap orchestration) so it has NO sm_120 dependency and carries essentially zero Blackwell-consumer kernel risk. The ONLY sm_120-sensitive parts are the GPU kernels that speculative decoding drives: (a) the draft/EAGLE3 forward pass, which reuses the target model's attention+GEMM path (sm_120 support for those is owned by the attention-kernel and GEMM agents - FlashInfer/FlashAttention/CUTLASS sm_120 is the real open question there, not here), and (b) the rejection/acceptance sampler and tree-verification kernels, which are elementwise gather/scatter + softmax and compile cleanly for -arch=sm_120 under CUDA 13.1. Risk is therefore deferred to whether you enable model-based spec decode (EAGLE3) and whether its attention/tree-verify kernels have sm_120 builds; ngram/suffix spec is pure CPU and 100% safe.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: Verified locally: nvidia-smi reports NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 Laptop GPU, compute_cap 12.0 (=sm_120), 24463 MiB; nvcc release 13.1 present (also 12.8) - nvcc 13.1 targets sm_120. vLLM V1 scheduler source (vllm/v1/core/sched/scheduler.py, raw GitHub main) is plain Python/CPU. SGLang event_loop_overlap (python/sglang/srt/managers/scheduler.py) is Python/CPU + torch CUDA streams/events (stream/event API is arch-agnostic). Spec-decode B=1 efficacy on consumer-class single GPU is demonstrated: HF EAGLE3 benchmark (huggingface.co/blog/lujangusface/tw-eagle3-gpu) shows 1.39x TPOT at batch 1 on GLM-4.7-Flash MoE with 2.4 accepted tokens/step at k=6 (single H100, SGLang v0.5.6, FlashInfer) - the same software stack people already run on RTX 50-series. vLLM async scheduling is a documented config flag (docs.vllm.ai .../config/scheduler) that 'helps avoid gaps in GPU utilization, leading to better latency and throughput'; MRV2 (v0.20+) adds GPU-native async scheduling. CUDA graphs (vLLM piecewise) are arch-agnostic and capture fine on sm_120.

components.beats_competitors_how: "vs llama.cpp (the primary baseline to beat on this GGUF/24GB/single-GPU config): llama.cpp uses a coarse fixed-slot scheduler (n_parallel) with batch-size-driven prompt chunking and no true token-budget chunked prefill, no hash/radix prefix tree (only per-slot reuse), and does not overlap CPU scheduling behind GPU compute. We beat it on (1) ITL via the SGLang-style overlap loop that removes the CPU scheduling gap llama.cpp leaves between decode steps, (2) TTFT-under-load via real token-budget chunked prefill that piggybacks prefill chunks onto decode batches instead of stalling a slot, (3) cross-request reuse via a block-hash/radix prefix cache (llama.cpp lacks this for multi-turn/agent prompts), and (4) optional ngram/suffix spec decode for repetitive/code output at zero extra VRAM. vs vLLM/SGLang: we keep their best ideas (vLLM unified token-budget + always-on prefix cache; SGLang overlap loop + retraction) but drop their multi-GPU/distributed/PD-disaggregation machinery, and we replace vLLM's recompute-only V1 preemption with swap/offload preemption that cooperates with this engine's mandatory weight/expert/KV spilling on 24GB - so under memory pressure we offload-and-resume instead of recomputing prefills (a large single-stream latency saver vLLM V1 forfeits). vs TensorRT-LLM: we match its native-C++/Rust low-overhead ceiling (no Python per-step cost) and adopt its GUARANTEED_NO_EVICT capacity policy as the low-jitter single-stream default, while staying runtime-flexible (no per-shape AOT engine build). Net: a CPU-overhead-minimized, spill-aware, spec-decode-aware scheduler tuned for one fast GPU + tight VRAM - the exact regime where the big engines carry distributed overhead we don't pay."
open_questions: What is the actual per-step CPU scheduling budget on the 24-core 275HX for our chosen models? Need to profile whether a Rust/C++ scheduler is required from day one or whether a tight Python loop + overlap is enough at the step rates our models hit (measure decode step ms for the target MoE on sm_120).

Does EAGLE3's draft forward + tree-verification have working sm_120 kernels in FlashInfer/vLLM/SGLang today, or only sm_90/sm_100? This gates whether model-based spec ships in v1 - must be answered jointly with the attention-kernel agent. ngram/suffix sidestep it entirely.

For our target MoE on 24GB with expert spilling, where is the spec-decode break-even acceptance rate? The HF writeup warns MoE verification of k draft tokens inflates expert-dispatch/routing cost; need a measured break-even alpha before enabling EAGLE3 with MoE (and whether to cap k or use dynamic spec).

Block-hash prefix cache vs RadixAttention tree: which wins for our expected workload (interactive chat vs agent/multi-turn shared prefixes)? Tree gives finer reuse but more CPU per step - decide empirically once workload mix is known.

Swap/offload preemption design: when KV must spill to the ~12-16GB free host RAM (or disk/mmap), what is the resume latency, and does it beat vLLM-style recompute for our prompt lengths? Need to co-design with the KV-spilling component so the scheduler's preemption choice (retract vs swap vs recompute) is cost-model driven.

Async/run-ahead scheduling adds a one-step output-latency lag and dependency-resolution complexity (future tokens, CUDA events) - quantify whether the GPU-gap it hides on our short decode steps is worth that complexity vs a simpler synchronous loop with CUDA graphs.

Interaction of chunked-prefill threshold with spec-decode lookahead and CUDA-graph batch-size bucketing: need to pick token-budget, chunk size, and graph capture shapes jointly so spec lookahead tokens don't blow past captured graph dims.

_verdict.component: Scheduler: continuous batching, chunked/piggyback prefill, preemption, and speculative decoding for single-GPU (RTX 5090 Laptop, sm_120, 24GB, 150W).
_verdict.riskiest_claim: That the scheduler should REPLACE vLLM V1's recompute-only preemption with swap/offload-to-host-RAM/disk preemption, framed as 'a large single-stream latency saver vLLM V1 forfeits' and 'mandatory because this box is 24GB and MUST spill.' This is the load-bearing differentiator in the 'beats vLLM/SGLang' story (sm_120 risk is correctly N/A since the scheduler is CPU-side).
_verdict.evidence: Primary sources contradict the framing and the cost model. (1) vLLM's own metrics design doc (docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/design/metrics, KV Cache Offloading section): the --swap-space flag 'has been removed as this feature is no longer used in V1'; prefix caching 'proved to be a BETTER option than CPU swapping since blocks can be evicted slowly on demand and the part of the prompt that was evicted can be recomputed'; 'with prefix caching being better (zero overhead) and on by default, the preemption and recompute strategy should work better.' (2) vLLM optimization doc (configuration/optimization): 'In vLLM V1, the default preemption mode is RECOMPUTE rather than SWAP, AS RECOMPUTATION HAS LOWER OVERHEAD in the V1 architecture.' So recompute-only is a DELIBERATE engineering win, not value 'forfeited.' (3) Cost model (backend.ai, 2026-04, 'KV cache offloading'): the technique 'only works if external KV cache can be loaded faster than the GPU can recompute it.' Its scenario table marks DISADVANTAGE for: general chatbot/one-shot prompts (no prefix reuse), single-long-shared-prefix (prefix stays GPU-resident, never evicted), and any non-RDMA transport ('transfer latency exceeds recompute latency'). Offload only wins for 70B+ long-context (10K+ tokens) multi-turn or multi-team multi-prefix RAG over 100-400Gbps RDMA fabrics (the published 3-10x and 11s->1.5s wins all required BlueField-3 + GPUDirect + RDMA). (4) This box's hardware kills the swap case: verified via nvidia-smi the 5090 Laptop is PCIe Gen5 x8 MAX (pcie.link.width.max=8), ~25-28 GB/s practical H2D/D2H, NOT x16 and nowhere near RDMA fabric speeds; only ~12-16GB host RAM free (tiny swap budget -> large spills hit disk/mmap = 'seconds' per the article); and that same PCIe path is already saturated by the engine's mandatory weight/expert spilling, so swap CONTENDS with the spill traffic it claims to 'cooperate' with. The target workload (per the rec's own rationale: latency-dominated single-stream + modest concurrency on a small/medium quantized MoE that must fit 24GB) is the small-model/short-context regime where backend.ai and arXiv:2410.03065 both say recompute wins. The rec also enables vLLM's always-on prefix cache in point (4) -- which is exactly what makes recompute cheap -- so adding swap duplicates that work while paying PCIe+hashing+movement overhead the article warns 'only introduces overhead, which slows the system down.'
_verdict.correction: Flip the default: ship vLLM V1's RECOMPUTE-FIRST preemption (cooperating with the always-on prefix cache so evicted prefix blocks are partially reused on resume, not fully recomputed) as the low-jitter single-stream default -- this is what vLLM measured to win and matches TRT-LLM's GUARANTEED_NO_EVICT spirit (avoid eviction entirely when capacity allows). Keep SGLang-style new_token_ratio retraction for graceful decode degradation. DEMOTE swap/offload preemption from 'mandatory default' to an OPTIONAL, cost-model-gated path enabled only when a measured break-even holds: load_time(KV over PCIe Gen5 x8) < recompute_time(prefill on 5090). That break-even is met only for long contexts (roughly >8-16K tokens) on a large-enough model with a healthy block-hash prefix-cache HIT on the spilled blocks; for short/one-shot/single-shared-prefix interactive chat (the stated primary workload) recompute is faster and offload is net-negative. IMPORTANT distinction the rec conflates: WEIGHT/EXPERT spilling (a residency problem -- experts genuinely don't fit 24GB, so DMA-streaming them is unavoidable) is NOT the same as KV preemption policy (a scheduling choice where recompute usually beats swap on this PCIe-bound box). Co-design the preemption choice (recompute vs retract vs swap) as a runtime cost model with the KV-spilling component, defaulting to recompute, and profile the actual prefill tok/s on sm_120 before ever enabling KV swap. Everything else in the rec (unified token-budget loop, SGLang overlap-one-batch-ahead, block-hash prefix cache, ngram/suffix-first spec, sm_120-N/A for the CPU scheduler) is sound and unaffected.

components.component: GGUF format parser + model loader (mmap, metadata→config, streamed dequant upload to GPU, partial-offload/spill integration; safetensors as secondary)

leading_impls.approach: Single binary GGUF reader in ggml/src/gguf.cpp (struct gguf_reader, gguf_init_from_reader). Read order: magic 'GGUF' (4B) -> version u32 (==3) -> tensor_count i64 -> kv_count i64 -> KV pairs (key=u64-len string + value_type u32 + value; ARRAY = elem-type u32 + u64 len + elems) -> per-tensor info (name u64-len string <=64B, n_dims u32 <=4, ne[] i64, ggml_type u32, offset u64 relative to data section). Strings are u64-len + non-null-terminated UTF-8; bool stored as int8; enums as int32. After tensor_infos, file is padded to general.alignment (default 32) via GGML_PAD(offset,align); data section begins there. Loader (src/llama-model-loader.cpp) mmaps the file (MAP_SHARED|MAP_POPULATE + posix_fadvise SEQUENTIAL + posix_madvise WILLNEED in src/llama-mmap.cpp) and either (a) mmap path: points cur->data into the mmap for host tensors (zero-copy via ggml_backend_tensor_alloc) or ggml_backend_tensor_set for GPU tensors; or (b) no-mmap path: 4 round-robin pinned host staging buffers (64MB w/ O_DIRECT, else 1MB) double-buffered with ggml_backend_event_synchronize + ggml_backend_tensor_set_async to overlap NVMe read with H2D upload. Partial offload: each weight carries idx + offs; model build assigns each layer's tensors to a CPU or CUDA ggml_backend_buffer_t (bufs map keyed by tensor) per n_gpu_layers, and load_all_data routes per-tensor. Dequant is NOT done at load; quantized blocks are uploaded verbatim and dequantized in-kernel (MMQ / dequant_mul_mat).
leading_impls.refs: ggml/src/gguf.cpp (gguf_reader, gguf_init_from_reader, GGML_PAD alignment, ctx->offset/ctx->size); ggml/include/gguf.h (GGUF_MAGIC, GGUF_VERSION 3, GGUF_DEFAULT_ALIGNMENT 32, gguf_get_data_offset/gguf_get_tensor_offset API); src/llama-mmap.cpp (llama_file w/ O_DIRECT read_aligned_chunk, llama_mmap impl, llama_mlock); src/llama-model-loader.cpp (load_all_data async-upload pipeline, load_data_for, get_mapping_range, weights_map); ggml/src/ggml-common.h (block_q4_K/q5_K/q6_K/q8_K, block_mxfp4{u8 e; u8 qs[16]} QK_MXFP4=32, block_nvfp4{u8 d[4]; u8 qs[32]} QK_NVFP4=64, QK_K=256); docs/gguf.md (spec). ggml_type enum: F32=0,F16=1,Q4_0=2..Q8_0=8,Q2_K=10..Q6_K=14,Q8_K=15,IQ*=16-23/29,BF16=30,TQ1_0=34,TQ2_0=35,MXFP4=39.
leading_impls.strengths: Canonical format owner; cleanest, dependency-free, mmap-first parser (easy to copy 1:1). Handles every quant type, MoE (ffn_*_exp[s] split-expert tensors, expert_count/expert_used_count), GGUF metadata->arch mapping, embedded tokenizer (tokenizer.ggml.*), sharded files (00001-of-NNNNN), big-endian. Robust bounds/overflow checks (GGUF_MAX_STRING_LENGTH, duplicate-name, ne overflow). Async pinned-buffer upload already overlaps IO+H2D. Partial CPU/GPU offload is per-tensor and battle-tested. CONFIRMED working on sm_120 with CUDA 12.8 + MMQ (pp 5611-6566, tg 201-211 t/s on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B MoE).
leading_impls.weaknesses: mmap path uploads to GPU synchronously (ggml_backend_tensor_set) without the pinned double-buffer, so for pure-GPU loads from cold cache the no-mmap async path is faster. block_mxfp4 has only 1-byte alignment (e + 16B qs = 17B) which is a known CUDA-perf drawback (discussion #18427) -- misaligned global loads. No GPU Direct Storage (file->pinned->GPU bounce through host). GGUF metadata->hparams mapping is hand-written per architecture in llama-model.cpp (large switch); must be reimplemented per arch.

leading_impls.approach: safetensors layout: [8B u64 LE header_size][UTF-8 JSON header][contiguous raw tensor data]. JSON maps tensor_name -> {dtype (F32/F16/BF16/I32/...), shape[], data_offsets:[start,end] relative to data region}. Absolute pos = 8 + header_size + data_offsets[0]; header capped at 100MB. mmap-friendly (offsets index directly into contiguous data). vLLM loads via its model_loader (DefaultModelLoader) reading safetensors + config.json/HF AutoConfig for hparams + tokenizer.json. For GGUF, vLLM has a GGUF adapter that remaps GGUF tensor names to HF/transformer names and runs a GGUF linear method that dequantizes on the fly. fastsafetensors uses GPU Direct Storage (cuFile) to DMA weights file->GPU bypassing the host bounce buffer.
leading_impls.refs: github.com/huggingface/safetensors (Rust + python); vLLM csrc/quantization/gguf (gguf dequant kernels) and docs/features/quantization/gguf.md; fastsafetensors (docs.vllm.ai/.../fastsafetensor, GPU Direct Storage / cuFile); vLLM nvfp4 sm120 kernels csrc/quantization/fp4/nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu, nvfp4_blockwise_moe_kernel.cu (ENABLE_NVFP4_SM120, commit e502098, v0.12.0/0.13.0).
leading_impls.strengths: safetensors is trivial to parse (8B + one JSON), 100% mmap-able, secure (no pickle/code-exec), and the native format for un-quantized / NVFP4 / AWQ / GPTQ HF checkpoints. fastsafetensors/cuFile gives true GPU Direct Storage (NVMe->GPU, no host bounce) -- the fastest cold load path. config.json/AutoConfig is a clean hparam source. vLLM proves NVFP4 sm_120 GEMM kernels exist and compile.
leading_impls.weaknesses: safetensors carries NO architecture/tokenizer metadata in-band -- needs sidecar config.json + tokenizer.json + generation_config.json (multi-file, not single-file deploy). No k-quant support (only whole-tensor dtypes), so the rich GGUF quant zoo (Q4_K_M etc.) is GGUF-only. vLLM GGUF path is a second-class adapter (name remap, slower dequant-linear) and NVFP4 MoE on sm_120 is still buggy (falls back to Marlin W4A16; issues #30135/#31085).

leading_impls.approach: candle-core gguf_file.rs and mistral.rs implement the same GGUF binary parse in safe Rust: Content::read parses header/metadata/tensor_infos, then memmap2-mmaps the data section; tensors are read lazily by offset. mistral.rs maps gguf metadata keys to its model configs and supports ISQ (in-situ quant) and GGUF k-quants with custom CUDA dequant kernels.
leading_impls.refs: candle: candle-core/src/quantized/gguf_file.rs (Content, TensorInfo, Value enum mirrors gguf_metadata_value_type); mistralrs-quant (gguf cuda dequant); memmap2 crate.
leading_impls.strengths: Clean idiomatic Rust reference for a from-scratch Rust loader; safe parsing with the exact same wire format; memmap2 gives zero-copy mmap; good template if the engine is Rust. Shows how to map GGUF metadata->config in Rust.
leading_impls.weaknesses: CUDA dequant kernels are less mature/tuned than llama.cpp MMQ; no async pinned double-buffer upload as polished as llama.cpp; sm_120 tuning not specifically validated.

recommendation.design: Build a single dependency-free GGUF reader copied structurally from llama.cpp's ggml/src/gguf.cpp, plus a safetensors reader as a thin secondary path. (1) PARSE: mmap the file (MAP_SHARED + posix_fadvise SEQUENTIAL; do NOT MAP_POPULATE for partial-offload models so the OS only faults in pages we touch). Read magic/version(==3)/tensor_count/kv_count, then KV pairs into a typed map, then tensor_infos. Compute data_offset = GGML_PAD(end_of_tensor_infos, alignment) where alignment = general.alignment or 32. Each tensor's absolute file offset = data_offset + info.offset. Validate offset==running_padded_size like llama.cpp does. (2) CONFIG: map general.architecture + [arch].* keys (block_count, embedding_length, feed_forward_length, attention.head_count[_kv], attention.key_length/value_length, rope.freq_base/dimension_count/scaling.*, expert_count, expert_used_count, attention.layer_norm_rms_epsilon) into a ModelConfig struct; read embedded tokenizer (tokenizer.ggml.model/tokens/merges/scores/token_type + bos/eos/etc ids) and tokenizer.chat_template. (3) PLACEMENT + SPILL TIER: classify each tensor by name (token_embd, output*, blk.N.*, ffn_*_exp[s]) and a placement policy that, given the 24GB/12-16GB-host budget, assigns a tier: GPU-resident (hot dense layers + attention), GPU-paged (MoE expert weights, paged in on demand), host-pinned (spill), or mmap-on-disk (cold experts). Store per-tensor {file_idx, file_offset, ggml_type, ne[], tier}. (4) UPLOAD: copy llama.cpp's load_all_data async pipeline verbatim -- 4 round-robin pinned host staging buffers (64MB when O_DIRECT alignment available, else 1MB), ggml_backend_event_synchronize before buffer reuse, async H2D copy overlapping NVMe read. Upload QUANTIZED blocks verbatim (no dequant at load); dequantize in the matmul kernel (MMQ-style) or just-in-time for paged experts. (5) For the spill tier specifically: keep expert weights mmap'd or in host-pinned memory and stream them to a small GPU ring buffer on expert activation, reusing the same pinned-buffer/event machinery. Use O_DIRECT + posix_memalign aligned reads (copy llama.cpp read_aligned_chunk) for the cold-disk path to bypass page cache pressure (host RAM is scarce here).
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: Loader/parser is pure C/C++ or Rust, no GPU lib needed. CRITICAL build constraint for the kernels that consume the loaded tensors: compile all CUDA with CUDA 12.8 (present at /usr/local/cuda-12.8, V12.8.93), NOT 13.1 -- CUDA 13.1 + MMQ segfaults on sm_120 (llama.cpp issue #19868). Use -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=120a-real (the 'a' suffix is mandatory: Blackwell FP4 tensor-core PTX is NOT forward-compatible; '120' alone or '120-virtual' will JIT-fail). cmake 4.2.3 present (fine). For dequant/matmul of GGUF blocks, lift llama.cpp's MMQ kernels (ggml/src/ggml-cuda/mmq.cu + template-instances/mmq*.cu) and dequantize.cu/convert.cu; they are the proven-fast sm_120 path (pp~5600-6600, tg~210). For optional native FP4 tensor-core GEMM use CUTLASS 3.9.0+ SM120 blockscaled NVFP4 collectives (CUTLASS added 'Blackwell SM120 epilogue + full EVT fusions' and SM12x NVFP4 examples; see Colfax SM12x NVFP4 tutorial) -- but treat as experimental. For the cold-load fast path optionally add cuFile (GPU Direct Storage) like fastsafetensors.
recommendation.copy_from_sources: Copy 1:1 the binary read order and bounds-checking from ggml/src/gguf.cpp::gguf_init_from_reader and the gguf_reader::read<T> overloads. Copy struct field semantics from ggml/include/gguf.h. Copy the mmap/O_DIRECT file layer from src/llama-mmap.cpp (llama_file::impl::read_aligned_chunk, init_fd O_RDONLY|O_DIRECT, llama_mmap::impl ctor). Copy the entire async-upload pipeline from src/llama-model-loader.cpp::load_all_data (4 pinned buffers, events, ggml_backend_tensor_set_async, alignment handling) and load_data_for/get_mapping_range. Copy block struct layouts from ggml/src/ggml-common.h (block_q4_K, block_q6_K, block_q8_K, block_mxfp4 17B/32elem, block_nvfp4 36B/64elem, QK_K=256). Copy MMQ + dequantize CUDA kernels from ggml/src/ggml-cuda/. For safetensors copy the 8B-LE-header + JSON {dtype,shape,data_offsets} parse (huggingface/safetensors) and candle-core/src/quantized/gguf_file.rs if building in Rust.
recommendation.rationale: GGUF is llama.cpp's own format; its parser is small, dependency-free, mmap-first, and already handles every quant type, MoE split-expert tensors, embedded tokenizer, sharding, and big-endian -- reimplementing from the spec risks subtle offset/alignment bugs, so copying it verbatim is both allowed and optimal. The async pinned-buffer upload pipeline is the single highest-leverage piece to copy: it overlaps NVMe IO with H2D and is exactly what a 24GB/150W laptop needs for fast cold loads and for the spill tier (same machinery streams paged experts). Loading quantized blocks verbatim + in-kernel dequant (MMQ) is what gives the proven 5-6x speedup over cuBLAS on sm_120. safetensors is kept as a thin secondary path for native/NVFP4 HF checkpoints where the FP4 tensor cores can eventually be exploited. The build-toolchain constraint (12.8 not 13.1, 120a-real) is the make-or-break detail: get it wrong and the engine either segfaults or silently runs 5x slow.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: The loader/parser itself is pure host code and has ZERO sm_120 risk -- it is byte-identical regardless of GPU. The real risk is in the kernels that consume the loaded tensors and in the BUILD TOOLCHAIN: (1) MUST build CUDA with 12.8, NOT the 13.1 that is the default nvcc on this box -- CUDA 13.1 + MMQ segfaults on sm_120 (llama.cpp issue #19868); (2) MUST use arch 120a-real (the 'a' suffix) because Blackwell FP4 tensor-core instructions are not forward-compatible -- plain 120/120-virtual JIT-fails; (3) MUST NOT leave GGML_CUDA_FORCE_CUBLAS=ON in CMake cache or you silently lose ~5-6x prompt-processing throughput; (4) block_mxfp4's 17-byte/1-byte-aligned layout causes misaligned global loads on CUDA (discussion #18427) -- a perf wrinkle, not a correctness blocker.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: llama.cpp ggml/src/ggml-cuda/CMakeLists.txt explicitly: '120 == Blackwell, needs CUDA v12.8, FP4 tensor cores' and appends 120a-real for CUDAToolkit>=12.8, 121a-real for >=12.9, with a comment that 12X must be rewritten to 12Xa because 'Blackwell FP4 tensor core instructions are not forwards compatible'. Benchmark (zenn.dev/toki_mwc, RTX 5090 Blackwell SM120, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B MoE, llama.cpp b8240): CUDA 12.8 + MMQ = pp 5611-6566 / tg 201-211 t/s (FASTEST); CUDA 12.8 + cuBLAS = 5-6x slower; CUDA 13.1 + MMQ = SEGFAULT; cites NVIDIA Blackwell migration guide recommending CUDA 12.8 for cc120. MXFP4 (ggml type 39) has real tensor-core acceleration on sm_120 in llama.cpp (insiderllm.com FP4 guide). vLLM v0.12.0/0.13.0 ships NVFP4 sm_120 kernels (csrc/quantization/fp4/nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu, ENABLE_NVFP4_SM120). CUTLASS 3.9.0 added 'Blackwell SM120 epilogue and full set of EVT fusions' + SM120 examples; Colfax 'NVFP4 Blockscaled GEMM on RTX Pro Blackwell SM12x' tutorial. Local box verified: GPU compute_cap 12.0 (sm_120), CUDA 12.8.93 present at /usr/local/cuda-12.8 alongside 13.1, cmake 4.2.3.

components.beats_competitors_how: "By copying llama.cpp's proven sm_120-correct loader and kernels (avoiding the CUDA-13.1 segfault and the FORCE_CUBLAS 5x trap that bite naive builders) while adding a smarter, hardware-aware placement+spill tier that the competitors lack on this exact config. vLLM/SGLang assume the model fits in VRAM and treat GGUF as a second-class adapter (name remap + slow dequant-linear) and their NVFP4 MoE on sm_120 is still buggy (falls back to Marlin); they have no first-class disk/host expert-spill story for a 24GB card. llama.cpp has the format and MMQ kernels but its mmap path uploads to GPU synchronously and its offload is coarse (n_gpu_layers, whole-layer granularity) with no demand-paged expert streaming. Our edge: (a) per-tensor tiering (hot dense+attn resident, MoE experts demand-paged from host-pinned/mmap into a small GPU ring buffer via the copied 4-buffer async pipeline) so a 35B-A3B-class MoE that won't fit in 24GB still runs near memory-bandwidth limits; (b) O_DIRECT aligned cold reads to avoid evicting the host LLM servers sharing this box's scarce ~12-16GB free RAM; (c) build pinned to CUDA 12.8 + 120a-real + MMQ on, guaranteeing the fast path the benchmarks show (pp~6.5k/tg~210) instead of the cuBLAS fallback; (d) keep blocks quantized through upload and dequant in-kernel to stay power/bandwidth-efficient at 150W. The loader is not where we 'win' raw matmul -- it's where we win by fitting bigger MoE models into 24GB without the spill stall that kills competitors."
open_questions: Does MMQ on CUDA 13.x get fixed soon? If a future llama.cpp/CUDA combo makes 13.1 work on sm_120, we could use the newer toolchain features; until then we are pinned to 12.8 which constrains other kernels (FlashAttention-4, tcgen05 MMA) that may want 13.x.

Is native NVFP4/MXFP4 tensor-core GEMM (CUTLASS SM120 blockscaled) actually faster than llama.cpp MMQ on THIS 150W laptop for our quant mix, given the 1-byte-aligned block_mxfp4 misalignment penalty (discussion #18427)? Needs an empirical bake-off; may justify re-packing MXFP4 to an aligned layout at load time.

For the spill tier: what is the real PCIe/host-RAM bandwidth ceiling for demand-paging MoE experts on this laptop, and does cuFile/GPU Direct Storage (fastsafetensors-style) beat the host-pinned bounce buffer for cold expert loads given only ~12-16GB free host RAM?

Should we re-pack/re-align quantized blocks at load time (e.g. pad block_mxfp4 to 4/16B alignment, or separate scales from quants per discussion #18427) to speed in-kernel dequant, accepting a one-time load-time transform cost and a non-standard on-GPU layout?

How to robustly map GGUF [arch].* metadata to our internal ModelConfig for the long tail of architectures (Qwen3.5 MoE, hybrid Mamba, etc.) without copying llama.cpp's giant per-arch switch -- is a data-driven arch registry sufficient, and how do we handle missing/optional keys and big-endian files?

Tokenizer fidelity: GGUF's embedded tokenizer.ggml.* can be lower-accuracy than the original; do we need to also support loading tokenizer.json / tokenizer.huggingface.json when present for correctness?

_verdict.component: GGUF format parser + model loader (sm_120 build-toolchain + MMQ-kernel feasibility for the copied-from-llama.cpp consume path)
_verdict.riskiest_claim: "Build with CUDA 12.8 (not 13.1) + arch 120a-real + MMQ-on, and you get the proven, CONFIRMED-working sm_120 fast path (pp~5611-6566 / tg~201-211); CUDA 13.1 + MMQ segfaults. This guarantees the fast path the benchmarks show instead of the cuBLAS 5x fallback." Plus the supporting claim that "MXFP4 (ggml type 39) has real tensor-core acceleration on sm_120."
_verdict.evidence: Core build-toolchain claim CONFIRMED by primary sources. (1) Local box matches: nvidia-smi shows RTX 5090 Laptop, compute_cap 12.0 (sm_120); /usr/local/cuda-12.8 (V12.8.93) and /usr/local/cuda-13.1 (V13.1.115, default nvcc) both present. (2) llama.cpp canonical ggml/src/ggml-cuda/CMakeLists.txt (master, fetched): comment "120 == Blackwell, needs CUDA v12.8, FP4 tensor cores"; auto-appends 120a-real for CUDAToolkit>=12.8 and force-rewrites 12X->12Xa with comment "Blackwell FP4 tensor core instructions are not forwards compatible and therefore need 12Xa" -> the '120a-real' requirement and toolkit pin are real and owner-endorsed. Issue #18398 confirms older nvcc fatal-errors on 'compute_120a'. (3) zenn.dev/toki_mwc benchmark (RTX 5090 SM120, llama.cpp b8240, CUDA 12.8.61 vs 13.1.115 both tested) reproduces the exact numbers: CUDA12.8+MMQ pp5611-6566/tg201-211; CUDA13.1+MMQ = Segmentation fault; FORCE_CUBLAS cache trap = ~5-6x pp loss. It built with -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=120, which CMake auto-rewrites to 120a-real, so the fast path WAS on 120a. NVIDIA's Blackwell RTX migration guide independently recommends CUDA 12.8 for cc120. HOWEVER the recommendation UNDERSTATES fragility / OVERSTATES MXFP4: (a) Issue #19662 - MXFP4 MMQ template instances FAIL TO COMPILE on sm_120 ("Instruction 'mma with block scale' / .kind::mxf4 / .block_scale / .scale_vec::2X not supported on .target 'sm_120'"); those are sm_100 datacenter-Blackwell PTX, NOT consumer sm_120 -> MXFP4 native tensor-core MMA does NOT run on this card; it gets dp4a/int-MMQ + memory savings only. InsiderLLM FP4 guide corroborates: native NVFP4 MMQ dispatch on sm_120 only landed b8967 (Apr 29 2026), MXFP4-native is mostly ik_llama.cpp, and consumer Blackwell FP4 is "experimental, quality vs Q4_K_M unsettled." (b) Issue #23385 - NVIDIA driver bug (sharedMemPerBlockOptin overflow on Blackwell drivers incl 590.x; THIS box runs driver 595) fatally aborts MMQ at RUNTIME regardless of 12.8 vs 13.1, unless llama.cpp's smpbo sanity-check workaround is present. (c) Issue #18331 - nvcc -O3 codegen bug crashes MMQ even on CUDA 13.0/sm_121; safe fallback was sm_89/-O2. So the path is real but NOT "guaranteed"; a from-scratch copy of MMQ that omits these defensive workarounds will crash.
_verdict.correction: Keep the design — copy llama.cpp's GGUF reader + MMQ kernels, pin CUDA 12.8, use 120a-real, keep MMQ on and FORCE_CUBLAS off. But correct three over-confident points before relying on them: (1) Drop/qualify "MXFP4 has real tensor-core acceleration on sm_120." On consumer sm_120, MXFP4 (and pre-b8967 NVFP4) runs via int dp4a/MMQ + memory savings, NOT the block-scale FP4 MMA (that PTX is sm_100-gated, issue #19662). If you want true FP4 tensor-core GEMM, target NVFP4 with a recent (>=b8967) MMQ dispatch or CUTLASS SM120 blockscaled collectives, and treat it as experimental + benchmark vs Q4_K_M — do not assume a free 5x from "FP4 tensor cores." (2) Change "guaranteed fast path" to "fast path subject to two runtime landmines you MUST also port": copy llama.cpp's sharedMemPerBlockOptin sanity-check (clamp smpbo when driver returns 0 or >~1MB, issue #23385 / PR #22338) because this box's driver 595 may hit it, and avoid nvcc -O3 on the MMQ TUs or test for the codegen-corruption crash (issue #18331; fallback -O2 or sm_89 PTX). (3) Pin to a llama.cpp commit that already contains the smpbo MMQ fix (post-#22338 era) when lifting kernels, and validate the actual build end-to-end on THIS driver+12.8 with a small MoE before trusting the benchmark numbers. The "beats competitors via copy-the-correct-build + smarter spill tier" thesis still stands, but the win is the per-tensor MoE spill tiering, not FP4 tensor-core magic on consumer Blackwell.

components.component: CUDA runtime: graphs, allocator, streams (decode-loop execution runtime for a from-scratch sm_120 LLM engine)

leading_impls.approach: Two-tier CUDA-graph dispatch: PIECEWISE (per-subgraph, attention split out so paged attention runs eager) and FULL (whole-step) graphs, keyed by a BatchDescriptor (num_tokens padded to a bucket, num_reqs, uniform-decode flag, LoRA count). Decode is captured at a fixed set of `cudagraph_capture_sizes`; runtime batch size is padded up to the nearest captured bucket. Capture/replay via torch.cuda.CUDAGraph with a shared graph mem pool (torch caching allocator backs persistent input/output buffers). An offloader hook syncs/forks a copy stream around capture so weight prefetch overlaps replay.
leading_impls.refs: vllm/v1/cudagraph_dispatcher.py (CudagraphDispatcher: _compute_bs_to_padded_graph_size, _create_padded_batch_descriptor, dispatch); vllm/compilation/cuda_graph.py (CUDAGraphWrapper.__call__ capture-or-replay, weak_ref_tensors, set_graph_pool_id, get_offloader().sync_prev_onload()/join_after_forward()); torch caching allocator + CUDAGraph pool; FULL-mode needs exact num_reqs for FA3 scheduler_metadata
leading_impls.strengths: Battle-tested padding/bucketing; PIECEWISE keeps data-dependent paged-attention OUT of the graph (graph-safe by construction); shared mem pool reuses capture memory; offloader stream integration for prefetch overlap; debug input-address verification on replay
leading_impls.weaknesses: Python/torch.compile-coupled, heavy; FULL graphs need static num_reqs; one graph per bucket = VRAM cost (mitigated by pool sharing); still 1 launch per decode step (~1.3us launch cost remains); padding wastes compute on small batches; assumes torch caching allocator owns memory

leading_impls.approach: CudaGraphRunner pre-captures decode graphs for a list of batch sizes into static input buffers (input_ids, positions, out_cache_loc, kv block tables) that are overwritten in place each step then replayed; pairs with torch.compile (piecewise/full) and a graph-safe attention backend (FlashInfer/FA3) whose metadata is precomputed into captured buffers. Padding to nearest captured bs.
leading_impls.refs: python/sglang/srt/model_executor/cuda_graph_runner.py (CudaGraphRunner.capture / replay / can_run; static input tensors; bs buckets); integrates flashinfer plan/run split so the data-dependent plan stays outside the graph
leading_impls.strengths: Clean static-buffer model; explicit graph-safe attention metadata precompute; good high-concurrency decode
leading_impls.weaknesses: Same per-step replay launch overhead; capture memory scales with #buckets; torch-dependent; designed for datacenter throughput not single-stream latency

leading_impls.approach: Graph REUSE rather than re-capture: captures the decode graph once, then on subsequent tokens checks if topology is unchanged and uses cudaGraphExecUpdate / per-node param update (cudaGraphExecKernelNodeSetParams) to patch pointers/kernel args in place; disables graphs when node count changes or when ops are graph-unsafe (e.g. some MoE/host-decided paths), tracking number_consecutive_updates and disable_due_to_* flags. Allocator is a simple per-device VRAM pool (ggml_cuda_pool) over cudaMalloc, plus optional CUDA VMM (cuMemCreate/cuMemMap) pool for growable reservations.
leading_impls.refs: ggml/src/ggml-cuda/ggml-cuda.cu (ggml_backend_cuda_graph_compute: use_cuda_graph, cuda_graph_update_required, cudaGraphExecUpdate, maintain_cuda_graph, is_cuda_graph_update_required); ggml-cuda/common.cuh defines GGML_CUDA_CC_BLACKWELL=1200 (consumer Blackwell is a first-class gated arch); ggml_cuda_pool / pool_vmm
leading_impls.strengths: In-place exec update avoids re-instantiation cost; explicit graph-safety disable heuristics; VMM pool grows without re-reserving; sm_120 explicitly recognized (GGML_CUDA_CC_BLACKWELL); broad model coverage
leading_impls.weaknesses: Update path is fragile (any topology change forces re-capture); graphs disabled for several MoE/dynamic paths; dequant-to-FP16 hot path leaves FP4 tensor cores unused; pool is a basic free-list, not stream-ordered

leading_impls.engine: imp (kekzl/imp) — from-scratch sm_120a engine, the closest reference to this project's goal
leading_impls.approach: THREE runtime tiers: (1) CudaGraphCapture = capture-once + replay with cudaGraphExecUpdate fast-path (drop_graph_keep_exec / end_capture_and_update) and cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim on reset; (2) CudaGraphRunner = warmup-steps then capture, permanent fallback-to-eager on capture failure; (3) CudaGraphConditionalRunner = a GPU-AUTONOMOUS decode megaloop using a cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional WHILE node — the whole decode body (forward_decode_async -> sample -> post_decode_step_kernel) is captured into the body graph and the GPU loops on-device, streaming tokens to the host via cudaHostAlloc(Mapped) ring buffer + __threadfence_system() + cudaGraphSetConditional to break on EOS/stop/budget. Capture uses cudaStreamCaptureModeRelaxed (CUTLASS grouped-GEMM HANGS under Global). PDL: post-capture pass rewrites kernel->kernel graph edges to cudaGraphDependencyTypeProgrammatic (cudaGraphKernelNodePortProgrammatic) for tail/head overlap. Allocator = thin VRAMAllocator tracker over raw cudaMalloc with headroom fraction + cudaMemGetInfo guard (NOT a pool). Paged KV cache block_size=16.
leading_impls.refs: src/runtime/cuda_graph.cu (get_capture_mode relaxed-default w/ CUTLASS-hang rationale; apply_pdl_edges; CudaGraphCapture::{begin_capture,end_capture,try_update,end_capture_and_update,drop_graph_keep_exec,reset+cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim}; CudaGraphRunner::execute warmup->capture->replay; CudaGraphConditionalRunner::setup with cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate + cudaGraphCondTypeWhile + cudaStreamBeginCaptureToGraph + post_decode_step_kernel + mapped ring buffer; rearm() to re-run burst without recapture); src/memory/vram_allocator.cu; src/memory/kv_cache.cu; docs/architecture.md (Phase 2 warmup captures decode graph; non-Gemma-4 MoE DISABLES graph due to host-side routing)
leading_impls.strengths: Eliminates per-token CPU round-trip via on-GPU WHILE loop (closest practical thing to a megakernel without writing one); proven on sm_120a; documents the exact sm_120 capture pitfalls (relaxed mode, CUTLASS hang, split-K pipeline broken in conditional body); PDL edge overlap; graph mem trim to reclaim VRAM; MIT-licensed, directly liftable
leading_impls.weaknesses: Single-stream/single-GPU only; MoE with host-side routing can't be graphed (falls back to per-step); allocator has no pooling/defrag (raw cudaMalloc); conditional-graph decode needs careful state (position/context_len) management; experimental

leading_impls.engine: Megakernel / persistent-kernel research (Hazy Research 'no-bubbles', Mirage MPK, FlashFormer, Fleet)
leading_impls.approach: Fuse the entire decode forward pass into ONE persistent kernel with an on-GPU instruction interpreter + global-memory counter synchronization, eliminating kernel boundaries (and thus launch overhead entirely) and pipelining weight loads across 'instructions'. Mirage MPK compiles a tensor program into a megakernel with step/tokens meta-tensors. Targets bs=1 latency.
leading_impls.refs: hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-05-27-no-bubbles (H100/B200, 78% BW, sub-ms 1B forward, 2.5x vLLM); github.com/mirage-project/mirage (mpk); arxiv 2512.22219 (Mirage paper); FlashFormer; Fleet (arxiv 2604.15379)
leading_impls.strengths: Removes ALL launch overhead (CUDA-graph replay still costs ~1.3us/step per their measurement); best possible decode latency; finer-grained sync than PDL
leading_impls.weaknesses: All published megakernels target sm_90/sm_100 (H100/B200), NOT sm_120; enormous engineering effort; per-model instruction schedules; no sm_120 megakernel exists today — high risk to build from scratch

recommendation.design: Adopt imp's three-tier runtime, tuned for this 24GB/150W 5090 laptop. (1) Allocator: build a SLAB/paged GPU memory pool, NOT raw cudaMalloc-per-tensor and NOT torch's allocator.
