Reserve weights + workspaces once at init via a bump/arena allocator; for the KV cache use a paged pool of fixed 16-token blocks (block_size=16 like imp/vLLM) handed out by a free-list block manager — this is what lets attention be graph-safe (block tables are device pointers, contents change but the captured kernel args don't). Use cudaMallocAsync + a cudaMemPool (confirmed working here) ONLY for transient prefill scratch with a set release threshold (cudaMemPoolAttrReleaseThreshold) so it doesn't fight the LLM servers sharing host/VRAM; keep the model-weight arena on plain cudaMalloc so it never moves. Spilling: a separate host-pinned staging pool (cudaHostAlloc) feeds a dedicated copy stream for expert/layer prefetch. (2) Streams/events: one compute stream + one copy(H2D)stream + one copy(D2H/token-out) stream; use cudaEvent_t to fork the copy stream for next-expert/next-layer prefetch DURING the current matmul, joining before consumption (vLLM offloader pattern). Wrap kernel->kernel edges with PDL (programmatic dependent launch) to overlap kernel tails/heads, since the 5090 has no tcgen05 async pipeline. (3) Graphs for decode: capture-once + replay with cudaGraphExecUpdate fast-path. Use cudaStreamCaptureModeRelaxed (mandatory — Global hangs CUTLASS grouped GEMM). For dense + graph-safe MoE, go one step further and use a cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional WHILE megaloop (empirically verified working on this GPU) so the GPU runs N decode steps autonomously and streams tokens via a mapped-pinned ring buffer + __threadfence_system — this removes the ~1.3us/step launch + CPU sync that vLLM/SGLang still pay, the single biggest single-stream decode win available without writing a true megakernel. Bucket only for prefill chunk sizes; decode is bs=1..small so capture a few concrete batch sizes and pad up. Do NOT graph MoE with host-side routing — fall back to per-step replay there (imp's rule). (4) Skip building a from-scratch megakernel (all existing ones are sm_90/sm_100 and the effort is enormous); the conditional-WHILE graph captures ~80% of the megakernel's launch-overhead win at a fraction of the risk.
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: CUDA Driver/Runtime API: cudaStreamBeginCapture(cudaStreamCaptureModeRelaxed), cudaStreamBeginCaptureToGraph, cudaGraphInstantiate, cudaGraphLaunch, cudaGraphExecUpdate (in-place re-instantiation), cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate + cudaGraphAddNode(cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional, cudaGraphCondTypeWhile) + cudaGraphSetConditional (device), cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim. Allocator: cudaMallocAsync/cudaFreeAsync + cudaDeviceGetDefaultMemPool/cudaMemPoolSetAttribute(cudaMemPoolAttrReleaseThreshold) for prefill scratch; plain cudaMalloc arena for weights+KV. Overlap: cudaEventRecord/cudaStreamWaitEvent for copy/compute overlap; PDL via cudaGraphEdgeData{type=cudaGraphDependencyTypeProgrammatic, from_port=cudaGraphKernelNodePortProgrammatic} (imp apply_pdl_edges) + cudaTriggerProgrammaticLaunchCompletion/cudaGridDependencySynchronize in kernels. Mapped token streaming: cudaHostAlloc(cudaHostAllocMapped)+cudaHostGetDevicePointer. Optional growable reservation: CUDA VMM (cuMemCreate/cuMemMap/cuMemSetAccess) like llama.cpp pool_vmm for KV growth without re-reserve. All compiled -arch=sm_120a (SASS) with compute_120f PTX fallback.
recommendation.copy_from_sources: PRIMARY: lift imp's src/runtime/cuda_graph.cu near-verbatim — CudaGraphCapture (capture/exec-update/reset+trim), CudaGraphRunner (warmup->capture->replay->permanent-fallback), CudaGraphConditionalRunner (WHILE-node decode megaloop + post_decode_step_kernel + mapped ring buffer + rearm), and apply_pdl_edges. It is MIT, sm_120a-native, and already encodes the hard-won sm_120 gotchas. Lift imp's src/memory/vram_allocator.cu as the tracking layer but ADD pooling on top. For paged KV block management copy the block_size=16 paged scheme (imp src/memory/kv_cache.cu / vLLM PagedAttention block tables). For graph bucketing/padding logic copy vLLM's cudagraph_dispatcher.py _bs_to_padded_graph_size + _create_padded_batch_descriptor. For copy/compute overlap around capture copy vLLM cuda_graph.py offloader sync pattern (sync_prev_onload/join_after_forward) and weak_ref_tensors output handling. For the in-place update-vs-recapture decision copy llama.cpp ggml-cuda.cu cudaGraphExecUpdate heuristics.
recommendation.rationale: On a bs=1 single-user/agentic 5090 (the stated goal), decode is memory-bandwidth + launch-overhead bound, not FLOP bound (150W cap, 1.8TB/s GDDR7, 64MB L2). The runtime's job is to (a) never re-touch the CPU per token and (b) keep the copy engines busy prefetching spilled experts/layers while the tensor cores run. imp already proves a from-scratch sm_120 engine beats llama.cpp on decode and ties/beats vLLM on NVFP4 using exactly this runtime; the conditional-WHILE megaloop is the mechanism that gets the per-token launch/sync cost to ~zero (vs vLLM/SGLang's ~1.3us/step). A true megakernel would win a bit more but every published one is datacenter-Blackwell and the risk/effort is unjustified for a single-author engine. The allocator must be a real pool (not raw cudaMalloc) to avoid fragmentation and to make paged attention graph-safe, but must use a release-threshold mem-pool for scratch so it coexists with the other LLM servers sharing this box's tight 12-16GB free host RAM and 24GB VRAM.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: The CUDA *runtime* primitives (graphs, conditional WHILE/IF nodes, stream-ordered mem pools, PDL edges, mapped pinned streaming) all work on consumer Blackwell sm_120 — I verified them on THIS RTX 5090 Laptop GPU. The sm_120 risk is NOT in the runtime layer; it is in the GEMM/attention kernels the graph wraps: consumer Blackwell has NO tcgen05, NO TMEM, NO wgmma, NO TMA warp-specialized grouped GEMM (those are datacenter sm_100 only), and CUTLASS NVFP4 grouped-GEMM (mm_fp4) has been observed to produce garbage / hang under Global capture mode on sm_120 — so MoE FP4 must use mma.sync mxf4nvf4 / FA2-style block scaling (imp) or a Marlin fallback, and capture must use Relaxed mode. A secondary runtime gotcha: imp documents the split-K *pipeline* attention kernel being broken when captured inside a conditional WHILE body (it auto-detects capture and falls back to non-pipeline split-K).
sm120_feasibility.evidence: Empirically verified on this machine (2026-06-26, RTX 5090 Laptop, cc 12.0, CUDA 13.1 nvcc, driver 13.2): /tmp/cuda_rt_probe -> MemoryPoolsSupported=1, DefaultMemPool ok, cudaMallocAsync ok, 82 SMs, 64MB L2, 99KB shared opt-in. /tmp/cuda_graph_probe -> stream capture(relaxed)+instantiate+replay ok (counter=3 after 3 replays); cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate ok; cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional/cudaGraphCondTypeWhile addNode ok; cudaStreamBeginCaptureToGraph ok; full WHILE-loop graph instantiated and ran 5 iterations on-device. External: CUDA conditional nodes are 12.4+ (IF/WHILE), SWITCH/ELSE added 12.8 for Blackwell (developer.nvidia.com/blog/dynamic-control-flow-in-cuda-graphs-with-conditional-nodes). kekzl/imp ships a working sm_120a engine using exactly cudaStreamCaptureModeRelaxed + conditional-WHILE decode loop + PDL edges, with in-source rationale (CUTLASS grouped GEMM hangs under Global; prefill_graph_blockers_2026_05_14 memo). llama.cpp common.cuh defines GGML_CUDA_CC_BLACKWELL=1200 and gates Blackwell features on it. vLLM v0.12-0.13 added native NVFP4 sm_120 kernels (nvfp4_scaled_mm_sm120_kernels.cu, ENABLE_NVFP4_SM120; issue #31085, PRs #24968/#21309) though backend selection still defaults sm_120 MoE to Marlin in some versions.

components.beats_competitors_how: "Single-stream decode latency is the battleground on a bs=1 5090, and the runtime is where the win is. (1) vs vLLM/SGLang: they still pay ~1.3us launch + a CPU sync PER decode token (graph replay model). Our cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional WHILE megaloop runs the entire N-token decode burst on-GPU, streaming tokens back through a mapped-pinned ring buffer (__threadfence_system + atomic-acquire poll) — zero per-token CPU round-trip, which directly moves tok/s on a low-latency single user (imp demonstrates this beats vLLM NVFP4 decode and is +37-72% over llama.cpp on dense GGUF). (2) vs llama.cpp: its graphs are disabled on many MoE/dynamic paths and its hot path dequants FP4->FP16; we keep the decode loop fully graphed (where routing allows) and feed native sm_120a NVFP4 mma.sync kernels, and we use cudaGraphExecUpdate in-place so config/batch changes don't re-instantiate. (3) vs everyone: a release-threshold cudaMemPool for scratch + a fixed weight/KV arena means we coexist with the other LLM servers sharing this box's 12-16GB free RAM / 24GB VRAM without fragmentation OOMs, and the copy/compute stream overlap (PDL edges + dedicated prefetch stream) keeps the GDDR7 copy engines loading spilled experts during matmuls — essential because at 150W/24GB this engine is bandwidth- and spill-bound, not FLOP-bound. We deliberately skip a from-scratch megakernel (all are sm_90/sm_100) because the conditional-WHILE graph captures most of the launch-overhead win at a fraction of the risk."
open_questions: Can the conditional-WHILE decode megaloop be made graph-safe for MoE with host-side expert routing on sm_120, or must we always fall back to per-step replay there (imp falls back)? This determines whether 30B-class MoE — the headline VRAM-fitting use case — gets the launch-overhead win or not.

cudaMallocAsync mem-pool release-threshold behavior under contention with the OTHER LLM servers on this box: does the pool's reserved VRAM starve them, and what release threshold avoids both fragmentation and over-reservation? Needs measurement, not assumption.

Is it worth building a true sm_120 megakernel for the dense decode path (no published sm_120 megakernel exists)? Estimate the delta over the conditional-WHILE graph empirically before committing the large effort.

Exact PDL benefit on sm_120: imp converts edges to programmatic dependencies, but consumer Blackwell lacks tcgen05 async pipelines — measure whether PDL tail/head overlap actually helps decode on the 5090 or is mostly bookkeeping overhead.

cudaGraphExecUpdate success rate across the dynamic shapes we hit (context-length growth, batch changes): how often does it fall back to full re-instantiate, and does the graph mem pool (cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim) actually reclaim VRAM promptly enough for the 24GB budget?

Validate whether the split-K pipeline attention kernel issue (broken inside conditional WHILE body, per imp) also affects whatever attention kernel we choose, and whether the non-pipeline fallback costs decode throughput.

Whether to use CUDA VMM (cuMemCreate/cuMemMap) for a growable KV reservation (llama.cpp style) vs a fixed pre-reserved KV arena, given KV-cache quantization (INT8/NVFP4 KV) changes the per-token footprint dynamically.

_verdict.component: CUDA runtime: graphs, allocator, streams (decode-loop execution runtime for a from-scratch sm_120 LLM engine)
_verdict.riskiest_claim: "imp's cudaGraphNodeTypeConditional WHILE-node decode megaloop (streaming tokens via mapped-pinned ring buffer + __threadfence_system) is the mechanism that beats vLLM/SGLang by removing the ~1.3us/step launch + CPU sync, and is liftable to win the headline use case: 30B-class MoE that fits in 24GB." This single mechanism is what the entire 'beats_competitors_how' and the chosen runtime design rest on.
_verdict.evidence: The runtime-primitive and existence claims CONFIRM, but the headline win mechanism does NOT apply to the stated use case. Confirmed: (a) kekzl/imp exists, MIT, sm_120a-native; README claims +37-72% over llama.cpp dense decode and at-or-ahead of vLLM on NVFP4. (b) The CudaGraphConditionalRunner WHILE-node megaloop is REAL, non-stub, actively maintained code: src/runtime/cuda_graph.cu contains cudaGraphCondTypeWhile, cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate, post_decode_step_kernel (writes sampled token to a mapped-pinned ring buffer, calls cudaGraphSetConditional to break on EOS/stop/think-budget, with post-#798 fixes), rearm(), and the relaxed-capture rationale ("CUTLASS 3.x grouped GEMM was observed to silently HANG under cudaStreamCaptureModeGlobal during NVFP4 MoE prefill capture" — prefill_graph_blockers_2026_05_14 memo). (c) CUDA conditional WHILE nodes are a runtime/driver feature (CUDA 12.4+; SWITCH/ELSE 12.8), per NVIDIA's "Dynamic Control Flow in CUDA Graphs" + CUDA Programming Guide — NOT an sm_100-only hardware opcode, so the verified-on-this-5090 probe is credible. (d) ~1.3us CUDA-graph launch cost and "all published megakernels target sm_90/sm_100, none on sm_120" — confirmed verbatim by the Hazy Research 'no-bubbles' primary source. WHAT REFUTES THE HEADLINE FRAMING: In imp's actual decode entry (src/runtime/engine.cpp Engine::generate), the conditional graph loop runs ONLY when "config_.use_cuda_graphs && !offload_mgr_", else it falls to a per-token step() loop ("Decode - try conditional graph loop, fall back to step()"). On this 24GB laptop (vs imp's 32GB box) spilling/offload is MANDATORY per the task spec, so offload_mgr_ is active and the per-token-replay fallback runs - NOT the megaloop - for the very large-MoE case the win is claimed on. Additionally docs/architecture.md (canonical: "if code and doc disagree it's a doc bug") describes decode as "Replay the captured CUDA graph per token... Graph capture is enabled for most architectures; non-Gemma-4 MoE disables it because of host-side routing." So 30B-class non-Gemma MoE (Qwen3-30B/Coder-30B) gets NEITHER the WHILE megaloop NOR a basic decode graph - its 307 tok/s number is a 32GB-resident, non-offloaded config. The recommendation's own open_question #1 admits this ("must we always fall back to per-step replay there? This determines whether 30B-class MoE gets the launch-overhead win") - which is exactly the headline use case, so the win is unproven precisely where it is sold.
_verdict.correction: Keep the recommended runtime design (it is sound and sm_120-feasible), but re-scope the competitive claim and de-risk the MoE path: (1) Honestly state the WHILE-megaloop launch-overhead win applies to DENSE decode and Gemma-4-style graph-safe MoE that fits resident in VRAM - NOT to non-Gemma host-routed MoE and NOT when offload/spilling is active. On the 24GB target where large MoE MUST spill, the engine (like imp) falls back to per-token replay, so the headline "removes ~1.3us/step vs vLLM on the 30B-fits use case" is not delivered as written. (2) To actually capture the MoE win, the real engineering task is making expert routing graph-safe: do routing on-device (device-side top-k + a fixed-capacity grouped-GEMM with device block/expert tables as static kernel args, contents mutated in place) so the topology is constant and capturable - this is the open problem imp has not solved and is where the differentiated value (and risk) lives. (3) For the spill path, the win is NOT launch-overhead elimination but copy/compute overlap: a dedicated H2D prefetch stream + double-buffered expert staging hiding GDDR7/PCIe behind matmuls; benchmark that the prefetch fully hides at 24GB/150W before claiming any parity with a 32GB-resident config. (4) Treat the +37-72%/at-or-ahead-of-vLLM numbers as imp's own un-replicated, 32GB single-author measurements (README admits up to 2.6x prefill variance and depressed-host 8-15% decode swings) - do not inherit them as this engine's expected result on 24GB until measured locally. (5) PDL benefit on sm_120 is unverified (Hazy notes cudaGridDependencySynchronize is coarse and consumer Blackwell lacks tcgen05 async pipelines) - measure, do not assume.

components.component: Sampling / logits kernels + tokenizer (the model output path: temperature, top-k/top-p/min-p, repetition/presence/frequency penalties, logit bias, structured/guided decoding via grammar bitmask, plus prompt tokenization and detokenization)

leading_impls.approach: Sorting-free fused sampling kernels. Dual Pivot Rejection Sampling: instead of sorting the vocab (O(V log V)) for top-p/top-k, it does inverse-transform sampling over the probability vector and rejects/raises a pivot threshold until the accepted token satisfies the filter, provably O(log(1/eps)) rounds. One CUB BlockReduce+BlockScan threadblock per distribution. Temperature handled by a fused online-softmax kernel; top-k/top-p/min-p done from probs OR directly from logits; renorm helpers (top_p_renorm_probs/top_k_renorm_probs/top_k_mask_logits). RNG via curand Philox 4x32. Provides PyTorch, TVM, and header-only C++ APIs.
leading_impls.refs: include/flashinfer/sampling.cuh (entry pts: SamplingFromProb, SamplingFromLogits, TopPSamplingFromProb, TopKSamplingFromProb, TopKTopPSamplingFromProb, MinPSamplingFromProb, TopPRenormProb, ChainSpeculativeSampling, OnlineSoftmax); blog flashinfer.ai/2025/03/10/sampling.html; docs.flashinfer.ai/api/sampling.html (v0.6.13). Algorithm introduced v0.0.5, Dual-Pivot in v0.2.3.
leading_impls.strengths: Fastest known sampler; cuts vLLM sampling time >50% on large vocab (H100 measurements). No sort, no GPU->CPU sync, single fused kernel, CUDA-graph friendly (seed-as-tensor). Pure CUB/CCCL + curand - NO tensor cores, NO FP4/FP8, NO MMA. Only sm-gated code is griddepcontrol PDL (guarded __CUDA_ARCH__>=900, falls back gracefully). Header-only C++ path means it links into a non-Python engine. Bounded latency (logarithmic worst case) => predictable inter-token latency.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Does NOT implement penalties (repetition/presence/frequency) or logit-bias - those are a separate logits-processor step. Per-request torch.Generator unsupported since 0.2.3 (vLLM falls back to native for those). Outputs only statistically (not bitwise) equal to a reference categorical sampler. min-p CUDA kernel was added later than top-p/top-k.

leading_impls.approach: Dispatcher TopKTopPSampler with platform forward_cuda/forward_native/forward_cpu/forward_hip/forward_xpu. CUDA path calls flashinfer.sampling.top_k_top_p_sampling_from_logits (joint) / top_p_sampling_from_probs / top_k_sampling_from_probs; falls back to a PyTorch sort-based path (apply_top_k_top_p_pytorch) or a Triton kernel (apply_top_k_top_p_triton, used when batch>=8) when FlashInfer is off/unsupported or per-request generators present. Sampling uses the exponential-noise (Gumbel) trick - probs.div(q).argmax - to avoid torch.multinomial CPU sync. Penalties/logit-bias/bad-words are applied earlier in a separate logits-processor pipeline. Structured output applies an xgrammar/llguidance bitmask before sampling.
leading_impls.refs: vllm/v1/sample/ops/topk_topp_sampler.py (flashinfer_sample, apply_top_k_top_p, apply_top_k_only, random_sample, compiled_random_sample), vllm/v1/sample/ops/topk_topp_triton.py; structured output via vllm/v1/structured_output (xgrammar default, llguidance, outlines fallback). Env: VLLM_USE_FLASHINFER_SAMPLER.
leading_impls.strengths: Battle-tested dispatch with clean fallbacks; apply_top_k_only avoids full sort for top-k-only via torch.topk; Gumbel-noise sampling avoids CPU sync; gates FlashInfer on compute capability (sm_120 supported). Good reference for the exact call sequence and the temperature->penalty->bitmask->topk/topp->sample ordering.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Native fallback path sorts the whole vocab (slow at large V); Triton path needs Triton present; lots of Python orchestration overhead per step that a from-scratch engine can flatten.

leading_impls.approach: Also uses FlashInfer sampling kernels (same top_k_top_p_sampling_from_probs family). Key differentiator is structured-output integration: overlaps CPU-bound grammar mask generation with the GPU forward pass (pipeline) so guided decoding adds near-zero latency; supports xgrammar (default) and llguidance backends (--grammar-backend).
leading_impls.refs: sglang grammar backend (xgrammar/llguidance); SqueezeBits benchmark blog.squeezebits.com/guided-decoding-performance-vllm-sglang; sglang PR #3298 (llguidance).
leading_impls.strengths: Best guided-decoding latency hiding (overlap mask gen with GPU) - the architectural lesson to copy. Stable structured throughput vs vLLM's serial mask gen which stalls on complex schemas.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Same FlashInfer dependency; complexity of the overlap scheduler.

leading_impls.approach: CPU sampler chain (llama_sampler API): a composable list of samplers (top_k, top_p, min_p, typical, temperature/temp_ext, penalties incl. repetition/presence/frequency, dry, logit_bias, grammar, mirostat, xtc, dist). Each is a CPU pass over the logits/candidate array; top_k/top_p sort or partial-sort on CPU. Tokenizer is pure C++: 7 vocab types (SPM, BPE, WPM, UGM, RWKV, PLAMO2, NONE) and 53 pre-tokenizer regex variants (LLAMA3, GPT2, QWEN2, DEEPSEEK*, GEMMA4, etc.), all loaded from GGUF-embedded vocab/merges; naive_trie + byte-fallback. Optional grammar via built-in GBNF or -DLLAMA_LLGUIDANCE=ON.
leading_impls.refs: src/llama-vocab.cpp (LLAMA_VOCAB_TYPE_*, LLAMA_VOCAB_PRE_TYPE_* x53, naive_trie), src/llama-sampling.cpp (llama_sampler_init_top_k/top_p/min_p/penalties/dry/logit_bias/grammar), common/sampling.cpp. unicode.cpp for regex split.
leading_impls.strengths: GOLD STANDARD for GGUF: tokenizer reads everything from the GGUF, no external files - exactly matches the project's GGUF requirement. Pure C++/no deps, runs on CPU concurrently with GPU forward. Complete penalty/logit-bias/grammar suite. The 53 pre-tokenizer cases are the de-facto correctness spec for GGUF tokenization.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Sampler runs on CPU (fine for batch=1 single-user; full softmax+sort over 150k vocab is ~sub-ms on the Core Ultra but slower than a fused GPU kernel and forces a logits D2H copy). No GPU sampling kernel.

leading_impls.approach: Compiles JSON-schema/EBNF grammar into an automaton; partitions vocab into context-independent (precomputed mask) vs context-dependent (validated per step). Emits a packed int32 token bitmask (32 tokens/int). A tiny CUDA kernel (LogitsBitmaskKernel) masks invalid tokens to -inf in-place on the logits, vectorized via float4, dtype dispatch for fp32/fp16/bf16. Kernel is JIT-built with torch cpp_extension.load_inline (-use_fast_math), so it compiles to the local arch automatically.
leading_impls.refs: python/xgrammar/kernels/apply_token_bitmask_inplace_cuda.cu (LogitsBitmaskKernel, BITS_PER_BLOCK=32, THREADS_PER_THREAD_BLOCK=256, PackedNegativeInfinity); .py loader. NVIDIA-authored kernel.
leading_impls.strengths: Fastest mask APPLY (trivial elementwise, no tensor cores, sm-agnostic). Best for repetitive/cached schemas (pre-computation + caching). The bitmask+apply contract is the clean integration boundary between any grammar engine and the sampler.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Pre-computation can take seconds-to-minutes for complex schemas; mask gen stalls (tens-hundreds of ms) when slicer/cache misses on novel complex schemas; vLLM integration filter rejects some schemas.

leading_impls.approach: Rust constrained-decoding engine: Earley parser over a derivative-of-regex lexer, computes the token mask on-the-fly by walking a tokenizer prefix-trie (toktrie). ~50us single-core CPU per token for a 128k vocab (avg on JSONSchemaBench; <1% >1ms), negligible startup. Grammar formats: large JSON-schema subset, regex, Lark-like CFG. Bindings: Rust, C/C++ (llguidance.h), Python. Supports fast-forward tokens. Emits the same kind of token mask consumed by the apply-bitmask kernel.
leading_impls.refs: github.com/guidance-ai/llguidance README + docs/toktrie.md, docs/optimizations.md (slicer), parser/llguidance.h (C API), microsoft/derivre (lexer). Integrated in OpenAI structured outputs, vLLM (PR #14779), SGLang (#3298), llama.cpp (-DLLAMA_LLGUIDANCE=ON, b4613), mistral.rs (#899), Chromium.
leading_impls.strengths: Best for DYNAMIC/novel/complex schemas - no per-schema precompute, stable per-token cost, no pathological stalls. Native Rust + C API => links directly into a Rust or C++ engine WITHOUT Python. v1.0.0 (2025-06). CPU-only mask gen overlaps with GPU forward. Powers OpenAI's production structured outputs.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Slightly higher per-token cost than xgrammar's cache HIT (50us vs <8us); a few more JSON-schema compilation failures than xgrammar in MaskBench; CFG parsing CPU-bound (fine here, single user).

leading_impls.engine: HF tokenizers (Rust crate) + mistral.rs/candle GGUF path
leading_impls.approach: tokenizers crate: full normalizer->pre-tokenizer->model(BPE/Unigram/WordPiece)->post-processor pipeline, loads tokenizer.json. For GGUF, the vocab/merges are embedded; mistral.rs reconstructs a HF tokenizer from GGUF metadata (or GGUF can carry tokenizer.huggingface.json) so no separate tokenizer file is needed. candle discussion #2941 tracks GGUF tokenizer loading.
leading_impls.refs: crates.io/crates/tokenizers; huggingface/candle discussion #2941; mistral.rs GGUF tokenizer reconstruction. HF tokenizer.json as the canonical fast-tokenizer format.
leading_impls.strengths: Fast (Rust, parallel), exact match to HF reference tokenization, huge model coverage, trivial to embed in a Rust engine. tokenizer.json is the most portable spec.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Pure GGUF models that only embed llama.cpp-style vocab (not tokenizer.huggingface.json) require reconstructing the HF pipeline from GGUF metadata (the 53 pre-tokenizer regex cases) - non-trivial; risk of subtle mismatches on edge models. Doesn't itself read GGUF.

recommendation.design: Build a fully GPU-resident, sorting-free sampling pipeline plus a CPU tokenizer, single-user (batch ~1) tuned. Output path per step: (1) Keep logits on-GPU (fp32). (2) Apply a fused logits-processor kernel for penalties + logit-bias + structured bitmask: write one custom CUDA kernel that takes the raw logits and (a) applies repetition/presence/frequency penalties using a small per-request 'seen-token' buffer, (b) adds any logit_bias, (c) applies the grammar token bitmask to -inf (lift xgrammar's LogitsBitmaskKernel verbatim - int32 packed, float4-vectorized). This is ALL elementwise/scatter - no tensor cores. (3) Apply temperature via FlashInfer's fused online-softmax (or fold temperature into the logits scale before sampling-from-logits). (4) Sample with FlashInfer's Dual-Pivot rejection sampler: call TopKTopPSamplingFromProb / MinPSamplingFromProb / SamplingFromLogits from the header-only C++ API (sampling.cuh) - NO full sort, single threadblock per distribution, CUDA-graph friendly. Order matches vLLM: temp -> penalties/bias -> grammar mask -> top-k -> top-p -> min-p -> sample. For structured output, use a CPU grammar engine running CONCURRENTLY with the GPU forward (SGLang's overlap trick) that emits the int32 bitmask each step. Pick llguidance as the primary grammar engine (Rust/C API, no Python, stable per-token cost, OpenAI-proven) and optionally cache like xgrammar for hot repetitive schemas. Tokenizer: pull vocab/merges directly from the GGUF and run a CPU tokenizer; for correctness parity, port llama.cpp's vocab loader (the 7 vocab types + 53 pre-tokenizer regex cases ARE the GGUF tokenization spec) OR, when the GGUF embeds tokenizer.huggingface.json, hand it to the Rust 'tokenizers' crate. Detokenization streams via the same vocab with byte-fallback handling. Keep the sampler optional-bypass: greedy (argmax) and pure-temperature paths skip rejection sampling entirely.
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: GPU sampler: FlashInfer include/flashinfer/sampling.cuh, header-only C++ API - functions SamplingFromLogits, TopKTopPSamplingFromProb, TopPSamplingFromProb, TopKSamplingFromProb, MinPSamplingFromProb, OnlineSoftmax/softmax(temperature), TopPRenormProb/TopKMaskLogits, ChainSpeculativeSampling (for future spec-decode). Built on CUB/CCCL BlockReduce+BlockScan+BlockAdjacentDifference + curand Philox4x32. Compile -arch=sm_120 with the local nvcc 13.1. Grammar mask apply: xgrammar python/xgrammar/kernels/apply_token_bitmask_inplace_cuda.cu (LogitsBitmaskKernel template<T,PackedT,kBitsPerThread>, BITS_PER_BLOCK=32, THREADS_PER_THREAD_BLOCK=256). Grammar engine: llguidance (Rust crate + parser/llguidance.h C API; microsoft/derivre lexer) as primary; xgrammar as cache-optimized alternative for repetitive schemas. Tokenizer: port llama.cpp src/llama-vocab.cpp + unicode.cpp (GGUF-native, all 53 pre-tokenizer variants) as the GGUF source of truth; HF 'tokenizers' Rust crate when tokenizer.json/tokenizer.huggingface.json is available. Penalties/logit-bias: own fused CUDA kernel (model on llama.cpp's llama-sampling.cpp semantics + vLLM's logits-processor ordering).
recommendation.copy_from_sources: Lift verbatim: (1) FlashInfer sampling.cuh in full - it is header-only, MIT/Apache-2.0, and the whole Dual-Pivot rejection sampler + fused online-softmax is exactly what we want; do NOT reimplement the floating-point-safe prefix-sum logic (the blog warns it took many tries to get right). (2) xgrammar apply_token_bitmask_inplace_cuda.cu LogitsBitmaskKernel verbatim (Apache-2.0, NVIDIA-authored) for grammar masking. (3) llama.cpp llama-vocab.cpp vocab/pretokenizer tables and unicode.cpp as the GGUF tokenizer reference (MIT). (4) Mirror vLLM topk_topp_sampler.py's dispatch ordering and the Gumbel exponential-noise sampling trick (probs.div(q).argmax) for the no-filter fast path, and its apply_top_k_only torch.topk trick as a CPU/edge fallback. (5) Copy SGLang's grammar/GPU overlap scheduling pattern.
recommendation.rationale: The entire output path is the LOWEST-RISK component for sm_120: it has NO dependency on tensor cores, FP4/FP8 MMA, tcgen05, or any 5th-gen-tensor-core feature - the exact things that are broken/unwired on consumer Blackwell (FlashInfer #2577, vLLM #31085). FlashInfer's sampler is pure CUB + curand and its only arch guard is the griddepcontrol PDL hint under __CUDA_ARCH__>=900, which sm_120 (1200) satisfies and which I compiled and RAN on this exact RTX 5090 Laptop with nvcc 13.1 ('launch+sync: no error'). The xgrammar mask kernel is trivial elementwise and JIT-builds to the local arch. For single-user batch~1 on a 150k-vocab model, avoiding the O(V log V) sort (the vLLM-native and llama.cpp CPU fallback cost) and the logits D2H copy is the whole game - the rejection sampler keeps logits on-GPU and finishes in O(log) rounds. llguidance gives a no-Python, Rust/C structured-output engine matching the from-scratch-engine constraint, with stable latency that overlaps the GPU forward. GGUF tokenization is mandatory per the project goal, and llama.cpp's vocab loader is the only complete GGUF-native reference.

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: Essentially none for sampling/logits/tokenizer. The ONE thing to watch: FlashInfer's PyPI wheels historically gate the SAMPLING module behind compute-capability checks tied to attention/GEMM backends, and its broader sm_120 breakage (FlashInfer #2577) is in NVFP4 mm_fp4 GEMM and cutlass/cudnn/trtllm backends - NOT sampling. To be safe, compile FlashInfer's header-only sampling.cuh directly with the local nvcc -arch=sm_120 rather than relying on a prebuilt wheel's gating; that bypasses any over-broad capability guard. Secondary: griddepcontrol PDL is only a launch-overlap optimization, harmless if absent.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: EMPIRICAL on this GPU: compiled the exact FlashInfer arch-guarded snippet (asm 'griddepcontrol.wait/launch_dependents' under __CUDA_ARCH__>=900) with nvcc 13.1 -arch=sm_120 and ran it on the RTX 5090 Laptop (compute_cap 12.0) -> 'COMPILE OK' + 'launch+sync: no error'. SOURCE: FlashInfer sampling.cuh uses only <cub/cub.cuh>, <curand_kernel.h>, BlockReduce/BlockScan/AdjacentDifference - zero tensor-core/MMA/FP4 code; the only sm guard is the PDL hint at __CUDA_ARCH__>=900 (sm_120 qualifies). vLLM flashinfer_sampler_supported() gates on compute capability and is reported working on sm_120 (vLLM issue #37242: RTX 5090 + --attention-backend flashinfer, ~140 tok/s WSL2 / ~180-200 native, CUDA graphs work; sampling never flagged as broken). FlashInfer #2577 explicitly scopes the sm_120 breakage to NVFP4 GEMM, says 'Quantization step works fine - only GEMM backends are broken'. xgrammar apply_token_bitmask_inplace_cuda.cu JIT-builds via torch cpp_extension to the local arch (no sm gating, simple elementwise). llguidance + tokenizers + llama.cpp vocab are CPU/Rust/C++ and arch-irrelevant.

components.beats_competitors_how: Three concrete wins on this single-5090-laptop, batch~1 config. (1) Eliminate the sort AND the orchestration tax: lift FlashInfer's Dual-Pivot rejection sampler as a header-only kernel and call it from a thin Rust/C++ host with no Python - we keep vLLM/SGLang's >50% sampling-time reduction over sort-based sampling but without their per-step Python dispatch overhead, and without llama.cpp's CPU sampler + logits D2H copy. For greedy/temperature-only requests we bypass the rejection sampler entirely (argmax / Gumbel-noise), which neither llama.cpp's full sampler chain nor vLLM's general path special-case as cheaply. (2) Fuse the logits-processor stage: one custom CUDA kernel does penalties + logit-bias + grammar bitmask in a single pass over the on-GPU logits (vLLM does these as several separate ops; llama.cpp does them serially on CPU). (3) Structured output that doesn't stall: run llguidance on the idle CPU cores (Core Ultra 9, 24 cores) overlapped with the GPU forward (SGLang's trick), giving xgrammar-cache-hit-class throughput on repetitive schemas AND llguidance's stall-free behavior on novel/complex schemas - avoiding xgrammar's tens-to-hundreds-of-ms mask-gen stalls that erratically halt vLLM. Tokenizer correctness matches llama.cpp exactly (same GGUF-native vocab tables) so no accuracy regression, while keeping the hot path off the GPU's critical sub-150W power budget. Net: same or better tokens/s than vLLM+FlashInfer on sm_120, with lower single-stream latency and zero sm_120 risk because nothing here touches the broken FP4/FP8 tensor-core paths.
open_questions: Exact end-to-end sampling latency of FlashInfer header-only sampler on sm_120 at vocab 128k-160k, batch 1, 150W cap - needs a microbench on this GPU (the published numbers are H100 batch-scaling). Verify the rejection sampler's round count stays low for peaky low-temperature distributions typical of single-user decoding.

Does the FlashInfer PyPI/JIT cache wheel actually expose sampling on sm_120 today, or must we vendor sampling.cuh and compile it ourselves? Need to test 'import flashinfer; flashinfer.sampling.top_k_top_p_sampling_from_logits' on this box (no torch currently installed) vs a direct nvcc build of the header.

GGUF tokenizer fidelity: for target MoE GGUFs (e.g. Qwen3.5/MiniMax-style), does the GGUF embed tokenizer.huggingface.json (use Rust tokenizers crate) or only llama.cpp-style vocab+merges (must port the matching LLAMA_VOCAB_PRE_TYPE regex)? Confirm per target model to avoid silent tokenization drift.

Penalty kernel design: best data structure for the per-request seen-token set feeding repetition/frequency penalties at batch~1 (ring buffer of last-N vs full presence bitmap over vocab) and whether to fuse it with the bitmask kernel or keep separate for CUDA-graph stability.

Whether to also adopt FlashInfer ChainSpeculativeSampling now to keep the door open for EAGLE/MTP speculative decoding (the mtp-tail-predictor work), since it reuses the same rejection-sampling machinery.

CUDA-graph capture of the full sampler+bitmask+penalty chain on sm_120 (seed-as-tensor path) - confirm it captures cleanly given vLLM #37242 showed graph capture is fragile on Blackwell/WSL2 (native Linux here should be fine, but verify).

_verdict.component: Sampling / logits kernels + tokenizer (FlashInfer Dual-Pivot sampler + xgrammar bitmask + llguidance + llama.cpp GGUF tokenizer) on RTX 5090 Laptop sm_120
_verdict.riskiest_claim: The sm_120 feasibility claim: that FlashInfer's header-only sampling.cuh compiles and runs correctly on this consumer-Blackwell RTX 5090 Laptop (compute_cap 12.0) with nvcc 13.1, and that the well-documented Blackwell breakage (FlashInfer #2577) is scoped strictly to NVFP4/FP8 tensor-core GEMM backends and does NOT touch the sampling/logits path - i.e. the entire output path has zero dependency on tcgen05/wgmma/FP4-MMA, the broken-on-sm_120 features.
_verdict.evidence: Four independent lines of evidence all corroborate, none refute. (1) EMPIRICAL, reproduced on THIS box: compiled a CUB BlockReduce+BlockScan + curand Philox4x32 + griddepcontrol-PDL probe (the exact primitives sampling.cuh uses) with /usr/local/cuda-13.1/bin/nvcc -arch=sm_120; ran on the RTX 5090 Laptop (compute_cap 12.0, 24463 MiB) -> 'launch err: no error / sync err: no error'. (2) SOURCE: fetched include/flashinfer/sampling.cuh from main - it includes ONLY cuda.h, curand*, cub/cub.cuh, cuda/std; all 6 __CUDA_ARCH__ guards are exactly '>= 900' PDL/griddepcontrol hints (sm_120=1200 satisfies); grep found zero occurrences of sm_100, tcgen05, wgmma, cp.async.bulk, mxf4, nvf4, e4m3, e5m2 - no tensor-core/FP4/FP8 code path exists in the sampler. (3) PRIMARY MAINTAINER AUDIT: FlashInfer issue #3170 'DGX Spark (SM121) Current Support Audit' (MEMBER-authored, updated 2026-06-26) Section 7 'Sampling' lists sampling_from_logits/from_probs as 'Supported / Arch-agnostic', all top_k/top_p/min_p 'Supported', chain_speculative_sampling 'Supported'; the ONLY caveat is the clusters-topk optimization being SM100-only (LOW severity, falls back to slower path on sm_12x). The broken items it flags are GDN, mm_bf16, trtllm_allreduce, b12x MoE heuristic - none in the output path. (4) Issue #2577 (RTX PRO 6000 / sm_120) states verbatim 'Quantization step (nvfp4_quantize) works fine - only GEMM backends are broken'. (5) BONUS - merged PR #3461 (2026-06-09) ships official SM120-hardware (RTX PRO 6000, same cc 12.0) sampling benchmarks: top_k_top_p_sampling_from_logits batch=1 vocab=128k = 0.042ms, vocab=256k = 0.042ms, CUDA-graph compatible - directly answering the recommendation's open-question #1 and proving sub-0.1ms single-user sampling on sm_120. The xgrammar mask kernel, llguidance, tokenizers, and llama.cpp vocab are CPU/Rust/C++ or JIT-to-local-arch and arch-irrelevant, as claimed.
_verdict.correction: No correction to the core verdict - the sm_120 sampling claim holds. Three caveats to fold into the design, none fatal: (1) sampling.cuh is NOT a single self-contained header - it #includes topk.cuh, math.cuh, utils.cuh, vec_dtypes.cuh, allocator.h, so 'vendor sampling.cuh' means vendoring that whole include subtree (still header-only, MIT/Apache-2.0, all CUB/curand). Budget for that, not one file. (2) The fastest large-vocab path (top_k_first fast path, PR #3461, 2-4x speedup) lives in the PYTHON wrapper sampling.py (it orchestrates flashinfer.top_k radix-select -> renorm over k survivors -> top_p over k), NOT inside the .cuh kernel - a from-scratch C++ engine must re-implement that orchestration itself; calling the raw full-vocab TopKTopPSamplingFromProb kernel directly gives the slower 'before' numbers (still ~0.1ms at batch=1/128k, fine for single-user). (3) Open FlashInfer bug #3618/#3625 (still open 2026-06-26): multi-CTA top-k row_states buffer is shared across concurrent CUDA streams (per-device cache key) causing cross-stream corruption - harmless at the project's batch~1 single-stream target, but a correctness landmine if the engine ever runs concurrent decode streams; pin one stream or vendor the #3625 fix. Recommended fallback hierarchy if any of these bite: greedy/argmax bypass (trivial) -> single-CTA full-vocab rejection sampler (proven 0.042-0.1ms here) -> CPU sampler ala llama.cpp (sub-ms on the Core Ultra) as last resort.

components.component: Where competitors are weak on RTX 5090 Laptop (24GB, 150W, sm_120) and where a from-scratch engine can beat vLLM / SGLang / llama.cpp

leading_impls.approach: Python orchestration + CUDA-graph-captured PyTorch graph; PagedAttention; backends auto-selected by compute capability. On sm_120 it uses FA2 (not FA3/FA4 - those need SM90/SM100 TMEM) or the Triton/FLASHINFER attention backend; weight GEMM via Marlin (awq_marlin/gptq_marlin) for W4A16, and CUTLASS/FlashInfer for NVFP4 W4A4. Continuous batching + chunked prefill are its real strength at batch.
leading_impls.refs: vLLM issue #37242 (5090: ~140 tok/s Qwen3-14B-AWQ awq_marlin with CUDA graphs, ~180-200 bare-metal; enforce-eager only ~17 tok/s = 8x slower); FP8 W8A8 was 3x SLOWER than AWQ on WSL2 (44.9 tok/s) because dxgkrnl didn't expose Blackwell FP8 cores. NVFP4 MoE on sm_120: vLLM native CUTLASS grouped GEMM produces GARBAGE output (vLLM #33416, #31085; CUTLASS #2800/#2820); only Marlin W4A16 reliably correct. FlashInfer cutlass path needs 10+ file patches and CUDA 13 compute_120f to reach 39 tok/s (flashinfer issue #2723). vLLM v0.12+ added NVFP4 sm_120 (reddit 1pe4xm4).
leading_impls.strengths: Best batched/concurrent throughput (continuous batching + chunked prefill); mature Marlin W4A16; broadest model coverage; FlashInfer attention available.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Heavy Python/launch overhead per step (CUDA graphs mandatory just to be usable - 8x penalty without); multi-GB RAM + slow startup hostile to 12-16GB free host RAM; sm_120 NVFP4 MoE path is broken/garbage or 3x slower than dense FP4 due to broken CUTLASS grouped GEMM; NO weight/expert offload to host for models bigger than VRAM (no --n-cpu-moe equivalent) - it simply OOMs; single-stream decode latency is not its focus and loses to a tight custom kernel.

leading_impls.approach: Same family as vLLM: Python runtime, RadixAttention prefix cache, continuous batching, torch.compile, FlashInfer/Triton attention, CUTLASS/Marlin GEMM. Has an explicit 'SM120 Performance Optimization Plan' (issue #19637) tracking small-tile-N blockscaled GEMM and grouped GEMM for sm_120/sm_121.
leading_impls.refs: SGLang issue #19637 (SM120 plan: integrate CUTLASS Small Tile N Blockscaled GEMMs/Grouped GEMMs for SM120/121, ref flashinfer-ai/flashinfer#3152; SwapAB small-N tiles for FP8/NVFP4 dense + grouped MoE GEMM). Shares the same FlashInfer/CUTLASS sm_120 immaturity as vLLM.
leading_impls.strengths: Best-in-class prefix caching (RadixAttention) for shared-prefix/agent workloads; strong batched throughput; actively closing sm_120 kernel gaps.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Same Python per-token overhead and startup/RAM cost as vLLM; sm_120 grouped-GEMM MoE kernels still in-progress (the optimization plan is open, not done); no host expert offload; single-stream decode not its target.

leading_impls.approach: C++ GGML backend, GGUF format, per-op kernel dispatch (one CUDA launch per op, not a fused graph), -fa flash-attention, --n-cpu-moe / --cpu-moe / -ot for MoE expert offload to CPU RAM. CUDA graphs added but per-op model means more launch overhead than a megakernel. Quant via K-quants (Q4_K_M, Q4_K_XL) dequantized in custom CUDA kernels rather than CUTLASS tensor-core GEMM.
leading_impls.refs: hardware-corner desktop 5090 (32GB): Qwen3-8B Q4_K_XL 186 tok/s TG @4k, Qwen3moe-30B-A3B 234 tok/s @4k, Qwen3-32B dense 61 tok/s, gpt-oss-20B 112 tok/s, PP up to 10,400 tok/s. llama.cpp Discussion #15013 has a literal RTX 5090 LAPTOP 24GB CUDA13.2 entry. MoE offload: HF guide (Doctor-Shotgun) shows --n-cpu-moe / -ot exps=CPU; default GPU-offload-prefill threshold 32 tokens (GGML_OP_OFFLOAD_MIN_BATCH). Two-tier expert-cache RFC #20757: current --cpu-moe RE-COPIES ~144 hot experts per token RAM->GPU (most identical to prior token), discarding GPU copy each pass.
leading_impls.strengths: Best lightweight single-stream decode today (low overhead C++); GGUF + K-quants; the ONLY engine of the three with built-in MoE host offload (--n-cpu-moe); runs in tight VRAM/RAM; CUDA-graph support.
leading_impls.weaknesses: Per-op dispatch leaves bandwidth on the table vs a persistent megakernel; weaker tensor-core utilization than CUTLASS (dequant-then-GEMM K-quant path, not native blockscaled FP4 MMA); shallow/no real continuous batching (loses badly at concurrency); MoE offload is naive - re-copies hot experts every token (no persistent VRAM expert cache) so it is GPU/PCIe-thrash bound not compute bound; sampling/LM-head sometimes not fully fused.

recommendation.design: Build a single-GPU, sm_120-native engine around THREE edges competitors structurally lack on this laptop. (1) PERSISTENT MEGAKERNEL DECODE: one cooperative/persistent CUDA launch for the whole forward pass (a la Alpin's qwen_megakernel) to crush the per-token launch + Python overhead that forces vLLM/SGLang into mandatory CUDA graphs and that llama.cpp's per-op dispatch pays repeatedly. Target 71%+ of the laptop's ~896 GB/s = bandwidth-bound decode. Use custom atomic-generation grid barriers, flag-based partial barriers for the 16 attention blocks, and 'productive spin' (idle blocks issue prefetch.global.L2 to warm next-phase weights into the 96MB L2 during the bandwidth-idle attention phase). (2) TWO-TIER MoE EXPERT CACHE (the biggest win): persistent VRAM slot buffer (N<<n_expert slots, SLRU + frequency-gated admission) backed by pinned host RAM, backed by mmap/SSD - keeps the ~15-20% hot experts (which serve ~80% of tokens) resident across tokens. This is the gap ALL THREE leave open: vLLM/SGLang have NO host offload (OOM), llama.cpp re-copies hot experts every token. PoC showed 14 tok/s steady-state vs 0.5-1 tok/s naive offload (14-28x). (3) POWER/LOW-BIT TUNING for the 150W cap: prefer native sm_120 NVFP4 blockscaled MMA (m16n8k64, .kind::mxf4nvf4, scale_vec::4X, ue4m3) and MXFP4 (m16n8k32, mxf8f6f4, scale_vec::1X, ue8m0) - ~3-4x FLOPs/byte and ~41% energy reduction vs BF16, which directly buys tok/s under a power cap where FLOPs are not the binding constraint. Pre-quantize the KV cache to FP4/FP8 so attention consumes pre-packed uint8 (the on-the-fly quant is what killed Florian Mattana's kernel - never quantize K/V inside the attention kernel).
recommendation.exact_kernels_libs: Attention: hand-written sm_120 FA in CUDA C++ using warp-level mma.sync (NO tcgen05/TMEM on sm_120) - gau-nernst's kernel hits 94.4% SOL (197.7 TFLOPS, beats Dao flash-attn 90.97% and within 3% of cuDNN 97.19%); use ldmatrix.x4 for K/V, 2-3 stage pipelining, fast_exp = ex2.approx.ftz.f32, RoPE via __shfl_sync. Weight GEMM: CUTLASS 4.3.2+ sm_120 blockscaled NVFP4 (cute DSL MmaMXF4NVF4Op, 128x128x128 CTA tile, atom layout (4,2,1)=8 warps) - tops ~60% peak, ~3x over cuBLAS FP16; MUST compile with CUDA 13.x compute_120f (NOT 120a - 120a's TMA WS grouped GEMM tactics fail, 14.6 tok/s; 120f gets 39 tok/s). Marlin W4A16 as the safe fallback for GPTQ/AWQ GGUF-adjacent paths (only correct NVFP4-MoE path on broken-CUTLASS builds). Loads: ld.global.L1::no_allocate.v4.b32 (128-bit max on sm_120; 256-bit caps out), fence.acq_rel.gpu (lighter than __threadfence on Blackwell).
recommendation.copy_from_sources: github.com/AlpinDale/qwen_megakernel (persistent megakernel, atomic-gen barrier, productive-spin prefetch, two-phase->fused LM head argmax - lift wholesale for the decode loop). github.com/gau-nernst/learn-cuda/tree/e83c256/07_attention (v5 attention kernel = 94% SOL on 5090, copy the mma.sync + ldmatrix.x4 + pipelining structure). github.com/florianmattana/fp4-fused-attention-sm120 (the UNDOCUMENTED sm_120 FP4 MMA fragment + scale-factor lane layout for m16n8k32 - the load-bearing reverse-engineering). NVIDIA/cutlass include/cute/atom/mma_traits_sm120.hpp + examples/python/CuTeDSL/cute/blackwell_geforce/kernel/blockscaled_gemm (NVFP4 blockscaled GEMM reference, Colfax tutorial walks the SFA/SFB TV layouts). e1n00r/tinyserve + llama.cpp RFC #20757 (two-tier SLRU expert cache design: GPU slot buffer + pinned RAM + POSIX_MADV_WILLNEED/DONTNEED, ggml-backend.cpp:1445-1564 is the hook). FlashInfer for fp4 GEMM JIT entry points if not writing CUTLASS by hand.
recommendation.rationale: On this laptop (24GB, ~896 GB/s, 150W) decode is memory-bandwidth- and POWER-bound, not FLOP-bound, and models bigger than ~14B-Q4 must spill. The three structural weaknesses are exactly addressable: (a) python engines pay launch/dispatch overhead that a megakernel eliminates (proven 494->1000 tok/s just from overhead removal on Qwen3-0.6B); (b) NONE of the three has a good host-spilling MoE expert cache - vLLM/SGLang OOM, llama.cpp thrashes PCIe - so the SLRU two-tier cache is a clean 14-28x win for 30B+ MoE that doesn't fit; (c) the 150W cap means native FP4 (3-4x intensity, -41% energy) converts directly to tok/s where competitors fall back to BF16/FP8-emulated or broken-CUTLASS-FP4. CUDA 13.1 is already installed (enables the crucial compute_120f path that python engines often miss).

sm120_feasibility.critical_risk: sm_120 (consumer GB20x, cc 12.0) is architecturally DIFFERENT from sm_100 (datacenter GB100): NO tcgen05, NO TMEM, NO FA4. It uses Ampere/Ada-style warp-level mma.sync. So any kernel/library gated on sm_100a/tcgen05/TMEM (FlashAttention-4, many TensorRT-LLM cubins, SM100-only CUTLASS templates) WILL NOT run on this GPU. The concrete trap: NVFP4 grouped-GEMM (MoE) in CUTLASS/FlashInfer produces GARBAGE or crashes on sm_120 unless built with CUDA 13.x and compute_120f (the 'f'/full feature suffix); compute_120a's TMA warp-specialized grouped GEMM tactics fail and silently fall back ~3x slower (14.6 vs 39 tok/s). compute_120 (no suffix) errors on arch-conditional MMA. DENSE FP4 GEMM and dense attention are fine; the MoE grouped path is the fragile one.
sm120_feasibility.evidence: Colfax Research 'NVFP4 Blockscaled GEMM on SM12x' (research.colfax-intl.com, Dec 2025/2026): sm_120 has no tcgen05/TMEM, uses mma.sync; NVFP4 blockscaled works, ~3x over FP16, tops ~60% peak; CUTLASS 4.3.2 CHANGELOG explicitly adds 'Blackwell SM120 Blockscaled GEMM with NVFP4'. flashinfer issue #2723 (real 4xRTX-PRO-6000 sm_120 debug, Mar 2026): native FP4 MoE garbage until 10+ patches + compute_120f/CUDA13 -> 39 tok/s vs Marlin 46-49; documents compute_120 vs 120a vs 120f table. Spheron FA4 guide (Apr 2026): explicit table - RTX 5090 sm_120 = FA2 only, 'lack the TMEM subsystem FA4 requires'; FA4 only on SM100/SM103. gau-nernst (Aug 2025): hand-written CUDA C++ FA on 5090 = 94.4% SOL, BEATS Dao flash-attn, proving from-scratch sm_120 attention is very achievable. Florian Mattana (Mar 2026): documents the otherwise-undocumented sm_120 FP4 MMA m16n8k32 fragment+scale layout, container nibble in bits 5-2, scale_vec::1X only. CUTLASS issue #2800: 'C++ API works: vLLM uses CUTLASS FP4 on sm_120 via C++ API'. Local verify: nvidia-smi confirms cc 12.0, nvcc 13.1 present (compute_120f available). Laptop GB203 is ~896 GB/s / ~150W = ~half desktop 5090's 1792 GB/s/575W, so scale published desktop tok/s down roughly 2x for memory-bound decode.

components.beats_competitors_how: "Concrete, measurable beat-targets on THIS laptop (24GB, ~896 GB/s, 150W). Metric we optimize: single-stream decode tok/s at low context (primary), then tok/s-per-watt, then 30B-MoE-with-offload tok/s. (1) 7B/8B Q4 single-stream decode: llama.cpp/Ollama ~ (desktop 142-186 tok/s) -> expect ~75-95 tok/s on the laptop's halved bandwidth; vLLM needs CUDA graphs just to reach ~140 desktop. BEAT-TARGET: >= 100 tok/s on 8B-Q4 single-stream via persistent megakernel hitting >=65% of 896 GB/s (~580 GB/s effective), i.e. beat llama.cpp by 15-25% by removing per-op launch overhead, and beat vLLM/SGLang outright on cold-start latency (no Python/graph warmup). (2) 0.6B-1.5B draft/small decode: target >=500 tok/s (Alpin proved 1000 tok/s on desktop 5090 -> ~500 on halved-BW laptop) - python engines cannot touch this because launch overhead dominates at this size. (3) 30B-A3B MoE that does NOT fit comfortably (or 120B-class with offload): target >=12-15 tok/s steady-state via two-tier SLRU expert cache where vLLM/SGLang OOM (0 tok/s) and naive llama.cpp offload gives ~0.5-2 tok/s -> a 6-25x win, the single biggest differentiator. (4) Power efficiency: native NVFP4 decode at ~41% lower energy than BF16 -> best tok/s-per-watt under the 150W cap, where vLLM falls to FP8-emulated/BF16 or broken-FP4-MoE. (5) Prefill: target competitive PP (llama.cpp desktop ~6-10k tok/s -> ~3-5k laptop) using CUTLASS sm_120 blockscaled NVFP4 GEMM at ~60% peak. We win decode latency + MoE-offload + power; we do NOT try to beat vLLM/SGLang on high-concurrency batched throughput (their continuous-batching is mature) - that is explicitly not our metric on a single-user laptop."
open_questions: Laptop GB203 exact specs unverified empirically: need to confirm SM count, real achievable memory bandwidth (~896 GB/s nominal), and sustained clocks at the 150W cap (thermal throttling may make effective decode even more power-bound than bandwidth-bound). Run a bandwidth microbench + a power-vs-tok/s sweep before fixing beat-targets.

Does compute_120f / CUDA 13.1 fully fix the NVFP4 grouped-GEMM (MoE) correctness+speed on THIS sm_120 (cc 12.0, not 12.0a/120f-reported) laptop? flashinfer #2723's 39 tok/s was on RTX PRO 6000 (also sm_120 but workstation); must re-verify the grouped GEMM produces correct output on the consumer laptop and whether TMA WS grouped tactics initialize.

Is the two-tier SLRU expert-cache win as large on a fast PCIe-bound laptop with only ~12-16GB FREE host RAM? PoC was 96GB-RAM-class; with tight host RAM the Tier-2 pinned region may not hold all experts and we fall to SSD/mmap (Tier-3) more often - need to measure hot-expert working-set size vs available pinned RAM for target MoE models.

Megakernel generality: Alpin's kernel is hand-fused for one Qwen3-0.6B shape with no quantization. How much does a persistent megakernel approach generalize to (a) quantized weights with blockscaled dequant in-loop, (b) GQA/different head dims, (c) MoE routing inside the persistent launch? May need a per-architecture codegen path rather than one kernel.

KV-cache FP4/FP8 quant quality vs the asymmetric-K/V kernel trap (from memory: llama.cpp CUDA FA only had SYMMETRIC K/V kernels by default - asymmetric was silently 18x slow). Need to confirm our hand-written sm_120 attention supports asymmetric KV quant (e.g. K=q8 V=q4) without a slow path.

GGUF as the model format vs needing NVFP4 weights: GGUF K-quants are not native sm_120 blockscaled FP4. Do we transcode GGUF->NVFP4 at load, or write a fast K-quant dequant->tensor-core path? Determines whether we get the FP4 power win on existing GGUF models or only on NVFP4-native checkpoints.

_verdict.component: Where competitors are weak on RTX 5090 Laptop (24GB, 150W, sm_120) and where a from-scratch engine can beat vLLM / SGLang / llama.cpp
_verdict.riskiest_claim: Edge #2, the stated keystone: a two-tier SLRU MoE expert cache is "the biggest win / single biggest differentiator," delivering a "6-25x" speedup (beat-target 12-15 tok/s steady-state) on this laptop's MoE targets where "vLLM/SGLang OOM (0 tok/s) and naive llama.cpp offload gives ~0.5-2 tok/s." The load-bearing sub-claim is the 0.5-2 tok/s llama.cpp baseline and the implied 14-28x headroom drawn from RFC #20757's PoC.
_verdict.evidence: The "0.5-2 tok/s naive offload" baseline conflates two different things and does not represent llama.cpp's actual --n-cpu-moe. Primary sources: (1) RFC #20757 itself (github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/issues/20757) shows its 14 tok/s steady-state vs "0.5-1 tok/s pure CPU offload with no cache" was measured on RTX PRO 2000 8GB VRAM running GPT-OSS-120B (~57GB experts = 7x VRAM); the 0.5-1 figure is PURE CPU with no GPU acceleration, NOT llama.cpp --n-cpu-moe (which already does per-token selective expert copy via ggml_backend_sched_compute_splits). The RFC is still PoC-only, "help wanted," unmerged, and on Python/transformers, not llama.cpp. (2) hardware-corner.net/gpt-oss-offloading-moe-layers (Nov 2025) measured llama.cpp's EXISTING --n-cpu-moe on GPT-OSS-120B-F16: desktop RTX 5090 = 8.1-9.6 tok/s (naive whole-layer split was 3.4 t/s), single RTX 3090 = 1.6 t/s. (3) llama.cpp Discussion #15396 + r/LocalLLaMA report 23-31 tok/s on a 5090 with --n-cpu-moe on quantized (MXFP4) GPT-OSS-120B. So llama.cpp's real offload baseline for 120B-class is 8-31 tok/s, not 0.5-2. The proposed 12-15 tok/s beat-target is therefore at or below what llama.cpp already does on a same-class desktop card, and the laptop has ~half the bandwidth (~896 vs ~1792 GB/s), so the cache must work hard just to match existing llama.cpp. (4) The laptop's STATED PRIMARY MoE target (30B-A3B Q4) doesn't need offload at all: it fits in 24GB and runs at 120-183 tok/s fully resident on a desktop 5090 (llama.cpp Discussion #19890; HF Qwen3.6-35B-A3B discussion #37: "Q3 takes 23GB, runs at 120 tok/s") — the cache delivers ZERO benefit there because nothing spills. Separately, the sm_120 NVFP4 grouped-GEMM claim (the other candidate for riskiest) is CONFIRMED and properly hedged: flashinfer #2723, #2577, cutlass #3096, vllm #38718 all corroborate garbage output on compute_120/120a, the compute_120f fix (39 tok/s), and Marlin W4A16 fallback (46-49) — that claim is not the weak link.
_verdict.correction: Keep the two-tier expert cache, but demote it from "the biggest win / 6-25x" to a "narrow-regime win (~1.5-3x) for models that overflow VRAM AND host RAM." Concretely: (a) The honest comparison is vs llama.cpp --n-cpu-moe, not vs pure-CPU. Realistic laptop beat-target for 120B-class offload: if desktop 5090 --n-cpu-moe gives 8-9.6 t/s (F16) to ~25 t/s (quantized), the laptop at half bandwidth + tight host RAM (~12-16GB free, NOT the 96GB-class the RFC assumed) is bandwidth/PCIe-bound and likely lands 4-8 t/s; a good SLRU cache might reach 8-12 t/s — i.e., beat llama.cpp by ~1.5-2x in that regime, not 6-25x. Open-question #3 already names this risk; treat it as a confirmed constraint: with only 12-16GB free host RAM, Tier-2 pinned cannot hold a 120B's experts, so you fall to SSD/mmap Tier-3 and the win shrinks further. (b) Drop the claim that the cache helps the 30B-A3B primary target — that model is fully resident on 24GB and is plain bandwidth-bound; the megakernel (edge #1) is the relevant lever there, not the cache. (c) Re-rank the three edges: the structurally strongest, best-supported differentiators are edge #1 (persistent megakernel removing Python/launch overhead — well-proven) and edge #3 (native sm_120 NVFP4/MXFP4 under the 150W cap — confirmed feasible via compute_120f, Marlin fallback). The MoE cache is a real but conditional third edge. (d) Before fixing any beat-target, run the open-question #1/#3 microbenches: measure the laptop's hot-expert working-set vs free pinned RAM for each candidate MoE, and measure llama.cpp --n-cpu-moe on THIS laptop as the true baseline rather than citing the 8GB/120B PoC.
